Description (what the sim teaches): The **four fundamental forces**: gravitational, electromagnetic, strong nuclear, weak nuclear. Relative strengths (~1 : 10⁻² : 10⁻¹³ : 10⁻³⁸, taking strong = 1) and ranges (gravity + EM infinite; strong ~10⁻¹⁵ m; weak ~10⁻¹⁸ m). EVERY force in nature is one of these four.
Notes: PW-G3; **the one V1-worthy atomic**; **cognitive_error_target:** "dozens of different forces" → only 2 act at everyday scale (EM + gravity)

Description (what the sim teaches): The comparison table: strong (1, ~10⁻¹⁵ m), EM (10⁻², infinite), weak (10⁻¹³, ~10⁻¹⁸ m), gravity (10⁻³⁸, infinite). **Simulatable as a strength-bar + range-scale visual.** Why gravity dominates cosmically (infinite range + always attractive) despite being weakest.
Notes: parent; PW-G3; the genuinely-simulatable fragment

Description (what the sim teaches): Friction, tension, normal reaction, spring force, drag — ALL are the electromagnetic repulsion/attraction between the electron clouds of surface atoms. **The single most unifying aha**: the macroscopic contact-force zoo of T11/T12 is ONE force (EM) in disguise. Genuine "small → smaller" recursion.
Notes: parent; PW-G4; retroactively unifies T11/T12

Description (what the sim teaches): The drive toward unification: Maxwell unified electricity + magnetism + optics into electromagnetism; the electroweak theory unified EM + weak; the search for grand unification + quantum gravity continues. Physics seeks the fewest principles explaining the most phenomena.
Notes: parent; PW-G7; narrative (not standalone sim)

Targets misconception: conservation law = just one more formula
Description (what the sim teaches): Conservation of **energy, linear momentum, angular momentum, and charge** as the DEEP organizing principles of physics — rooted in symmetries of nature (qualitative Noether: time-symmetry → energy; space-symmetry → momentum; rotational-symmetry → angular momentum). More fundamental than any specific force law: they hold even when the detailed forces are unknown.
Notes: PW-G5; **cognitive_error_target:** "conservation law = just one more formula"

Description (what the sim teaches): Qualitative Noether: every conservation law mirrors a symmetry of nature. Energy ← laws don't change over time; momentum ← laws don't change across space; angular momentum ← laws don't change with orientation. **Why conservation is DEEP, not arbitrary.**
Notes: parent; PW-G5 cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Conservation laws let you relate "before" and "after" WITHOUT tracking the messy forces in between (e.g. collision: momentum before = momentum after, no need to integrate the contact force). This is WHY they are the physicist's first tool.
Notes: parent; the operational payoff

Targets misconception: classical mechanics is wrong, replaced by quantum/relativity
Description (what the sim teaches): The reach of physics across **scale** (10⁻¹⁵ m nucleus → 10⁺²⁶ m observable universe, ~41 orders of magnitude) and the **three domains of validity**: CLASSICAL (everyday sizes/speeds), QUANTUM (atomic/sub-atomic), RELATIVISTIC (speeds near c). Each theory is the correct model WITHIN its domain.
Notes: PW-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "classical mechanics is wrong, replaced by quantum/relativity"

Description (what the sim teaches): The scale ladder: quark < proton (10⁻¹⁵ m) < atom (10⁻¹⁰) < human (10⁰) < Earth (10⁷) < solar system (10¹³) < galaxy (10²¹) < observable universe (10²⁶). Links to T2 order-of-magnitude estimation. **Simulatable as a logarithmic zoom.**
Notes: parent; **education anchor**; zoom-sim fragment

Description (what the sim teaches): Classical mechanics is EXACT for everyday scales/speeds (a cricket ball, a car); quantum is needed at atomic scale; relativity at speeds near c. The boundaries (not right-vs-wrong) — classical is a limiting case of both.
Notes: parent; PW-G6 cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Physics → technology → society loop: ISRO (orbital mechanics + EM), DAE nuclear power (nuclear forces), semiconductor/IT (quantum). The Indian national-science narrative. **Marketing-relevant framing**: physics is the engine behind India's strategic capabilities.
Notes: parent; **research/policy + space anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): The **7 SI base units** (metre, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela) and how every **derived unit** (N = kg·m/s², J = N·m, ...) is built from them. **Unit consistency** is the zeroth-order check on any equation; the **factor-label method** uses units as an algebraic derivation tool.
Notes: U-G7; catalog-anchor atomic; **cognitive_error_target:** "units are droppable labels"

Description (what the sim teaches): The 7 base quantities + their SI units + symbols; every other physical quantity is derived. Why exactly 7 (independence — none derivable from the others).
Notes: parent; U-G7 scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Convert via multiplying by 1 in disguise (e.g., 72 km/h × (1000 m/km) × (1 h/3600 s) = 20 m/s). Units cancel like algebra; the method PREVENTS conversion errors.
Notes: parent; cognitive-error countermeasure (units-as-tool)

Description (what the sim teaches): 2019 SI redefinition: kg now fixed via Planck constant h (no physical artefact); ampere via electron charge e; kelvin via Boltzmann k. **Modern Indian-relevant anchor**: CSIR-NPL realises the new kg with a Kibble balance. Constants, not objects, define units.
Notes: parent; **metrology anchor**; modern

Description (what the sim teaches): The **dimensional formula** [MᵃLᵇTᶜ...] of a quantity; the **principle of homogeneity** (every additive term in a valid equation has identical dimensions). Three legitimate uses: (1) CHECK an equation; (2) CONVERT units between systems; (3) DERIVE a relation UP TO a dimensionless constant. The universal sanity-check applied implicitly across all of physics.
Required-by (unlocks): [error_analysis_and_propagation_atomic, EVERY formula-bearing atomic (universal); absorbs math-tool `dimensional_analysis`+`unit_analysis` stubs]
Notes: U-G2/U-G8; **Diamond candidate** (dimensional-balance animation); **cognitive_error_target:** "gives complete formula incl. constants"

Description (what the sim teaches): Every additive term in a physically valid equation carries the SAME dimensions (you cannot add a length to a time). The instant equation-validity test: if the terms don't match dimensionally, the equation is WRONG (necessary, not sufficient).
Notes: parent; U-G2 scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Find the FORM of a relation by matching powers: T = k·mᵃ·lᵇ·gᶜ → solve a,b,c by dimensional balance → T = k√(l/g). **The k (= 2π here) is NOT obtainable** — must come from theory/experiment. The power-matching method.
Notes: parent; the "derive up to a constant" use

Description (what the sim teaches): Three hard failures: (a) cannot find dimensionless constants (½, 2π, sin's coefficient); (b) cannot handle multi-term-of-unknown-form equations; (c) arguments of sin/cos/log/exp MUST be dimensionless (ωt is fine; t alone inside sin is dimensionally illegal). **The single most-tested conceptual trap.**
Notes: parent; U-G3; mandatory cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): The digits that carry real measurement information. Counting rules (non-zero always count; captive zeros count; leading zeros never; trailing zeros after a decimal count). The **asymmetric arithmetic rule**: +/− keeps the least DECIMAL PLACES; ×/÷ keeps the least SIG-FIG COUNT.
Notes: U-G4; **cognitive_error_target:** "decimal places = precision"

Description (what the sim teaches): Leading zeros (0.0025 → 2 sf) vs captive zeros (1002 → 4 sf) vs trailing zeros (2.50 → 3 sf; 2500 → ambiguous, use scientific notation 2.5×10³). Scientific notation removes ambiguity.
Notes: parent; U-G4 scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): The asymmetric rule in action: 12.11 + 0.3 = 12.4 (1 decimal place); 4.32 × 1.1 = 4.8 (2 sf). The weakest measurement caps the result's precision.
Notes: parent; the asymmetric +/− vs ×/÷ rule

Description (what the sim teaches): Standard rounding (≥5 rounds up); only round the FINAL answer, never intermediate steps (intermediate rounding compounds error). Carry a guard digit.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): **Absolute error** Δx, **relative error** Δx/x, **percentage error** (Δx/x)×100. Propagation: errors ADD (absolute) for sums/differences; RELATIVE errors add for products/quotients; for Z = (A^p B^q)/C^r the relative error = p(ΔA/A) + q(ΔB/B) + r(ΔC/C). The exponent MULTIPLIES the relative error.
Notes: U-G5; **cognitive_error_target:** "errors always just add"

Description (what the sim teaches): Derivation of the propagation rules from differentials: for Z = A·B, dZ/Z = dA/A + dB/B (relative errors add). The power rule from Z = Aⁿ: ΔZ/Z = n·(ΔA/A).
Notes: parent; bridges to T3 (differentials)

Description (what the sim teaches): Worked: density ρ = m/V; m = 5.00 ± 0.01 g (0.2%), V = 4.0 ± 0.1 cm³ (2.5%) → Δρ/ρ = 0.2% + 2.5% = 2.7%. **The volume measurement dominates** — improving the balance is wasted effort. Error budgeting.
Notes: parent; **healthcare/lab anchor** (dosage budgeting)

Description (what the sim teaches): The instrument's least count is the floor on absolute error for a single reading. A vernier reading to 0.01 cm cannot claim 0.001 cm precision. Connects instrument choice → achievable error.
Notes: parent; links instrument → error

Description (what the sim teaches): **Least count** = smallest measurable division. **Vernier caliper** LC = 1 MSD − 1 VSD (typically 0.01 cm). **Screw gauge** LC = pitch / number-of-circular-divisions (typically 0.001 cm). **Zero error** (systematic; subtract it). **Accuracy** (closeness to true value) vs **precision** (repeatability/spread) — independent qualities.
Notes: U-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "accuracy = precision"

Description (what the sim teaches): Vernier: main-scale reading + (coinciding vernier division × LC). Screw gauge: main-scale + (circular-scale × LC), watch for backlash. The two workhorse Indian-physics-lab instruments.
Notes: parent; **education/lab anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Bullseye picture: tight cluster off-centre = precise-but-inaccurate (systematic error / zero error); scattered around centre = accurate-but-imprecise (random error). The two are independent; good measurement needs both.
Notes: parent; U-G6 cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): A screw gauge reading 0.002 cm with jaws closed has +0.002 cm zero error → SUBTRACT from every reading. Systematic (not random) — repeating the measurement does NOT average it out.
Notes: parent; systematic-vs-random scaffold

Targets misconception: derivative is just a formula to memorise
Description (what the sim teaches): The derivative dy/dx = **slope of the tangent** to the y(x) curve; d/dt = **instantaneous rate of change**. Physics meaning: v = dx/dt, a = dv/dt, I = dq/dt, P = dW/dt, F = dp/dt. The standard-derivatives table (xⁿ, sin, cos, eˣ) is downstream of this geometric/rate meaning.
Required-by (unlocks): [avg_vs_instantaneous_velocity_atomic(T6), acceleration_definition_atomic(T6), EVERY rate-defined quantity (universal); absorbs math-tool `calculus_derivative_basics`]
Notes: MT-G3; teaching-unit; **cognitive_error_target:** "derivative is just a formula to memorise"

Description (what the sim teaches): Geometric meaning: the derivative at a point = the slope of the line just touching the curve there. Secant → tangent as Δx → 0. The limit definition made visual.
Notes: parent; MT-G3 geometric scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): d(xⁿ)/dx = nxⁿ⁻¹; d(sin x)/dx = cos x; d(cos x)/dx = −sin x; d(eˣ)/dx = eˣ; chain + product rule. The operational toolkit — but taught AFTER the slope/rate meaning.
Notes: parent; operational toolkit

Description (what the sim teaches): The recurring physics derivatives: v = dx/dt (speedometer reads this), a = dv/dt, I = dq/dt, P = dW/dt, F = dp/dt. Each is a RATE the student already intuits before the calculus.
Notes: parent; **everyday-instrument anchor** (speedometer)

Description (what the sim teaches): The definite integral ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = **area under the f(x) curve** = **accumulated total**. Physics meaning: displacement = ∫v dt (area under v-t), work = ∫F dx (area under F-x), charge = ∫I dt, impulse = ∫F dt. The **Fundamental Theorem** links it to the derivative as the inverse operation.
Required-by (unlocks): [s_in_equations(T6), work-energy atomics(T13), EVERY accumulated quantity (universal); absorbs math-tool `calculus_integration_basics`]
Notes: MT-G4; teaching-unit; **cognitive_error_target:** "integration & differentiation unrelated"

Description (what the sim teaches): The integral as the limit of summed rectangles (Riemann sum) → exact area. For a v-t graph the area IS the displacement; for an F-x graph the area IS the work. Area carries physical meaning.
Notes: parent; MT-G4 geometric scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Integration UNDOES differentiation: if v = dx/dt then x = ∫v dt + x₀. The derivative gives the rate; the integral accumulates it back. **The two operations are inverses** — the deepest conceptual point of T3.
Notes: parent; MT-G4 cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): The recurring physics integrals: displacement = ∫v dt (odometer accumulates this), work = ∫F·dx, charge = ∫I dt, impulse = ∫F dt, ISRO trajectory = numerically-integrated acceleration.
Notes: parent; **everyday-instrument (odometer) + space anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): The 5 canonical physics function-shapes and how to READ them: **linear** (y = mx + c — slope m + intercept c), **quadratic** (parabola — e.g. projectile y vs x), **inverse** (1/x — e.g. Coulomb, Boyle), **sinusoidal** (A sin(ωt + φ) — SHM, waves, AC), **exponential** (e^(±t/τ) — decay/growth). Extract physics from slope, intercept, area, and curvature.
Notes: MT-G5; **cognitive_error_target:** "graph is just a picture"

Description (what the sim teaches): From a straight-line graph: slope = Δy/Δx (with composite units, e.g. v-t slope is m/s² = acceleration); intercept = value at x = 0 (physical initial condition). Reading physics OFF the line.
Notes: parent; MT-G5 scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): The 5 shapes mapped to physics: linear (uniform motion), parabola (projectile/accelerated), 1/x (inverse-square fields, Boyle), sine (oscillation/wave), exponential (RC/radioactivity). Recognising the shape names the physics.
Notes: parent; the 5-shape catalogue

Description (what the sim teaches): Convert a curved relation to a straight line to extract constants: plot T² vs l (not T vs l) for a pendulum → slope gives g; log-log plots for power laws. **The core Indian-physics-lab data-analysis skill.**
Notes: parent; **education/lab anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): **Controlled** approximations that extract leading behaviour: **small-angle** (sin θ ≈ tan θ ≈ θ, cos θ ≈ 1 for θ ≪ 1 rad) and **binomial** ((1 + x)ⁿ ≈ 1 + nx for x ≪ 1). The WHY (keep the dominant term, drop negligible ones) and the validity boundary (~0.1 rad ≈ 6° for ~1% error). Physics is BUILT on these (SHM, paraxial optics, g at small height).
Notes: MT-G6; teaching-unit; **cognitive_error_target:** "approximations are sloppy / less correct"

Description (what the sim teaches): For θ ≪ 1 rad: sin θ ≈ θ, tan θ ≈ θ, cos θ ≈ 1 − θ²/2 ≈ 1. Validity ~0.1 rad (≈ 6°) for ~1% accuracy. The reason the pendulum is SHM ONLY for small swings. **Must use radians.**
Notes: parent; MT-G6 scaffold; links to T2 radian-dimensionlessness

Description (what the sim teaches): (1 + x)ⁿ ≈ 1 + nx for x ≪ 1 (any real n). Used for g(h) = g(1 + h/R)⁻² ≈ g(1 − 2h/R) at small height; relativistic + index-of-refraction expansions. Keep the leading correction, drop x².
Notes: parent; the controlled-expansion workhorse

Targets misconception: the graph is just for plotting marks
Description (what the sim teaches): THE core experimental skill: take a physical relation, **linearise** it (choose axes so the plot is a straight line), plot the data, draw the **best-fit line**, and extract a physical constant from the **slope** (or intercept). The graph IS the measurement instrument — it averages out random scatter and exposes systematic trends.
Notes: T4-G3; catalog-anchor atomic; **cognitive_error_target:** "the graph is just for plotting marks" → the slope IS the answer

Description (what the sim teaches): Simple pendulum: time N oscillations (reduces reaction-time error), compute T, plot **T² vs l** (a straight line through origin), slope = 4π²/g → solve for g. Amplitude must stay small (SHM validity, links to T17 + T3 small-angle). The canonical "find a constant from a slope" experiment.
Notes: parent; T4-G4; **education/lab anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Searle's apparatus: hang increasing loads on a wire, measure extension with a vernier/micrometer, plot **extension vs load** (straight line), slope + wire geometry → Young's modulus Y. Links to T18 elasticity (stress/strain).
Notes: parent; T4-G4; **education/lab anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Loaded spring: plot **extension vs load** → slope = 1/k (Hooke's law); OR oscillation **T² vs mass** → slope = 4π²/k. Two routes to k; cross-check. Links to T17 SHM.
Notes: parent; T4-G4

Targets misconception: knowing the LC formula = being able to read it
Description (what the sim teaches): The PROCEDURAL skill of reading precision instruments: **vernier caliper** (main-scale reading + coinciding-vernier-division × least count), **screw gauge** (main-scale + circular-scale × least count, watch for backlash), **spherometer** (for radius of curvature). Distinct from the least-count CONCEPT (T2) — this is the hands-on reading technique.
Notes: T4-G2; **cognitive_error_target:** "knowing the LC formula = being able to read it"

Description (what the sim teaches): Read the main scale just before the zero of the vernier; find the vernier division that COINCIDES with a main-scale line; reading = MSR + (coinciding division × LC). Parallax-free viewing (eye perpendicular).
Notes: parent; T4-G4; **everyday-instrument anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Rotate the ratchet (not the thimble) to avoid over-tightening; read main-scale + (circular-scale × LC); account for backlash error (always rotate one way). Watch for zero error (correct per T2).
Notes: parent; T4-G4; **everyday-instrument anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Every experiment has characteristic error sources: pendulum (large amplitude breaks SHM; reaction-time → time many oscillations); Searle's (temperature drift, wire kinks); metre-bridge (end-corrections, jockey pressure). **Repetition cuts RANDOM error but NOT systematic error** — systematic sources must be identified + corrected separately.
Notes: parent; T4-G5; **cognitive_error_target:** "more readings = more accuracy"

Description (what the sim teaches): The bench-experiment skill set: **electrical** (metre bridge + potentiometer for resistance/EMF comparison; Ohm's-law verification) and **optical** (focal length of lens/mirror by u-v method; refractive index by real/apparent-depth or by travelling microscope). Each combines an instrument-reading skill with a graph/calculation.
Notes: T4-G4; bench-experiment skill class

Description (what the sim teaches): Wheatstone-bridge principle on a 1 m wire: balance point gives unknown resistance R = R_known × (l/(100−l)). End-corrections are the key systematic error (interchange known/unknown to cancel). Links to T34 Kirchhoff/Wheatstone.
Notes: parent; T4-G4

Description (what the sim teaches): Potentiometer compares EMFs (or measures internal resistance) by null-deflection — no current drawn at balance, so it reads TRUE EMF (unlike a voltmeter). Balance length ∝ EMF. Links to T34.
Notes: parent; T4-G4

Description (what the sim teaches): Lens u-v method: measure several (u, v) pairs, plot **1/v vs 1/u** (straight line) → intercepts give 1/f; OR plot v vs u and use the lens formula. Combines bench-reading + graph-method. Links to T42.
Notes: parent; T4-G4

Description (what the sim teaches): Travelling microscope: focus on a mark, then on it through a glass slab; real depth / apparent depth = refractive index n. A direct single-measurement n. Links to T42 refraction.
Notes: parent; T4-G4

Description (what the sim teaches): A vector of magnitude 1 that carries direction only; any vector = (magnitude) × (unit vector). Notation â = A/\|A\|. The basis of all component work.
Notes: DAG root; alias target of legacy `vector_basics`

Description (what the sim teaches): Multiplying a vector by scalar k scales its magnitude by \|k\| and keeps direction if k>0, reverses it if k<0.
Notes: foundation operation

Description (what the sim teaches): −A is A with the same magnitude but opposite direction (the k=−1 case of scalar multiplication). A + (−A) = 0.
Notes: sign-of-a-vector scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Geometric (triangle) addition: place the tail of B at the head of A; the arrow from A's tail to B's head is the resultant. Generalises to the polygon law.
Notes: foundational addition; alias-related to `vector_addition`

Targets misconception: vectors at different points can't be equal
Description (what the sim teaches): Equal vectors: same magnitude AND direction (position irrelevant). Parallel: same direction, any magnitude. Distinguishes "equal" from "parallel/collinear."
Notes: **cognitive_error_target:** "vectors at different points can't be equal"

Targets misconception: measure the angle without moving both to one tail
Description (what the sim teaches): Protocol: bring both vectors to a common tail, arrows pointing outward; the angle between them is ≤ 180°. Critical for dot/cross products and resultant direction.
Notes: **cognitive_error_target:** "measure the angle without moving both to one tail"

Description (what the sim teaches): The definitive test for whether a quantity is a vector: it must add by the parallelogram (equivalently triangle) law AND obey scalar multiplication. Direction alone is not enough.
Notes: V-G3; the "is it a vector?" gate

Targets misconception: anything with direction is a vector
Description (what the sim teaches): Electric current has a direction (along the wire) yet is a SCALAR — at a junction currents add algebraically (Kirchhoff), not by the parallelogram law. The counterexample that defines "vector."
Notes: V-G2; alias target of legacy `scalar_vs_vector`; **cognitive_error_target:** "anything with direction is a vector"

Targets misconception: pressure has a direction so it's a vector
Description (what the sim teaches): Pressure pushes in all directions at a point yet is a SCALAR; the vector is the FORCE on a chosen surface, F = P·(A n̂). Distinguishes the scalar field from the vector it generates.
Notes: V-G2; **cognitive_error_target:** "pressure has a direction so it's a vector"

Description (what the sim teaches): Area is a scalar (size of a surface) but can be promoted to a vector A = A n̂ (magnitude × outward normal) — the form used for flux. Two faces of one quantity.
Notes: bridge to flux chapters

Description (what the sim teaches): Magnitude of A + B from the law of cosines: \|R\| = √(A² + B² + 2AB cos θ). Why scalar addition fails when θ ≠ 0.
Notes: V-G4; alias-related to `vector_addition`

Description (what the sim teaches): The three shortcuts: θ=0° → R = A+B; θ=90° → R = √(A²+B²); θ=180° → R = \|A−B\|.
Notes: parent: resultant_formula; built as a fuller sim (V-G7)

Description (what the sim teaches): The resultant is bounded: \|A−B\| ≤ R ≤ A+B. Maximum when parallel, minimum when antiparallel.
Notes: parent: resultant_formula; built as a fuller sim (V-G7)

Description (what the sim teaches): The resultant's direction: tan α = B sin θ / (A + B cos θ), measured from A.
Notes: parent: resultant_formula; built as a fuller sim (V-G7)

Description (what the sim teaches): Splitting a vector into perpendicular components A_x = A cos θ, A_y = A sin θ; choosing axes to simplify a problem; recovering the vector by Pythagoras. The workhorse of all 2D physics.
Required-by (unlocks): [inclined_plane_components, negative_components, unit_vector_form, dot_product, position_frame_of_reference_atomic(T6), sign_convention_1d_atomic(T6), vector_kinematics_2d_atomic(T7), projectile_decoupling_atomic(T8), free_body_diagram(T11), magnetic_force_direction_right_hand_rule(T36), antenna_range_formula(T50)]
Notes: V-G5; alias target of legacy `vector_components`; **the most-required concept in the whole catalog**

Description (what the sim teaches): Resolving weight on a slope: mg sin θ along the incline, mg cos θ perpendicular. The canonical resolution application.
Notes: parent: vector_resolution; built as a fuller sim (V-G7)

Description (what the sim teaches): Components carry signs that track direction (e.g. a vector in the 2nd quadrant has negative x-component); sign ≠ size.
Notes: parent: vector_resolution; sign-discipline scaffold (V-G7)

Description (what the sim teaches): Writing a vector in î, ĵ, k̂ form: A = A_x î + A_y ĵ + A_z k̂. The algebraic representation behind component addition and products.
Notes: the algebra form of resolution

Description (what the sim teaches): Scalar product A·B = \|A\|\|B\| cos θ = A_x B_x + A_y B_y + A_z B_z. Geometric meaning (projection/overlap); zero ⇔ perpendicular.
Notes: gateway to work, flux, power

Description (what the sim teaches): A particle's location specified by coordinates relative to a chosen origin + axes. **Frame of reference** = the coordinate system + clock from which observations are made. Motion is always relative to a frame; "absolute" rest does not exist.
Notes: foundation; frame-relativity foreshadows special relativity (T-extra)

Description (what the sim teaches): Choice of origin is arbitrary — physics doesn't change. **What changes**: numerical values of x, v(if moving frame), a(if accelerating frame). **What stays the same**: relative-positions, time-intervals, accelerations between inertial frames.
Notes: parent; foundation of inertial-frame concept

Description (what the sim teaches): **Inertial frame**: a frame in which Newton's 1st law holds (free particle moves uniformly). **Non-inertial frame**: accelerating frame (e.g., braking-car frame); pseudo-forces appear. Examples: ground-frame, train-moving-uniform-velocity = inertial; train-braking = non-inertial.
Notes: parent; bridge to T11 Newton's Laws

Description (what the sim teaches): **Displacement** Δx = x_final − x_initial (vector in 1D = signed scalar). **Distance** = path length traversed (scalar, always ≥ 0). For round-trip: displacement = 0; distance = 2·(path length). Distance ≥ |displacement| always.
Notes: K1-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "displacement = distance always" — round-trip animation required

Description (what the sim teaches): Bengaluru→Mumbai→Bengaluru: distance = 2 × 980 km ≈ 1960 km; displacement = 0. Tabla-player travels back-and-forth between two notes: musically meaningful path; net displacement zero. **Cognitive scaffold** distinguishing scalar accumulation from vector return.
Notes: parent; **Indian-context anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Choose +x to the right. Object moves 3m right then 7m left: x_final − x_initial = (0+3−7) − 0 = −4 m. Negative displacement = position now LEFT of start. Sign carries direction.
Notes: parent; sign-mechanics scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): **Average velocity** v_avg = Δx/Δt over finite interval. **Instantaneous velocity** v = dx/dt = lim_{Δt→0} Δx/Δt. **Speed** = |v| = magnitude only (scalar). Average velocity ≠ average speed in general (round-trip: v_avg = 0 but avg speed ≠ 0).
Notes: K1-G3; **cognitive_error_target:** "v_avg = (v₁+v₂)/2 always" → only for constant a

Description (what the sim teaches): Geometrically: v(t) at instant t = slope of x-t graph at that point. Tangent-line construction. **CBSE/ISC physics-practical lab**: students sketch x-t curves and compute slopes at chosen instants.
Notes: parent; **education anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Mumbai-Pune Expressway: drive 150 km in 3 hr (Mumbai→Pune). Then drive back 150 km in 2 hr. **Average velocity** = (0 km displacement) / (5 hr) = 0. **Average speed** = (300 km) / (5 hr) = 60 km/hr. Two different scalars; both useful, different questions.
Notes: parent; **transport/Indian-Highway anchor**

Targets misconception: negative a always means slowing down
Description (what the sim teaches): **Acceleration** a = dv/dt = rate of change of velocity. Average acceleration a_avg = Δv/Δt; instantaneous a = lim_{Δt→0} Δv/Δt. **Sign carries direction**: positive a means v increasing in +x direction (if v already +); but if v is −, positive a means v becoming LESS NEGATIVE (slowing down then reversing).
Notes: K1-G4; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "negative a always means slowing down" → depends on sign of v

Description (what the sim teaches): **4 cases** (with +x as positive): (a>0, v>0) → speeding up forward; (a>0, v<0) → slowing down then reversing forward; (a<0, v>0) → slowing down then reversing backward; (a<0, v<0) → speeding up backward. **Diamond-candidate animation**: 2×2 grid showing object behaviour each case.
Notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): **Deceleration** = magnitude of acceleration opposing velocity. **Negative acceleration** = a < 0 in chosen sign convention. These are NOT synonyms. A car moving left (v < 0) with brakes (a > 0): "decelerating" but positive a.
Notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): When acceleration is constant: **(1) v = u + at**; **(2) s = ut + ½at²**; **(3) v² = u² + 2as**. All three derive from integrating a = constant; they are three rearrangements (one for each unknown). **Use what's given.** Also **s_n = u + ½a(2n−1)** for displacement in nth second.
Notes: K1-G5; **cognitive_error_target:** "memorise three formulas" → derive from a=constant once; pick by what's given

Description (what the sim teaches): Displacement during the nth second (between t=n−1 and t=n) = u + ½a(2n−1). **Common JEE/NEET problem-template**. Derive from s_n = s(n) − s(n−1) using eq.(2).
Notes: parent; problem-pattern

Description (what the sim teaches): If given (u, a, t) → use (1) for v. If given (u, a, t) → use (2) for s. If given (u, a, s) → use (3) for v. If given (v, u, s) → use (3) for a. **Decision-tree pedagogy** rather than blind memorisation.
Notes: parent; problem-solving scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Three synchronised views of same motion: **x-t graph** position vs time; **v-t graph** velocity vs time; **a-t graph** acceleration vs time. Relationships: slope of x-t = v(t); slope of v-t = a(t); area under v-t between t₁ and t₂ = Δx; area under a-t = Δv.
Notes: K1-G6; **Diamond candidate**; multi-panel synchronised graph animation

Description (what the sim teaches): x-t straight line = uniform v; x-t parabola opening up = positive a; x-t curve flattening = decelerating; v-t horizontal = zero a (uniform motion); v-t straight rising = constant positive a; v-t straight falling = constant negative a. **Cognitive scaffold**: shape ↔ physical meaning table.
Notes: parent; **CBSE/ISC physics-practical pedagogy**

Description (what the sim teaches): ∫_{t₁}^{t₂} v(t) dt = x(t₂) − x(t₁) = Δx. Geometric interpretation: signed area under v-t curve gives signed displacement. **Negative areas** (below t-axis) = negative displacement (motion in −x direction).
Notes: parent; calculus-geometric bridge

Description (what the sim teaches): Specialisation of constant-a kinematics: a = g downward (= 9.81 m/s² near Earth surface; round to 10 for problems). Drop from rest: v = gt; s = ½gt²; v² = 2gs. Throw up with u: max-height at t = u/g; max-height = u²/(2g); time-of-flight (return to start) = 2u/g.
Notes: K1-G7; **cognitive_error_target:** "heavier objects fall faster" → mandatory feather-vs-coin vacuum-tube demo

Description (what the sim teaches): Aristotle: heavier falls faster (incorrect). Galileo (~1590): all bodies fall at same rate in absence of air. **Bell-jar + feather + coin demonstration**: with air, coin lands first; with vacuum, both land together. **CBSE Class-11 lab demo** + Apollo-15 hammer-feather on Moon (David Scott, 1971; relevant for Chandrayaan-curious students).
Notes: parent; cognitive-error countermeasure; **education + space anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Object thrown up with u (+y up): max-height at t = u/g, v = 0; max-height = u²/(2g); returns with same speed |u| at t = 2u/g, v = −u. **Symmetric in time** (up-down) but NOT in distance (max-height reached once).
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): **Pick a positive direction** (typically +x to the right, +y up). **EVERY quantity carries that sign**: position x, velocity v, acceleration a, force F, displacement Δx. Once chosen, stay consistent throughout the problem. **Foundation rule for all sign-bearing physics quantities downstream** (E, B, I, q, P, M_torque).
Required-by (unlocks): [displacement_vs_distance_atomic, acceleration_definition_atomic, free_fall_gravity_atomic, ALL downstream physics requiring sign]
Notes: K1-G8; **cognitive_error_target:** "g is always −9.81" → only when up is positive; sign-convention foundation

Description (what the sim teaches): Worked-example pedagogy: **(a) declare** "+x to the right"; **(b) write** all knowns with explicit sign (u = +5 m/s, a = −2 m/s²); **(c) substitute** into kinematic eq. **Common error**: dropping signs midway through problem. Mandatory checklist nano.
Notes: parent; problem-solving discipline

Description (what the sim teaches): Velocity of A relative to B: **v_AB = v_A − v_B**. If A moves at +30 m/s and B at +20 m/s (both rightward): v_AB = +10 m/s (A appears to recede from B at 10 m/s). If they move toward each other: signs differ → magnitudes add. **1D preview of T7 2D vector relative motion.**
Notes: K1-G9; **cognitive_error_target:** "subtract speeds, ignore direction" → must preserve signs

Description (what the sim teaches): Two-train passing: same-direction (Mumbai-Delhi Rajdhani 110 km/hr passes goods 50 km/hr): relative speed = 60 km/hr; time to pass = (sum of lengths)/(rel speed). Opposite-direction: speeds add. **Classic Indian-railway-context problem.**
Notes: parent; **transport anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Pursuer at distance d, speed v_p; target at speed v_t (same direction, v_p > v_t). Catch-up time = d / (v_p − v_t). **Common JEE/NEET pattern.**
Notes: parent; problem-pattern

Description (what the sim teaches): Position **r⃗(t) = x(t) î + y(t) ĵ**; velocity **v⃗ = dr⃗/dt = (dx/dt) î + (dy/dt) ĵ**; acceleration **a⃗ = dv⃗/dt**. **Componentwise principle**: in absence of constraints, x-motion and y-motion are INDEPENDENT — each obeys 1D kinematics separately.
Notes: K2-G2; **Diamond candidate**; foundation of all 2D Mechanics

Description (what the sim teaches): Same three kinematic equations as 1D applied independently to x and y: v_x = u_x + a_x·t; v_y = u_y + a_y·t; x = x₀ + u_x·t + ½·a_x·t²; y = y₀ + u_y·t + ½·a_y·t². **No new formulas** — just two parallel applications of 1D machinery.
Notes: parent; K2-G3; **cognitive_error_target:** "2D needs new formulas" → no, componentwise

Description (what the sim teaches): Eliminate t between x(t) and y(t) → trajectory y(x). For constant a⃗: trajectory is parabola in general. **Geometric vs kinematic dual representation.** Bridge to T8 projectile-motion trajectory derivation.
Notes: parent; bridge to T8

Description (what the sim teaches): |Δr⃗| = √(Δx² + Δy²); direction θ = arctan(Δy/Δx). **Pythagoras + arctan applied to displacement**. Same idea applies to velocity, acceleration.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Two inertial frames S and S' with S' moving at constant velocity v⃗_S'S relative to S. **Velocity transformation:** v⃗_PS = v⃗_PS' + v⃗_S'S (velocity of particle in S = velocity in S' + velocity of S' wrt S). **Acceleration is invariant:** a⃗ same in all inertial frames. **Time is invariant** (classical, pre-relativistic).
Notes: K2-G4; foundation of classical relative-motion

Description (what the sim teaches): **v⃗_AB = v⃗_A − v⃗_B** (velocity of A in B's frame). Same formula in 1D and 2D. Vector subtraction = magnitude + direction (not just magnitude).
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): a⃗_PS = a⃗_PS' (acceleration the same in all inertial frames). **Why F⃗ = ma⃗ holds in any inertial frame** (Newton 1st-law statement). Bridge to T11.
Notes: parent; bridge to T11

Description (what the sim teaches): Δt is the same for all inertial observers (classical). **Breaks down in special relativity** (v approaching c) — flagged for V2 advanced extension. For all Indian-curriculum physics (NCERT, JEE, NEET): time-invariance holds.
Notes: parent; advanced extension flag

Description (what the sim teaches): Boat velocity v_b in still water; river current v_r downstream. **Resultant velocity** v⃗_actual = v⃗_b + v⃗_r. **Two distinct optimisations:** (a) **Shortest time**: boat heads straight across (perpendicular to bank); time = w/v_b; drift downstream = (v_r·w)/v_b. (b) **Shortest path** (= width w, no drift): boat heads upstream at angle θ such that v_b·sin(θ) = v_r; time = w/(v_b·cos(θ)) > w/v_b.
Notes: K2-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "shortest time = shortest path" → no, distinct optimisations

Description (what the sim teaches): Shortest time: head perpendicular. **Crossing-time t = w / v_b** (independent of river current). **Drift x = v_r · t = (v_r · w) / v_b**. Lands x downstream of starting point. Goa-river-tourism-context-anchor.
Notes: parent; **transport/consumer anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Shortest path (no drift): need v⃗_actual perpendicular to current → v_b·sin(θ) = v_r → **θ = arcsin(v_r / v_b)**. Requires v_b > v_r (else cannot avoid drift). Crossing time = w / (v_b · cos(θ)) > w/v_b. **Slower crossing trades time for zero drift.**
Notes: parent; trigonometric application

Description (what the sim teaches): If v_b ≤ v_r: shortest-path solution doesn't exist (boat cannot make headway upstream). Boat drifts regardless of heading. **Real-world example**: Brahmaputra in monsoon (current ~10 km/hr, slow boat 5 km/hr) — boats CANNOT cross straight; drift is unavoidable.
Notes: parent; physical-constraint nano

Description (what the sim teaches): Rain falls with v⃗_rain (typically vertical, downward); observer moves with v⃗_obs (typically horizontal). **Apparent rain velocity** as seen by observer = v⃗_apparent = v⃗_rain − v⃗_obs (Galilean transformation in observer's frame). **Apparent direction** makes angle θ with vertical: tan(θ) = v_obs / v_rain.
Notes: K2-G6; **Indian-monsoon-classic problem**; **cognitive_error_target:** umbrella-tilt-direction

Description (what the sim teaches): **Tilt umbrella INTO direction of apparent rain** (toward direction of motion). Common mis-intuition: "tilt umbrella backward to keep dry" — WRONG; that tilts away from rain. **Mandatory diagram**: stationary observer sees vertical rain → walks east → apparent rain tilts from east-down → umbrella tilts to east.
Notes: parent; K2-G6 cognitive-error countermeasure

Description (what the sim teaches): If observer reverses direction, apparent-rain direction also reverses (mirror image about vertical). **Indian-monsoon biking anchor**: when biking east in rain, tilt umbrella east; when biking west, tilt umbrella west. Reversal demo.
Notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): If v_rain is vertical and v_obs is horizontal: apparent-rain angle from vertical = arctan(v_obs / v_rain). Pure vertical rain (v_obs = 0) → no tilt. Faster observer → larger tilt. Cognitive scaffold for arctan-relationship.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Catalog of canonical relative-motion problem archetypes: (1) train-passenger-platform (Indian Railways daily), (2) aircraft-wind-correction (IndiGo navigation), (3) boat-river-crossing (covered separately as atomic), (4) rain-umbrella (covered separately), (5) cricket-fielder-running-to-catch (sports), (6) two-objects-meeting (general). All use **v⃗_AB = v⃗_A − v⃗_B**.
Notes: catalog wrapper for archetype-set; meta-atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Passenger walks on moving train. Velocity of passenger in ground frame = velocity of passenger in train frame + velocity of train in ground frame. **v⃗_PG = v⃗_PT + v⃗_TG.** Vande Bharat at 130 km/hr; passenger walking at 5 km/hr (forward in train) → 135 km/hr in ground frame. **Indian-Railways anchor.**
Notes: parent; K2-G7; **transport anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): IndiGo aircraft Delhi→Bengaluru: heading H ≠ track T because crosswind. v⃗_aircraft-ground = v⃗_aircraft-air + v⃗_air-ground (wind). Pilot must adjust heading to "crab" into wind. **IMD wind-velocity data + flight-planning anchor.**
Notes: parent; K2-G7; **transport/meteorology anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Cricket ball travelling toward fielder at relative velocity (ball − fielder). Fielder runs in direction such that apparent-velocity vector is straight at them. **Predictive interception** = constant-bearing rule (ball appears stationary in fielder's field of view). IPL fielding-training drill.
Notes: parent; **sports anchor: IPL fielding**

Description (what the sim teaches): Two objects with velocities v⃗_A, v⃗_B and positions r⃗_A, r⃗_B. Relative position r⃗_AB = r⃗_A − r⃗_B; relative velocity v⃗_AB = v⃗_A − v⃗_B. They meet when r⃗_AB(t) = 0. **JEE/NEET problem-template** for two-particle kinematics.
Notes: parent; problem-pattern

Targets misconception: horizontal & vertical motions affect each other
Description (what the sim teaches): A projectile's motion decomposes into TWO INDEPENDENT motions: **horizontal** (uniform, a_x = 0, x = u_x·t) and **vertical** (free-fall, a_y = −g, y = u_y·t − ½gt²). The only link is the shared time t. **Neither component affects the other.**
Notes: PR-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "horizontal & vertical motions affect each other" — monkey-hunter demo

Description (what the sim teaches): Bullet fired horizontally + bullet dropped from same height at same instant: **both hit the ground at the same time** (vertical motion identical; horizontal motion irrelevant to fall-time). Classic demonstration of decoupling. **CBSE Class-11 conceptual-favourite + Mythbusters viral demo.**
Notes: parent; decoupling cognitive proof

Description (what the sim teaches): Hunter aims directly AT a monkey hanging from a branch; monkey drops at the instant of firing. **Dart hits monkey** regardless of dart speed — because both dart and monkey fall by the same ½gt² in the same time. Decoupling masterclass.
Notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Launch at angle θ to horizontal with speed u: **x = u·cosθ·t**; **y = u·sinθ·t − ½gt²**. Eliminate t → **trajectory: y = x·tanθ − gx²/(2u²cos²θ)** = downward parabola. Symmetric about the peak.
Notes: PR-G3; **Diamond candidate**; parabola-trace animation

Description (what the sim teaches): Ascending half (launch→peak) is mirror-image of descending half (peak→landing) on level ground. Time-up = time-down = u·sinθ/g. Speed at any height on the way up = speed at the same height on the way down (directions differ).
Notes: parent; symmetry scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Two projectiles launched simultaneously: in the frame of one, the other moves in a **STRAIGHT LINE at constant velocity** (relative acceleration = (−g) − (−g) = 0). Elegant simplification; collision-condition reduces to straight-line geometry.
Notes: parent; PR-G8; **JEE-Advanced; NCERT-omitted**

Description (what the sim teaches): From decoupling on level ground: **Time-of-flight T = 2u·sinθ/g**; **Max-height H = u²sin²θ/(2g)**; **Range R = u²sin(2θ)/g**. Max range at **θ = 45°** (sin2θ = 1). Complementary angles (θ and 90°−θ) give the SAME range.
Notes: PR-G4; **cognitive_error_target:** "range max at θ=90°" → max at 45°; sin2θ form

Description (what the sim teaches): θ = 30° and θ = 60° give the same range (sin60° = sin120°). Trade-off: 30° = flatter, faster, lower; 60° = higher, slower, same horizontal distance. Cricket-fielder-throw vs lob choice.
Notes: parent; **sports anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): R = u²sin(2θ)/g is maximised when sin(2θ) = 1 → 2θ = 90° → θ = 45°. R_max = u²/g. **Only true on level ground** (incline changes optimal angle — see projectile_on_incline).
Notes: parent; derivation + boundary-condition flag

Description (what the sim teaches): At max-height: **v_y = 0** but **v_x = u·cosθ ≠ 0**. Total speed at top = u·cosθ (purely horizontal). **cognitive_error_target:** "velocity is zero at the top" → only vertical component; horizontal persists. Classic JEE/NEET trap.
Notes: parent; PR-G5 cognitive-error countermeasure

Description (what the sim teaches): Projectile launched HORIZONTALLY from height h with speed u: **time-to-ground t = √(2h/g)** (independent of u — pure free-fall vertically); **horizontal range = u·√(2h/g)**; lands with v_x = u, v_y = √(2gh). Trajectory = half-parabola.
Notes: PR-G6; foundation for drop-vs-shoot demo

Description (what the sim teaches): Two balls launched horizontally from the same height at different speeds (10 m/s and 50 m/s): **both land at the same time** (t = √(2h/g)); the faster one lands farther horizontally. Direct consequence of decoupling.
Notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Ball rolling off a table edge at speed u: classic CBSE/ISC physics-practical (measure table-height + landing-distance → compute g or u). **Indian physics-lab-standard experiment.**
Notes: parent; **education anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Projectile launched up/down an incline of angle α. **Rotated-axis method**: take axes along + perpendicular to incline; gravity components g·sinα (along, decelerating) + g·cosα (perpendicular). Range up-incline R_up = 2u²·sin(θ−α)·cosθ / (g·cos²α); max-range angle on incline = 45° + α/2 (up) or 45° − α/2 (down).
Notes: PR-G7; **DCM-problem-heavy; HCV+DCM only (NCERT-omitted)**; **cognitive_error_target:** "max-range angle always 45°" → 45°±α/2 on incline

Description (what the sim teaches): On an incline, choose x' along the slope + y' perpendicular. Then g resolves into −g·sinα (x') and −g·cosα (y'). Projectile lands when y' = 0 again. **Reduces incline-projectile to standard decoupling in rotated frame.**
Notes: parent; method scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Artillery + DRDO ballistic computations use elevation-angle vs range tables (corrected for air-resistance + terrain-slope). The incline-projectile + max-range-angle physics underpins range-table construction. **Indian-Army + DRDO Pokhran anchor.**
Notes: parent; **defence anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Ideal projectile (parabola, symmetric) vs real projectile (asymmetric trajectory — steeper descent, shorter range, lower peak) due to air drag ∝ v². Cricket-ball swing + golf-ball dimples + artillery drag-correction. **Deferred to V2 advanced.**
Notes: parent; PR-G9; V2 flag

Description (what the sim teaches): **Angular position θ** (radians, measured from reference radius); **angular velocity ω = dθ/dt** (rad/s); **angular acceleration α = dω/dt** (rad/s²). Direct rotational analogs of linear position/velocity/acceleration (T6). For rotation, these describe the motion compactly (one variable θ vs two x,y).
Notes: MP-G3; **cognitive_error_target:** "ω in degrees/sec" → SI radians

Description (what the sim teaches): For constant α: **ω = ω₀ + αt**; **θ = ω₀t + ½αt²**; **ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ**. **Identical form to T6 linear equations** (x→θ, v→ω, a→α). Same derivation; same problem-solving decision-tree. Reinforces linear-angular structural analogy.
Notes: parent; MP-G4; linear-analogy scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): **v = ωr** (tangential speed); **a_tangential = αr**; **arc-length s = rθ**. Convert between angular description (compact) and linear description (intuitive). Foundation for T15 rotational mechanics (every linear quantity has an angular analog).
Notes: parent; MP-G7; bridge to T15

Description (what the sim teaches): Radians (not degrees) make v = ωr and s = rθ clean (no conversion factor). 1 rad = arc-length equal to radius; full circle = 2π rad. **All physics formulae assume radians.** Common student error: plugging degrees into ω formulae.
Notes: parent; cognitive-error countermeasure

Description (what the sim teaches): Even at CONSTANT SPEED, circular motion is ACCELERATED because the velocity DIRECTION continuously changes. The acceleration points toward the centre: **a_c = v²/r = ω²r = vω**. This is a purely KINEMATIC fact (geometric consequence of direction-change) — it exists BEFORE any discussion of what force causes it (that's T10).
Notes: MP-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "constant speed = zero acceleration" → velocity-vector changes

Description (what the sim teaches): Geometric proof: draw velocity vectors v⃗₁, v⃗₂ at two nearby points on the circle. Δv⃗ = v⃗₂ − v⃗₁ points toward the centre. As Δt→0, a⃗ = Δv⃗/Δt is exactly centripetal. **Diamond-candidate animation**: rotating velocity-vector + Δv⃗-construction showing inward direction.
Notes: parent; geometric-derivation scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): a_c = v²/r (linear-speed form) = ω²r (angular form) = vω (mixed). All equivalent via v = ωr. **Use whichever the problem gives.** Decision-tree pedagogy (mirrors T6 kinematic-equation choice).
Notes: parent; formula-choice scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): Speed |v⃗| = constant; velocity v⃗ = changing (direction rotates). **Acceleration ≠ 0** despite constant speed. The deepest conceptual point of circular kinematics. **cognitive_error_target** countermeasure: explicit speed-vs-velocity distinction animation.
Notes: parent; MP-G5 cognitive scaffold

Targets misconception: acceleration in circular motion is always centripetal
Description (what the sim teaches): For NON-UNIFORM circular motion (changing speed): total acceleration has TWO perpendicular components: **a_tangential = dv/dt = rα** (along velocity, changes SPEED) + **a_radial = v²/r = ω²r** (toward centre, changes DIRECTION). **a_total = √(a_t² + a_r²)**; direction makes angle arctan(a_t/a_r) with the radius.
Notes: MP-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "acceleration in circular motion is always centripetal" → only uniform; non-uniform adds tangential

Description (what the sim teaches): Car accelerating while rounding a bend: a_tangential (speeding up along the path) + a_radial (centripetal, turning). Total acceleration tilts forward-of-radius. **Indian-driving anchor**: accelerating out of a curve on Mumbai-Pune Expressway.
Notes: parent; **transport anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Uniform circular motion = the special case where a_tangential = 0 (constant speed). Only a_radial (centripetal) remains. Non-uniform is the general case; uniform is the simplification. Cognitive-hierarchy scaffold.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): UCM = circular motion at CONSTANT speed. Characterised by: constant ω; constant a_c = v²/r (magnitude constant, direction rotating); **period T = 2πr/v = 2π/ω**; **frequency f = 1/T = ω/(2π)**. The canonical kinematic template for all rotational phenomena.
Notes: catalog-anchor atomic; **cognitive_error_target:** "uniform = no acceleration" (re-emphasis of MP-G5)

Description (what the sim teaches): T = 2πr/v = 2π/ω; f = 1/T = ω/(2π); ω = 2πf (angular frequency). **Bridge to T17 SHM**: projection of UCM onto a diameter = SHM (same ω). **Bridge to T16**: satellite orbital period from UCM + gravity (T16 owns the dynamics).
Notes: parent; MP-G8; bridge to T17 + T16

Description (what the sim teaches): Ceiling-fan (~300-350 rpm = ω ≈ 31-37 rad/s); record-player/turntable (33⅓ rpm); washing-machine-spin (~1400 rpm). Everyday Indian-household UCM anchors. Compute ω, v at blade-tip, a_c.
Notes: parent; **consumer anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): ISRO geostationary satellite (GSAT series): orbital period 24 hr → ω = 2π/(24×3600) rad/s; orbital radius ~42,164 km → v ≈ 3.07 km/s; a_c = v²/r (the KINEMATIC fact; T16 supplies that gravity PROVIDES this a_c). Pure-kinematics version; dynamics in T16.
Notes: parent; **space anchor**; bridge to T16

Description (what the sim teaches): HCV1 §7.1. s = rθ. Radian convention. V1.
Notes: HCV1 §7.1. s = rθ. Radian convention. V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): ω = dθ/dt; v = rω. V1.
Notes: ω = dθ/dt; v = rω. V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): α = dω/dt; a_t = rα. V1.
Notes: α = dω/dt; a_t = rα. V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): HCV1 §7.2. Coordinate choice that matters. V1.
Notes: HCV1 §7.2. Coordinate choice that matters. V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): a_r = v²/r = ω²r. Pure kinematics, no force. HCV1 Eq 7.9. V1 ABSOLUTE PRIORITY (highest IN-degree of Topic 10).
Notes: a_r = v²/r = ω²r. Pure kinematics, no force. HCV1 Eq 7.9. V1 ABSOLUTE PRIORITY (highest IN-degree of Topic 10).

Description (what the sim teaches): a_t = dv/dt. Zero in uniform circular motion. V1.
Notes: a_t = dv/dt. Zero in uniform circular motion. V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): The "default" case. dv/dt = 0. V1 ABSOLUTE PRIORITY (hub concept for 3+ other topics).
Notes: The "default" case. dv/dt = 0. V1 ABSOLUTE PRIORITY (hub concept for 3+ other topics).

Description (what the sim teaches): Both components present; total a = √(a_r²+a_t²). V1.
Notes: Both components present; total a = √(a_r²+a_t²). V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): F_c = mv²/r toward center. "Not a new force — give it a name" framing per HCV1 Points to Ponder #5. V1 ABSOLUTE PRIORITY.
Notes: F_c = mv²/r toward center. "Not a new force — give it a name" framing per HCV1 Points to Ponder #5. V1 ABSOLUTE PRIORITY.

Description (what the sim teaches): "Which real force provides centripetal?" Heuristic-rich. EPIC-C STATE_1 candidate (the wrong belief: "centripetal force is a force by itself"). V1.
Notes: "Which real force provides centripetal?" Heuristic-rich. EPIC-C STATE_1 candidate (the wrong belief: "centripetal force is a force by itself"). V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): v_max = √(μsRg); independent of mass. NCERT Eq 5.18. **Cross-references Friction A27.** V1.
Notes: v_max = √(μsRg); independent of mass. NCERT Eq 5.18. **Cross-references Friction A27.** V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): v_o = √(Rg tanθ). The "design speed". NCERT Eq 5.22. V1.
Notes: v_o = √(Rg tanθ). The "design speed". NCERT Eq 5.22. V1.

Description (what the sim teaches): v_max = √(Rg(μ+tanθ)/(1-μtanθ)). NCERT Eq 5.21. V1 — flag `allow_deep_dive: true` (the (1-μtanθ) denominator is a JEE trap).

Description (what the sim teaches): A body at rest stays at rest, in uniform motion stays in uniform motion, UNLESS net external force acts; operationally defines an inertial frame
Notes: NL-G2; foundational; bundles inertia + frame definition

Description (what the sim teaches): Inertial frame: 1st law holds. Non-inertial: 1st law fails → must add pseudo-forces. Earth's surface is approximately inertial for most problems.
Notes: parent: newton_first_law_atomic; **cognitive_error_target:** "1st law fails in moving cars" → frame-specification is mandatory before applying 1st law

Description (what the sim teaches): All inertial frames are equivalent; F = ma in one inertial frame holds in any other with the same form. Foundation of pre-relativistic mechanics
Notes: parent: newton_first_law_atomic; bridge to T-relativity (deferred)

Description (what the sim teaches): F_net = ma; in vector form ΣF = m·a; central quantitative law of mechanics
Required-by (unlocks): [newton_third_law_atomic, work_energy_theorem(T13), gravity_force_atomic, normal_force_atomic, friction_force_atomic, connected_bodies_atomic, block_on_incline_atomic, simple_harmonic_motion(T17), first_law_atomic(T26)]
Notes: NL-G3; **Diamond candidate** — F=ma is the most-iconic mechanics sim

Description (what the sim teaches): Generalised form: F = dp/dt = d(mv)/dt; reduces to F=ma when m constant; needed for rockets, falling chains, conveyor-belt-sand-loading problems
Notes: parent: newton_second_law_atomic; cognitive_error_target: "F=ma is universal" → F=dp/dt is universal; F=ma is the constant-m specialisation

Description (what the sim teaches): J = ∫F dt = Δp; impulse equals change in momentum. Carom-strike, cricket-bat-ball collision time-integrals all use this
Notes: parent: newton_second_law_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): ISRO PSLV/GSLV: M dv/dt = u·(dm/dt) − Mg; thrust = u·(rate of fuel ejection); cross-bridge to T14 momentum-conservation and Tsiolkovsky equation
Notes: parent: newton_second_law_atomic; **space-strand anchor: ISRO**

Targets misconception: action and reaction on same body
Description (what the sim teaches): Every action force has an equal-and-opposite reaction force; the action-reaction PAIR acts on TWO DIFFERENT bodies
Notes: NL-G4; **cognitive_error_target:** "action and reaction on same body" → most-prevalent misconception in NCERT Ch.5

Description (what the sim teaches): Side-by-side diagram: hand pushes wall (force ON wall by hand); wall pushes hand (force ON hand by wall). Both forces act, but on different bodies → they DO NOT cancel. **Cognitive-error-prevention nano** (mandatory per NL-G4).
Notes: parent: newton_third_law_atomic; NL-G4 cognitive_error_target nano

Description (what the sim teaches): Walking: foot pushes earth back; earth pushes foot forward → person accelerates. Rowing: oar pushes water back; water pushes oar forward. Swimming: hand pushes water back; water pushes hand forward. **Three Indian everyday examples**
Notes: parent: newton_third_law_atomic; **residential/sports strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): F_g = mg downward on Earth's surface (g ≈ 9.8 m/s²); more generally F = GMm/r²; mass × gravitational-field-strength
Notes: NL-G6; bridge atomic to T16 Gravitation

Description (what the sim teaches): Contact force perpendicular to the surface, magnitude adjusts to whatever keeps body from penetrating surface (it's NOT always equal to mg)
Notes: NL-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "N = mg always" → N depends on what equation you write, varies with incline-angle/acceleration

Description (what the sim teaches): In accelerating lift: N = m(g + a) upward acceleration; N = m(g − a) downward acceleration; N = 0 free-fall (passenger feels weightless). **Everyday Indian elevator examples**
Notes: parent: normal_force_atomic; residential strand

Description (what the sim teaches): Force transmitted along a string/rope; tension is uniform along massless inextensible string; tension at any point = force pulling along string at that point
Notes: NL-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "tension equals weight always" → tension equals force needed to maintain motion-state

Description (what the sim teaches): String mass = 0 → tension uniform along string. String inextensible → tangential velocity along string is same at every point. These are TWO separate idealisations bundled into "ideal string."
Notes: parent: tension_force_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Force opposing relative motion or tendency of motion between contacting surfaces; static μ_s (no relative motion yet) vs kinetic μ_k (motion occurring); cross-link to T12 for full derivation
Notes: NL-G6; cross-link to T12 Friction full atomic; T11 ships intro atomic only

Description (what the sim teaches): F = −kx (Hooke's law); restoring force proportional to displacement from natural length; sign convention "minus" indicates restoring
Notes: NL-G6; **bridge to T17 SHM** (most-cited mechanics-to-oscillations atomic)

Description (what the sim teaches): Any non-internal force from outside the system (push, pull, applied tension, applied gravity-field). The "F" in F=ma typically refers to applied + reaction-from-environment
Notes: NL-G6; conceptual atomic (no canonical sim)

Description (what the sim teaches): In non-inertial frame with acceleration a_frame: pseudo-force F_pseudo = −m·a_frame acts on every mass m in the frame. Real to observer in that frame; vanishes in inertial frame.
Notes: NL-G7; **cognitive_error_target:** "pseudo-force isn't real" → it's real to non-inertial-frame observer (forces are frame-dependent)

Description (what the sim teaches): In rotating frame: F_centrifugal = mω²r outward; explains why water-bucket-on-string works; classic NCERT/HCV example. Equivalent to centripetal-force-balance from inertial frame view.
Notes: parent: pseudo_force_atomic; cross-link to T10

Description (what the sim teaches): Passenger in accelerating train (a_train forward): pseudo-force m·a_train backward on passenger; passenger leans back. Indian Railways everyday experience.
Notes: parent: pseudo_force_atomic; **transport strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Cognitive tool: isolate ONE body, draw ALL external forces ACTING ON IT, set up F_net = ma equations. THE universal mechanics workflow.
Notes: NL-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "drawing forces ON other bodies in same FBD" → FBD-of-ONE-body-only training

Description (what the sim teaches): Step 1: identify body. Step 2: list all forces ACTING ON it (gravity, normal, tension, friction, applied, spring). Step 3: choose axes. Step 4: resolve. Step 5: write ΣF_x = ma_x and ΣF_y = ma_y.
Notes: parent: free_body_diagram_atomic; procedural nano

Description (what the sim teaches): Particle in equilibrium: ΣF = 0 → ΣF_x = 0 AND ΣF_y = 0; static equilibrium or dynamic-equilibrium (constant velocity); equivalent to 1st law special case
Notes: Bridging atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Two strings joined at a point with weight hanging: ΣF_x = 0 (horizontal tension components cancel); ΣF_y = 0 (vertical components support weight). Classic JEE example.
Notes: parent: equilibrium_of_particle_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Equations relating motion of different connected bodies: string-inextensibility, pulley-massless, rigid-body, surface-rigidity. Each constraint reduces # of independent variables.
Notes: NL-G8; bundles all kinematic constraint patterns

Description (what the sim teaches): Single fixed pulley + inextensible string: |v₁| = |v₂| but in opposite directions; sign convention via constraint equation x₁ + x₂ = L. Differentiate twice to get a₁ = −a₂.
Notes: parent: mechanical_constraints_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Two or more bodies connected by string/rod/pulley; apply F=ma to each body separately + constraint equation; combine for system solution
Notes: NL-G9; **Diamond candidate** — Atwood machine sim

Description (what the sim teaches): Two masses m₁ + m₂ over single fixed pulley: a = g(m₁−m₂)/(m₁+m₂); T = 2gm₁m₂/(m₁+m₂). Asymmetric acceleration is visually compelling.
Notes: parent: connected_bodies_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): m₁ on table, string over pulley at edge, m₂ hangs: a = m₂g/(m₁+m₂); if friction on table, a = (m₂ − μm₁)g/(m₁+m₂). Classic JEE template.

Description (what the sim teaches): Normal component of contact force
Notes: parent: A1. Sources: HCV1 §6.1, NCERT §5.9.

Description (what the sim teaches): Friction = parallel component (tangential)
Notes: parent: A1. Sources: HCV1 §6.1 (explicit), NCERT §5.9 ("parallel to the surfaces").

Description (what the sim teaches): R = √(N² + f²)
Notes: parent: A1. Sources: HCV1 §6.1. `granularity_question: candidate_micro`.

Description (what the sim teaches): "Would-move-if-not-for-friction" scenario; static friction acts on bodies at rest with applied force
Notes: parent: A2. Sources: NCERT §5.9.1 ("static friction opposes impending motion"), HCV1 §6.3. `granularity_question: candidate_micro`.

Description (what the sim teaches): Slipping ⇒ kinetic regime; no slipping ⇒ static regime
Notes: parent: A2. Sources: HCV1 §6.2 ("when relative motion has started").

Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 Eq. 6.1, NCERT Eq. 5.15, DCM1 (all problem solutions). **v1?:** TRUE.

Description (what the sim teaches): f doubles when N doubles (linearity of f-N relation)
Notes: parent: A3. Sources: HCV1 §6.2 (Law 1).

Description (what the sim teaches): Applied 2 N → friction 2 N; applied 5 N → friction 5 N (up to limit)
Notes: parent: A4. Sources: NCERT §5.9.1 ("equal and opposite to the applied force"), HCV1 §6.3.

Description (what the sim teaches): Graph: 45° line until f_max, then drops to μ_k N
Notes: parent: A4. Sources: HCV1 Fig 6.4 (graph). Strong visual primitive — high simulation value.

Description (what the sim teaches): f_s ≤ μ_s N; equality at impending motion
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §6.3 + Eq. 6.5, NCERT Eq. 5.13/5.14, DCM1 Concept block. **v1?:** TRUE.

Description (what the sim teaches): At the instant motion is about to start, f_s = μ_s N (boundary condition)
Notes: parent: A5. Sources: NCERT §5.9.1 ("static friction does not exist by itself"), HCV1 §6.3. `granularity_question: candidate_micro`.

Description (what the sim teaches): Overshoot: at breakaway, friction drops from μ_s N to μ_k N → block accelerates
Notes: parent: A5. Sources: HCV1 §6.3, NCERT §5.9.1.

Description (what the sim teaches): Empirical: μ_s > μ_k (e.g., steel-steel: μ_s=0.7, μ_k=0.6)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §6.3 ("found experimentally that μ_k is less than μ_s"), NCERT §5.9.1, coefficient table HCV1 §6.6. **v1?:** TRUE. Real-world: explains ABS brakes.

Description (what the sim teaches): Friction direction = opposite to **relative** velocity of object w.r.t. surface (not absolute motion!)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §6.2 ("direction of f_k on the body … is opposite to the velocity of the body with respect to the surface"); NCERT §5.9.1 ("frictional force opposes relative motion"); DCM1 Type 7 (explicit, two-block case). **v1?:** TRUE. **CRITICAL misconception:** "friction always opposes motion" → wrong (it opposes RELATIVE motion; can drive motion — see A26).

Description (what the sim teaches): For kinetic friction: direction = −v_rel
Notes: parent: A7. Sources: HCV1 §6.2.

Description (what the sim teaches): For static friction: direction = whatever opposes the would-be slip direction
Notes: parent: A7. Sources: HCV1 §6.3 ("Finding the Direction of Static Friction"), NCERT Ex 5.7 (box-in-train).

Description (what the sim teaches): Static friction direction is set by Newton's 2nd law on the constraint, not assumed
Notes: parent: A7. Sources: HCV1 §6.3 (worked example: which way does friction point on a block in an accelerating train?).

Description (what the sim teaches): Counterintuitive: same block on same surface, friction force doesn't depend on which face is down
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §6.4 (Law 4: "limiting friction is independent of the area of contact"); NCERT §5.9.1 ("independent of the area of contact"). **v1?:** TRUE. Strong EPIC-C: students expect "bigger area = more friction" → wrong. Visual: two blocks, same mass, one flat one on edge, identical f_max.

Description (what the sim teaches): μ is a dimensionless constant; depends ONLY on the pair of surfaces in contact, NOT on N, A, v
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §6.2 (μ_k defn), §6.3 (μ_s defn), §6.6 (coefficient table with 9 surface pairs); NCERT §5.9.1 (Eq. 5.13–5.15). **v1?:** TRUE. Visual: HCV1 §6.6 table — wood-wood 0.20, glass-glass 1.00, ice-ice 0.10, etc.

Description (what the sim teaches): f_k stays the same whether block moves at 1 m/s or 10 m/s (within ordinary speeds)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §6.2, §6.4 (Law 2: "independent of the speed"); NCERT §5.9.1 ("nearly independent of velocity"). **v1?:** TRUE. Counterintuitive — students often think "faster = more friction".

Description (what the sim teaches): Rolling friction is 2–3 orders of magnitude smaller than sliding friction; ball bearings exploit this
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §6.6 ("Rolling Friction" subsection); NCERT §5.9.1 ("Rolling friction" paragraph: ball bearings, air cushion). **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (depends on rotation; not foundational for V1 friction story).

Description (what the sim teaches): In pure rolling, contact point has zero instantaneous velocity → no kinetic friction
Notes: parent: A11. Sources: HCV1 §6.6, NCERT §5.9.1.

Description (what the sim teaches): Convert sliding contact to rolling contact → reduce friction
Notes: parent: A11. Sources: HCV1 Fig 6.10, NCERT Fig 5.13(a).

Description (what the sim teaches): Hovercraft / air bearing: no surface contact → no friction
Notes: parent: A11. Sources: HCV1 §6.6, NCERT Fig 5.13(b) "inflated balloon on plastic disc".

Description (what the sim teaches): Molecular bonds at the contact points break/reform → energy goes to heat

Description (what the sim teaches): V_CoM = (Σmᵢvᵢ)/M = P_total/M; CoM moves with system's average velocity. **cognitive_error_target:** "CoM is a material point" → CoM is a MATHEMATICAL location, may be outside any actual body
Notes: parent: centre_of_mass_atomic; MC-G2 cognitive_error_target

Description (what the sim teaches): p = mv (single body); P_system = Σmᵢvᵢ = M·V_CoM (system); SI units kg·m/s. Momentum is the natural quantity behind Newton's 2nd law (F = dp/dt)
Notes: MC-G3; foundational atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): J = ∫F dt = Δp; impulse equals change in momentum; cross-link to T11 `impulse_change_in_momentum_nano`
Notes: parent: linear_momentum_atomic; cross-link to T11

Description (what the sim teaches): When no external force acts on a system (or net external force is zero), total linear momentum is conserved: ΣP_initial = ΣP_final. Follows from Newton's 3rd law applied to internal forces (they cancel pairwise)
Notes: MC-G4; **cognitive_error_target:** "momentum conserved always" → only when ΣF_ext = 0

Description (what the sim teaches): Conservation holds ONLY when net external force is zero. Gravity acting on bouncing ball + earth: SYSTEM is conserved (ball + earth), but ball-alone is NOT. **cognitive_error_target nano**.
Notes: parent: conservation_of_momentum_atomic; MC-G4 cognitive_error_target

Description (what the sim teaches): One body → multiple fragments; total p conserved; KE INCREASES (internal energy → KE). Time-reversed perfectly-inelastic-collision. ISRO multi-stage rocket = controlled explosion at staging events.
Notes: parent: conservation_of_momentum_atomic; **space-strand anchor: ISRO multi-stage separation**

Description (what the sim teaches): 1D collision conserving BOTH momentum AND kinetic energy. Closed-form solution: v₁' = ((m₁−m₂)v₁ + 2m₂v₂)/(m₁+m₂); v₂' = ((m₂−m₁)v₂ + 2m₁v₁)/(m₁+m₂). Equal-mass case: simple velocity exchange.
Notes: MC-G5; **Diamond candidate** — equal-mass-velocity-exchange visualisation

Description (what the sim teaches): m₁ = m₂: v₁' = v₂; v₂' = v₁. Classic Newton's cradle / billiards visualization.
Notes: parent: elastic_collision_1d_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): 2D oblique elastic collision: conservation of momentum applied PER COMPONENT (x and y), plus conservation of KE; results in coupled equations. Geometric significance: post-collision velocity directions form right angle for equal masses
Notes: MC-G5; JEE-Advanced staple

Description (what the sim teaches): When equal masses collide elastically with one at rest: post-collision velocity vectors are perpendicular. Classic billiards-shot geometry.
Notes: parent: elastic_collision_2d_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Momentum conserved; KE NOT conserved (some converted to heat, sound, deformation). Coefficient of restitution e ∈ [0,1] characterises energy retention
Notes: MC-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "real collisions are elastic" → most real collisions are partly inelastic. **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed `coefficient_of_restitution_atomic` from Requires — teach the phenomenon (KE lost) first; e quantifies it (now in Required-by).

Description (what the sim teaches): Bodies stick together after collision; common post-collision velocity v_f = (m₁v₁ + m₂v₂)/(m₁+m₂); maximum KE-loss case; e = 0
Notes: MC-G6; canonical-formula atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Two Indian Railways wagons couple on track: bodies stick (Janney coupler engagement) → perfectly inelastic; coupling-and-uncoupling at Mughalsarai/Bandra/Itarsi yards.
Notes: parent: perfectly_inelastic_collision_atomic; **transport-strand anchor: Indian Railways**

Targets misconception: e is property of one ball
Description (what the sim teaches): e = |v_separation|/|v_approach|; e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic, 0 < e < 1 general inelastic. Property of the COLLISION PAIR (not single object)
Notes: MC-G7; **cognitive_error_target:** "e is property of one ball" → e is property of collision pair

Description (what the sim teaches): Ball dropped from height h₀ bounces to h₁ = e²h₀; nth bounce → h_n = e^(2n)h₀; series of decreasing heights
Notes: parent: coefficient_of_restitution_atomic; everyday Indian observation

Description (what the sim teaches): Cricket ball-bat collision: e ≈ 0.45 for hardened-leather + willow; affects six-vs-four trajectory; depends on bat-thickness, ball-condition, swing-speed. Indian cricket-equipment-industry context.
Notes: parent: coefficient_of_restitution_atomic; **sports-strand anchor: Indian cricket**

Description (what the sim teaches): Tsiolkovsky: v − v₀ = u·ln(M₀/M); rocket gains speed by ejecting mass at exhaust-speed u; specific impulse I_sp = u/g₀ measures fuel efficiency
Notes: MC-G8; **Diamond candidate** — Tsiolkovsky derivation sim with ISRO mission examples

Description (what the sim teaches): PSLV: 4 stages (PS1 solid + PS2 liquid + PS3 solid + PS4 liquid); each stage burns + separates (explosion → reverse collision). Total Δv achievable via Tsiolkovsky summed across stages.
Notes: parent: rocket_equation_atomic; **space-strand anchor: ISRO PSLV**

Description (what the sim teaches): I_sp = u/g₀ in seconds; chemical rocket: 250-450 s; ion thruster: 1000-5000 s; ISRO LAM (liquid apogee motor) ~300 s
Notes: parent: rocket_equation_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂); converts 2-body problem to equivalent 1-body problem of mass μ moving in central potential. Foundation of 2-body collision + Rutherford scattering + Bohr H-atom correction
Notes: MC-G9; **cross-cluster bridge atomic** to T47

Description (what the sim teaches): Alpha + gold-nucleus: μ ≈ m_α since m_Au >> m_α; cross-link to T47 atomic-models
Notes: parent: reduced_mass_atomic; cross-link to T47

Description (what the sim teaches): Rigid body = all internal distances constant; rotation about fixed axis = every point traces a circle of fixed radius around axis
Notes: Foundational; defines the rotational system

Description (what the sim teaches): Pure rotation = points trace circles around fixed axis (axis is stationary). Pure translation = all points have same velocity. General motion = translation + rotation about CoM
Notes: parent: rigid_body_rotation_atomic

Targets misconception: angular and linear kinematics are different physics
Description (what the sim teaches): ω = dθ/dt; α = dω/dt; ω = ω₀ + αt; θ = ω₀t + ½αt²; ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ. **Rotational analog of T6 linear kinematics.**
Notes: RM-G3; **cognitive_error_target:** "angular and linear kinematics are different physics" → same math, different variables (mass↔I; F↔τ; v↔ω; a↔α)

Description (what the sim teaches): Linear velocity of point at radius r: v = ωr. Linear acceleration tangential: a_t = αr. Linear acceleration centripetal: a_c = ω²r. **Three relations bridging linear ↔ angular.**
Notes: parent: rotational_kinematics_atomic

Targets misconception: I is a property of body alone
Description (what the sim teaches): I = Σmᵢrᵢ² (discrete); I = ∫r² dm (continuous). I is "rotational mass" — resists change in rotational motion. **DEPENDS ON CHOSEN AXIS.**
Notes: RM-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "I is a property of body alone" → I depends on the AXIS

Description (what the sim teaches): Solid sphere: I = (2/5)MR². Hollow sphere: (2/3)MR². Disc/cylinder about axis: ½MR². Ring: MR². Rod about end: ML²/3. Rod about centre: ML²/12. **Table for V1 reference.**
Notes: parent: moment_of_inertia_atomic; **canonical-formulas table**

Description (what the sim teaches): I_parallel = I_CoM + Md²; moment of inertia about any axis parallel to CoM-axis equals I_CoM + (mass × distance²). Allows computing I about arbitrary axis.
Notes: parent: moment_of_inertia_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): For PLANAR body in xy-plane: I_z = I_x + I_y. Only valid for thin laminar (planar) bodies; not for 3D solids. **cognitive_error_target:** "perpendicular-axis applies to all bodies" → planar only.
Notes: parent: moment_of_inertia_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): τ = r × F (vector form); τ = rF sin θ (magnitude); τ is to angular motion what F is to linear motion. SI unit: N·m
Notes: RM-G4; **cross-link to T36 magnetic dipole torque**

Description (what the sim teaches): τ = F × d_perpendicular = F × (r sin θ). Moment arm = perpendicular distance from axis to line-of-action of force. **cognitive_error_target:** "torque = r·F always" → r sin θ matters; force ALONG r contributes ZERO torque
Notes: parent: torque_atomic; RM-G12 cognitive_error_target

Description (what the sim teaches): Pair of equal-opposite forces with separation d: net force = 0; net torque = F·d (independent of axis choice). Door-handle, wrench-on-bolt examples
Notes: parent: torque_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): τ_net = Iα; central quantitative law of rotational dynamics. Rotational analog of F_net = ma
Notes: RM-G5; **Diamond candidate**

Description (what the sim teaches): Side-by-side table: m↔I, F↔τ, v↔ω, a↔α, F=ma↔τ=Iα, p=mv↔L=Iω, KE=½mv²↔KE=½Iω². **Cognitive scaffold nano.**
Notes: parent: tau_eq_i_alpha_atomic; conceptual scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): W_torque = ∫τ dθ; rotational KE = ½Iω²; total KE for rolling = ½mv² + ½Iω². Bridges T13 Work-Energy linear treatment
Notes: RM-G8

Description (what the sim teaches): L = Iω (rotational) = r × p (general); SI unit: kg·m²/s; vector quantity; direction by right-hand rule along ω
Notes: RM-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "L is a scalar" → L is a vector along the rotation axis

Description (what the sim teaches): For a single particle: L = r × p; for system: L_total = Σ(rᵢ × pᵢ); axis-independent definition
Notes: parent: angular_momentum_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): When no external torque acts (τ_ext = 0): L_total = constant. Derivation: τ_ext = dL/dt = 0 → L = const. **Pirouette, planetary Kepler-2, gyroscope-stability all apply**
Notes: RM-G6

Description (what the sim teaches): Ice-skater / Bharatnatyam dancer pirouette: arms pulled in → I decreases → ω increases (L = Iω constant). Indian classical-dance everyday observation
Notes: parent: conservation_of_angular_momentum_atomic; **dance/sports-strand anchor: Bharatnatyam + ice-skating + gymnastics**

Description (what the sim teaches): Geostationary satellites use reaction-wheel-based attitude control: spinning wheel inside satellite changes satellite's angular momentum; ISRO INSAT/GSAT/Cartosat all use this
Notes: parent: conservation_of_angular_momentum_atomic; **space-strand anchor: ISRO**

Description (what the sim teaches): Law of equal areas: planets sweep equal areas in equal times → dA/dt = L/2m = constant (when L conserved under central force). Cross-link to T16 Gravitation
Notes: parent: conservation_of_angular_momentum_atomic; cross-link to T16
