Description (what the sim teaches): Rolling-without-slipping condition: v_CoM − Rω = 0 → contact point has zero velocity at any instant. **Diamond visualisation candidate**: trace path of contact point = cycloid
Notes: parent: pure_rolling_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Race down same incline: solid sphere wins (smallest I/mR² = 2/5); disc 2nd (1/2); hollow sphere 3rd (2/3); ring last (1.0). Iconic Class 11 demo

Notes: δ=0: A = A₁ + A₂. Constructive.

Notes: δ=π: A = \|A₁ − A₂\|. Destructive. Zero amplitude if A₁=A₂.

Notes: Represent each SHM as a vector of magnitude A_i at angle δ_i. Resultant by parallelogram. HCV1 §12.11 Vector Method, Fig 12.16.

Notes: Per S-G6 split (B). Two SHMs along perpendicular axes (x and y). General path is ellipse: x²/A₁² + y²/A₂² − 2xy cos δ/(A₁A₂) = sin²δ. HCV1 §12.11(B).

Description (what the sim teaches): δ=0: y = (A₂/A₁) x. Line through origin, slope +A₂/A₁. Fig 12.19.
Notes: δ=0: y = (A₂/A₁) x. Line through origin, slope +A₂/A₁. Fig 12.19.

Notes: δ=π: y = −(A₂/A₁) x. Line, negative slope. Fig 12.20.

Notes: δ=π/2: x²/A₁² + y²/A₂² = 1. Standard ellipse along the coordinate axes. Fig 12.21.

Description (what the sim teaches): A₁=A₂ AND δ=π/2: x²+y² = A². Pure circle. (Foreshadow of UCM→SHM bridge.)
Notes: A₁=A₂ AND δ=π/2: x²+y² = A². Pure circle. (Foreshadow of UCM→SHM bridge.)

Description (what the sim teaches): Rigid-body assumption (T11-T17) is an idealisation; real bodies deform under applied forces; elasticity is the theory of small-deformation regime
Notes: Conceptual atomic; bridge from Mechanics

Description (what the sim teaches): Stress = F/A (force per unit area; SI: Pa = N/m²). Strain = ΔL/L (dimensionless ratio of deformation to original dimension). **Three stress types: tensile (axial), shear (tangential), hydraulic (uniform pressure)**.
Notes: EL-G1; foundational; **Diamond candidate**

Description (what the sim teaches): Force F along axis: F/A acts along normal. Tensile = pulling apart; compressive = pushing together. Same magnitude/formula, opposite directions.
Notes: parent: stress_strain_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Force F tangential to surface area A: F/A acts in plane. Pure shear deforms a rectangle into a parallelogram. Cross-link to T15 torque (rotational analog).
Notes: parent: stress_strain_atomic; **cross-cluster link to T15**

Description (what the sim teaches): Uniform pressure P acts on all surfaces of body: volumetric stress = P (SI: Pa). Strain = ΔV/V. Applies in deep oceans, hydraulic presses, atmospheric compression.
Notes: parent: stress_strain_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Within elastic limit: stress ∝ strain → stress = E × strain (where E is the relevant modulus). Linear regime only.
Notes: EL-G2; **cognitive_error_target:** "Hooke's law applies always" → only within elastic limit

Description (what the sim teaches): Graphical representation showing: proportional limit (Hooke's law) → elastic limit → yield point → plastic regime → ultimate stress → fracture. Different materials show distinct curves (steel: brittle; rubber: ultra-elastic; glass: brittle no-yield)
Notes: EL-G3; **Diamond candidate** — material-comparison sim

Description (what the sim teaches): (1) Proportional limit: Hooke holds. (2) Elastic limit: deformation reverses on unloading; non-linear in this band. (3) Yield point: plastic deformation begins. (4) Plastic regime: permanent deformation. (5) Ultimate stress: peak before fracture. **Cognitive scaffold nano.**
Notes: parent: stress_strain_curve_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Ductile (large plastic regime): copper, aluminum, gold — drawn into wire. Brittle (negligible plastic regime): glass, cast iron — shatter without warning. Indian materials industry context.
Notes: parent: stress_strain_curve_atomic

Targets misconception: Y depends on size of body
Description (what the sim teaches): Y = (F/A) / (ΔL/L) = stress / longitudinal strain; characterises tensile/compressive deformation. **Industrial values:** steel ~200 GPa, aluminum ~70 GPa, brass ~91 GPa, rubber ~0.01-0.1 GPa
Notes: EL-G4; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "Y depends on size of body" → Y is material property only; geometry cancels

Description (what the sim teaches): Steel Y ≈ 200 GPa (high stiffness; structural). Aluminum Y ≈ 70 GPa (lighter; aerospace). Rubber Y ≈ 0.01-0.1 GPa (extremely flexible; tires + seals). **Materials-selection table.**
Notes: parent: youngs_modulus_atomic; **industry anchor: Indian construction + automotive**

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian Railways rail tracks: high-carbon-steel Y ≈ 210 GPa; spec'd to specific elongation under thermal expansion + dynamic load. Tata Steel + SAIL manufacture per IR specs.
Notes: parent: youngs_modulus_atomic; **industry-strand anchor: Tata Steel + SAIL + Indian Railways**

Description (what the sim teaches): η = (F/A) / (Δx/L) = shear stress / shear strain; characterises shear deformation. Typically 0.3-0.5 × Y for metals
Notes: EL-G5; **bridges to T15 torsional pendulum**

Description (what the sim teaches): Torsion of cylindrical wire by torque τ: angle of twist φ = τL / (η · I_polar); used in torsional pendulum + torsion balance (Cavendish experiment for G measurement)
Notes: parent: modulus_of_rigidity_atomic; cross-link to T15 + T16 (Cavendish G)

Description (what the sim teaches): K = −V(dP/dV) = −(volumetric stress) / (volumetric strain). Negative sign because volume DECREASES as pressure INCREASES. Compressibility = 1/K
Notes: EL-G6; **closes T27 forward-edge** (mean_free_path → bulk modulus microscopic)

Description (what the sim teaches): Solids: K ~10^11 Pa (steel ~160 GPa). Liquids: K ~10^9 Pa (water ~2.2 GPa). Gases: K varies with process; ideal gas isothermal K = P; adiabatic K = γP. **Cross-cluster bridge to T27 (ideal gas K = P)**
Notes: parent: bulk_modulus_atomic; **closes T27 mean-free-path → bulk-modulus forward**

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian Navy submarines (INS Arihant + INS Kalvari) operate at depths ~200-400 m with hydrostatic pressure ~20-40 bar; hull design uses bulk modulus to predict deformation
Notes: parent: bulk_modulus_atomic; **defence/navy-strand anchor: Indian Navy**

Description (what the sim teaches): σ = −(transverse strain) / (longitudinal strain). When body stretched longitudinally, transverse dimensions shrink slightly. Dimensionless; typical values 0.2-0.5
Notes: EL-G7; NCERT-light; **cognitive_error_target:** "Poisson's ratio = 0.5 always" → varies 0.2-0.5 by material (cork ~0; steel ~0.3; rubber ~0.5)

Description (what the sim teaches): Cork ≈ 0 (no transverse change). Steel ≈ 0.30. Brass ≈ 0.34. Aluminum ≈ 0.33. Rubber ≈ 0.50 (incompressible volume). Glass ≈ 0.21.
Notes: parent: poissons_ratio_atomic; cognitive-scaffold nano

Description (what the sim teaches): Energy stored in deformed body within elastic limit: U = ½ × stress × strain × volume = ½ × (F/A) × (ΔL/L) × A·L = ½·F·ΔL. Bridges to T17 SHM spring elastic-PE (½kx²) and T13 Work-Energy general
Notes: EL-G8; **bridges to T17 SHM + T13 Work-Energy**

Description (what the sim teaches): For spring (Hooke's-law system): U = ½kx². Identical form to ½·stress·strain·V because spring IS a Hooke's-law system with effective E·A/L = k
Notes: parent: elastic_pe_atomic; cross-link to T11+T17

Description (what the sim teaches): Beam loading, cantilever bending, bridge engineering all use elasticity: deflection ∝ load/Y; safety factor = ultimate stress / working stress. **Indian civil engineering context**
Notes: EL-G10; V1.2-deferred (NCERT-light + JEE-Advanced); **bridge to engineering**

Description (what the sim teaches): L&T Construction (Mumbai Sea Link, Bandra-Worli, Bogibeel Bridge): structural-steel Y critical for deflection prediction; safety factor 2-3× ultimate stress
Notes: parent: construction_engineering_applications_atomic; **industry-strand anchor: L&T + Indian bridge engineering**

Description (what the sim teaches): DRDO + Indian armed forces armor: composites with high Y per unit weight; ceramic-metal-polymer combinations; Arjun tank + Tejas LCA airframe applications
Notes: parent: construction_engineering_applications_atomic; **defence/aerospace-strand anchor: DRDO**

Description (what the sim teaches): Wave = propagating disturbance. **Transverse:** particle displacement ⊥ to propagation (string, light, water surface). **Longitudinal:** particle displacement ∥ to propagation (sound, pressure). All waves transport energy + momentum WITHOUT net mass transfer.
Notes: WE-G6; **Diamond candidate**

Description (what the sim teaches): Transverse: shake one end of long string → pulse travels along string while each particle moves up-down only. Classroom demo + lab.
Notes: parent: transverse_vs_longitudinal_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Slinky compression-rarefaction = longitudinal wave demo; Indian classroom physics-lab standard.
Notes: parent: transverse_vs_longitudinal_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Surface water waves: particles trace small circles → mixed transverse + longitudinal. Indian coastal observation (Marina Beach, Goa).
Notes: parent: transverse_vs_longitudinal_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Sinusoidal travelling wave: y(x, t) = A sin(kx − ωt + φ). Right-moving: argument (kx − ωt). Left-moving: (kx + ωt). Phase velocity v = ω/k.
Notes: WE-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "particles travel with wave" → particles oscillate in place

Description (what the sim teaches): Pulse = single disturbance (e.g., one shake of string). Continuous wave = repeated sinusoidal source. Both obey wave equation; pulse = sum-of-sinusoids via Fourier (bridge to T22).
Notes: parent: travelling_wave_function_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Wave parameters: amplitude A, wavelength λ, period T, frequency f = 1/T, angular frequency ω = 2πf, wave-number k = 2π/λ. Universal relation v = fλ = ω/k.
Notes: WE-G4

Description (what the sim teaches): Maximum displacement from equilibrium. Units: m (transverse) or m (longitudinal displacement) or Pa (sound pressure amplitude).
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Spatial period: distance between consecutive crests (or any 2π phase-matched points). λ = v/f.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Temporal period T = time for one full cycle; f = 1/T = cycles/sec. Hz = s⁻¹.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): k = 2π/λ (rad/m); ω = 2π/T (rad/s). Both phase-rate quantities; central to wave-equation.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Second-order PDE: ∂²y/∂t² = v²·∂²y/∂x². Travelling wave y(x±vt) is general solution (d'Alembert). **Linear PDE → superposition holds (foundation for T22).**
Notes: WE-G3; **bridges to T22 + T38**

Description (what the sim teaches): Substitute y = A sin(kx − ωt) into PDE → ω² = v²k² → v = ω/k. Cognitive scaffold for derivation.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Speed of transverse wave on stretched string: v = √(T/μ); T = tension (N), μ = linear mass density (kg/m). Derived from Newton's 2nd law on small arc. **Industry-anchored: sitar/tabla/guitar string tuning, Indian Railways rail-vibration diagnostics.**
Notes: WE-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **closes T11 + T21 forward-edges**

Description (what the sim teaches): Sitar, tabla, sarod, guitar tuning: increase tension T → higher v → higher f (for fixed L). Indian classical music + Western. **Healthcare bridge:** ultrasound transducers also use string-resonance principles.
Notes: parent; **consumer/cultural-strand anchor: Indian musical instruments + ITC Sangeet Research Academy**

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian Railways uses ultrasonic rail-flaw detection: travelling-wave reflections reveal internal cracks. Tata Steel + IR Research Designs & Standards Organisation (RDSO Lucknow) maintain testing standards.
Notes: parent; **transport/industry-strand anchor: Indian Railways RDSO + Tata Steel**

Description (what the sim teaches): v_particle = ∂y/∂t = −Aω cos(kx − ωt) — oscillates ±Aω. v_wave = ω/k — constant phase-velocity. **Two fundamentally different quantities; common JEE confusion.**
Notes: WE-G7; **cognitive_error_target:** "v_particle = v_wave" — both quantities derived separately + visualised

Description (what the sim teaches): For non-dispersive medium: v_phase = v_group. For dispersive (e.g., deep water, glass-prism light): v_group can differ → wave packet envelope moves at v_group. **V2 extension; flagged.**
Notes: parent; V2 deferred

Description (what the sim teaches): Power transmitted: P = ½μvω²A². Energy density ∝ A²f². Bridges T13 Work-Energy (energy transported through fluid/string) + T17 SHM elastic-PE.
Notes: WE-G8; **cross-cluster bridge to T13 + T17**

Description (what the sim teaches): Wave intensity I = P/A ∝ A²ω² → doubling amplitude quadruples intensity. Critical to T23 sound (dB scale) + T44 wave optics (Malus law variation).
Notes: parent; bridges to T23 + T44

Description (what the sim teaches): Wave at boundary: **fixed end** → π phase change (crest reflects as trough). **Free end** → 0 phase change. Boundary impedance determines partial reflection + transmission. **Sets up T22 standing waves.**
Notes: WE-G9; **cognitive_error_target:** "phase change always at reflection" → only at fixed/denser-medium; opens T22

Description (what the sim teaches): Wave on light string hitting junction with heavy string: partial reflection (phase-inverted) + partial transmission (in-phase). Classroom demo.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): T19 reflection mechanism extends to T42 TIR + T50 optical-fibre communication (BSNL + ISRO satellite-to-ground).
Notes: parent; **telecom-strand anchor: BSNL + ISRO**

Description (what the sim teaches): Pressure in fluid: P = F/A; depth-dependence P = P₀ + ρgh (hydrostatic). Acts equally in all directions at a point. SI: Pa = N/m². Atmospheric pressure ≈ 10⁵ Pa.
Notes: FM-G1; foundational

Description (what the sim teaches): Pressure increases linearly with depth: P(h) = P₀ + ρgh. Indian-Navy submarine context: 10 m water = 1 atm; INS Arihant at 300 m ≈ 31 atm.
Notes: parent: fluid_pressure_atomic; cross-link T18 deep_ocean nano

Description (what the sim teaches): Torricelli mercury barometer: 760 mm Hg = 1 atm = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa. Indian meteorology stations (IMD network) measure surface pressure for weather prediction.
Notes: parent: fluid_pressure_atomic

Targets misconception: pressure increases force proportionally to area
Description (what the sim teaches): Pressure applied at any point in enclosed incompressible fluid transmits undiminished to every other point. F_out/A_out = F_in/A_in → mechanical advantage = A_out/A_in.
Notes: FM-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "pressure increases force proportionally to area" → pressure identical, FORCE scales with area

Description (what the sim teaches): Tata Motors + Ashok Leyland workshop hydraulic-lift systems use Pascal's law. Maruti/Hyundai service stations India-wide: 100 kg force on 10 cm² piston → 10,000 kg lift on 1 m² piston.
Notes: parent: pascals_law_atomic; **industry/consumer-strand anchor: Indian automotive service**

Description (what the sim teaches): JCB India earthmovers + Tata trucks + Indian Railways braking systems use hydraulic-pressure transmission. JCB Pune manufactures excavators with Pascal-law hydraulics.
Notes: parent: pascals_law_atomic; **industry-strand anchor: JCB India + Tata Motors**

Description (what the sim teaches): Object immersed in fluid experiences upward buoyant force equal to weight of fluid displaced: F_b = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g. Floats if ρ_object < ρ_fluid (effective density via shape).
Notes: FM-G3; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "heavier objects sink" → only if DENSER than fluid; iron ship floats via hull-shape lowering effective density

Description (what the sim teaches): Steel hull of cargo ship: total density (steel + air interior) << water density. INS Vikrant aircraft carrier displacement 45,000 tonnes — floats per Archimedes. **Cognitive scaffold nano.**
Notes: parent: archimedes_buoyancy_atomic; **defence/navy-strand anchor: INS Vikrant**

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian Navy submarines (INS Arihant, Kalvari class) adjust buoyancy by flooding/blowing ballast tanks — controls effective ρ.
Notes: parent: archimedes_buoyancy_atomic; **defence/navy-strand anchor: Indian Navy**

Description (what the sim teaches): Hot air at 100°C has ρ ≈ 0.95 kg/m³ vs cold air at 25°C ρ ≈ 1.18 kg/m³ → buoyancy lift. Indian Hot Air Balloon Festival (Pushkar Rajasthan).
Notes: parent: archimedes_buoyancy_atomic; **consumer/tourism-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Mass conservation in incompressible flow: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂. Volume-flow-rate Q = Av is constant along tube.
Notes: FM-G4; prerequisite for Bernoulli

Description (what the sim teaches): Ganges narrows at Rishikesh + Haridwar gorges → flow speeds up; widens at delta → slows. Same flow rate.
Notes: parent: continuity_equation_atomic; **geography/agriculture-strand anchor: Indian rivers**

Description (what the sim teaches): P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant along streamline (incompressible + non-viscous + steady flow). Pressure DROPS where velocity rises.
Notes: FM-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "Bernoulli applies to any flow" → only incompressible + non-viscous + steady + along-streamline

Description (what the sim teaches): HAL Tejas + Boeing 787 Air India + IndiGo A320 wing: curved upper surface → faster airflow → lower pressure → net upward lift. **Approximation only** (Bernoulli partially explains; angle-of-attack also critical).
Notes: parent: bernoullis_principle_atomic; **aviation-strand anchor: HAL + Air India + IndiGo**

Description (what the sim teaches): Pipe narrowing → higher v → lower P → manometer reading gives flow rate. Used in Indian petroleum (IOCL refineries) + water-supply (Hindustan Tin Works) + medical IV drips.
Notes: parent: bernoullis_principle_atomic; **industry + healthcare-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Asian Paints + Berger Paints spray-gun atomisers; horizontal high-v airflow creates low pressure that sucks paint up nozzle. Also IPL stadium watering sprinklers.
Notes: parent: bernoullis_principle_atomic; **consumer/industry-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): F = ηA(dv/dy); shear stress = η × velocity-gradient. SI: Pa·s. η_water(20°C) ≈ 10⁻³ Pa·s; η_honey ≈ 10 Pa·s; η_air ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. Temperature-dependent (liquids: η ↓ as T ↑; gases: η ↑ as T ↑).
Notes: FM-G6; **cross-cluster bridge to T18 shear stress**; **cognitive_error_target:** "viscosity = thickness" → viscosity is rate-dependent shear resistance; T-dependent

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian Oil (IOCL) + HP + BP engine-oil SAE grades: 10W-30, 5W-40 — multi-grade oils with η-T behaviour spec'd for Indian climate (40-45°C summer).
Notes: parent: viscosity_atomic; **industry/consumer-strand anchor: IOCL**

Description (what the sim teaches): Drag on small sphere in viscous fluid: F_drag = 6πηrv (low-Re limit). Terminal v: v_t = (2/9)·(ρ_sphere − ρ_fluid)gr²/η when drag = net weight.
Notes: FM-G7

Description (what the sim teaches): Monsoon raindrop (r ≈ 2 mm) reaches v_t ≈ 6-10 m/s in seconds; without air resistance would fall at >100 m/s from 1 km cloud. **Indian monsoon context.**
Notes: parent: stokes_terminal_velocity_atomic; **agriculture/meteorology-strand anchor: IMD + monsoon**

Description (what the sim teaches): DRDO + Indian Army parachute design (ADRDE Agra) uses Stokes/quadratic-drag scaling for terminal v ≈ 5 m/s landing speed.
Notes: parent: stokes_terminal_velocity_atomic; **defence-strand anchor: DRDO ADRDE**

Description (what the sim teaches): Re = ρvD/η; dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces. Re < 2000: laminar; Re > 4000: turbulent; 2000-4000: transition. Predicts onset of turbulence.
Notes: FM-G8; **cognitive_error_target:** "Re predicts flow type uniquely" → only approximately; transition zone is mixed

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian municipal water supply (Bangalore BWSSB, Delhi DJB, Mumbai BMC): pipe design uses Re to prevent turbulent losses + pump-power-spec.
Notes: parent: reynolds_number_atomic; **public-service-strand anchor: Indian municipal utilities**

Description (what the sim teaches): T = F/L (force per unit length along surface); equivalently energy per unit area (J/m²). Origin: net inward cohesive force on surface molecules. T_water ≈ 73 × 10⁻³ N/m at 20°C.
Notes: FM-G9; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "surface tension floats things" → only when surface tension force >> weight (insect-scale, NOT ship-scale)

Description (what the sim teaches): Surface tension minimises surface area → sphere for fixed volume. Rain droplets, mercury beads, soap bubbles all approach spherical.
Notes: parent: surface_tension_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Water strider (पानी का कीड़ा — observable in Indian ponds) walks on water: weight = surface-tension support across legs. Demonstrates scale-dependence.
Notes: parent: surface_tension_atomic; **consumer/observation-strand anchor: Indian ponds**

Description (what the sim teaches): Inside soap bubble: ΔP = 4T/R (two surfaces). Inside water droplet: ΔP = 2T/R (one surface). Smaller bubble → higher inside pressure.
Notes: parent: surface_tension_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Capillary rise in narrow tube: h = 2T cosθ / (ρgr). Angle of contact θ determines rise (θ<90°: rise) or depression (θ>90°: e.g., mercury in glass).
Notes: FM-G10; **agriculture + biology bridges**

Description (what the sim teaches): ICAR (Indian Council of Agricultural Research) studies capillary rise in soil — controls irrigation efficiency. Drip irrigation (Jain Irrigation Jalgaon) leverages capillary distribution.
Notes: parent: capillary_action_atomic; **agriculture-strand anchor: ICAR + Jain Irrigation**

Description (what the sim teaches): Plant xylem tubes lift water from roots to leaves via capillary + transpiration. Critical for Indian farming + horticulture.
Notes: parent: capillary_action_atomic; **agriculture/biology-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Paper chromatography (Class 11-12 lab + DRDO forensics) uses capillary rise in paper fibres to separate dye components.
Notes: parent: capillary_action_atomic; **research/education-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): A wave transports energy/disturbance from one region of space to another **without bulk motion of matter**
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.1 (queue analogy + "Hello" example); NCERT §15.1 (pebble-in-pond, cork-pieces-on-water explicit); DCWT §17.1 (energy transfer definition). **v1?:** TRUE — opens the topic. **PRIMARY aha:** the medium doesn't travel; the disturbance does.

Description (what the sim teaches): Person in queue leans → pushes neighbor → jerk propagates without anyone walking forward
Notes: parent: A1. Sources: HCV1 §15.1 (the classic Indian-context queue figure). Strong real-world visual.

Description (what the sim teaches): Drop pebble in pond; cork pieces bob up-down but don't move outward
Notes: parent: A1. Sources: NCERT §15.1 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): Raindrop on calm water → expanding circle. Each water particle locally up-down only.
Notes: parent: A1. Sources: HCV1 §15.1, NCERT §15.1.

Description (what the sim teaches): Mechanical waves require a medium (sound, string, water); non-mechanical do not (EM, matter waves)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.1 explicit definition; NCERT §15.1 (trichotomy: mechanical, EM, matter waves); DCWT §17.2 explicit. **v1?:** TRUE. EPIC-C: misconception "all waves need a medium" → confronted (EM waves in vacuum).

Description (what the sim teaches): Bell-jar experiment: bell rings inaudibly when air pumped out. Sound is a mechanical wave.
Notes: parent: A2. Sources: DCWT §17.2 ("Over the moon, sound waves cannot travel"), NCERT §15.1.

Description (what the sim teaches): Light from stars reaches earth across vacuum at c = 3×10⁸ m/s
Notes: parent: A2. Sources: NCERT §15.1 explicit (Eq 15.1 cites c). Cross-topic-ref Topic-40 EM Waves for depth.

Description (what the sim teaches): Quantum-mechanical waves associated with electrons, used in electron microscopes
Notes: parent: A2. Sources: NCERT §15.1 brief paragraph. Cross-topic-ref Topic-48 Dual Nature. ⚠ partial sim — quantum waves don't visualize as classical disturbances.

Description (what the sim teaches): Wave where medium particles oscillate **perpendicular** to propagation direction (string, water surface, EM)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.14 (within §15.1-15.5 scope referenced from §15.2); NCERT §15.2 (Fig 15.2 pulse on string, Fig 15.3 harmonic wave); DCWT §17.4 (Fig 17.3 crest-trough). **v1?:** TRUE. Key visual: string up-down vs wave left-right.

Description (what the sim teaches): Hand snap up-down sends pulse along x; each string element moves in y only
Notes: parent: A3. Sources: HCV1 Fig 15.2, NCERT Fig 15.2.

Description (what the sim teaches): Wave where medium particles oscillate **along** propagation direction (sound in air, compression in slinky)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.14 (sound waves are longitudinal); NCERT §15.2 (Fig 15.4 piston in pipe, compressions and rarefactions); DCWT §17.4 (Fig 17.4 compressions/rarefactions). **v1?:** TRUE. Key visual: slinky compression travels.

Description (what the sim teaches): Longitudinal wave = alternating high-density and low-density regions traveling
Notes: parent: A4. Sources: NCERT §15.2, DCWT §17.4.

Description (what the sim teaches): Push-pull piston creates sinusoidal compressions in air → sound wave
Notes: parent: A4. Sources: NCERT Fig 15.4 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): Single jerk = wave pulse; continuous oscillation of source = wave train (or wave packet)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.2 explicit (pulse if source active briefly; wave train if continuous); NCERT §15.2 (Fig 15.2 pulse vs Fig 15.3 harmonic wave); DCWT §17.3 implicit. **v1?:** TRUE. Foundational distinction before introducing the wave equation.

Description (what the sim teaches): The general form of a wave traveling in +x direction at speed v: y(x,t) = f(t − x/v). The argument MUST be in the combination (t − x/v).
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.2 derivation Eq 15.1 (explicit); DCWT §17.3 (3 conditions for a wave function: PDE form, single-valued, continuous; general solution y = f(ax ± bt)); NCERT §15.3 (Eq 15.2 = sinusoidal special case). **v1?:** TRUE — foundational equation. **PRIMARY aha:** any function whose argument is the combination t−x/v is a wave.

Description (what the sim teaches): y = f(x−vt) or y = f(t−x/v) for +x propagation; y = f(t+x/v) for −x propagation
Notes: parent: A6. Sources: HCV1 §15.2 (Eq 15.1–15.4), DCWT §17.3 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): Wave equation in PDE form (∂²y/∂t² = v² ∂²y/∂x²). Any solution f(x±vt) satisfies it.
Notes: parent: A6. Sources: DCWT §17.3 (Condition 1 explicit), HCV1 §15.4 (used implicitly). `granularity_question: candidate_micro` — single-equation insight.

Description (what the sim teaches): y = (t − x/v)², or y = A exp[-(t−x/v)/T], or y = sin... — all are wave equations. Shape is arbitrary; ARGUMENT is constrained.
Notes: parent: A6. Sources: HCV1 §15.2 explicit example; DCWT §17.3. EPIC-C target: students think only sin/cos count as waves.

Description (what the sim teaches): Three equivalent expressions for wave speed: v = ω/k = λ/T = νλ
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.9 v = λ/T = νλ); NCERT §15.4 (Eq 15.12 explicit triple equality); DCWT §17.4 (v = ω/k, v = fλ). **v1?:** TRUE. The relationship students use constantly.

Description (what the sim teaches): Wavelength = distance the wave travels in one period
Notes: parent: A7. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 explicit derivation.

Description (what the sim teaches): The canonical sinusoidal traveling wave: y(x,t) = A sin(kx − ωt + φ). A=amplitude, k=wave number, ω=angular freq, φ=phase constant.
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.6 y = A sin ω(t − x/v), also forms 15.10-15.15); NCERT §15.3 (Eq 15.2 standard form); DCWT §17.4 (y = A sin(ωt − kx + φ) with all alternate forms). **v1?:** TRUE — central equation. Special case of A6.

Description (what the sim teaches): A = max value of y; particles oscillate between +A and −A
Notes: parent: A8. Sources: NCERT §15.3.1 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): The entire argument is "the phase"; determines y at any (x,t)
Notes: parent: A8. Sources: NCERT §15.3.1 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): y = A sin(kx−ωt) ≡ A sin 2π(x/λ − t/T) ≡ A sin k(x−vt). All same wave.
Notes: parent: A8. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.10-15.12 explicit), DCWT §17.4 ("Extra Points to Remember" callout).

Description (what the sim teaches): φ in y = A sin(kx − ωt + φ) is the **initial phase angle** — sets the wave's lateral shift / starting position at (x=0, t=0). Determined by initial conditions. φ = π/2 makes the wave start at max; φ = π reverses the sine; etc.
Notes: parent: A8. Sources: NCERT §15.3.1 explicit ("φ is the phase at x=0 and t=0. Hence φ is called the initial phase angle"); HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.13-15.15 walk through different φ choices); DCWT §17.4. **Collapsed from former A20 atomic** per founder-style decision W-G1 (2026-05-25) — geometrically a lateral shift of the same wave, not a separate physical phenomenon.

Description (what the sim teaches): Wavelength = minimum spatial distance between two points oscillating in phase
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.8 λ = vT derivation); NCERT §15.3.2 (Eq 15.6 λ = 2π/k); DCWT §17.4 (Fig 17.3 between two crests). **v1?:** TRUE.

Description (what the sim teaches): Easiest measurement: λ = distance between consecutive crests (or troughs)
Notes: parent: A9. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 explicit, NCERT §15.3.2, DCWT §17.4.

Description (what the sim teaches): Angular wave number k = 2π/λ; unit rad/m. "Number of waves in 2π length."
Notes: parent: A9. Sources: HCV1 §15.3, NCERT §15.3.2 (Eq 15.6), DCWT §17.4 Note. **UNCERTAIN — flagged for founder review:** is `wave_number` a separate atomic or a nano of `wavelength`? They are tightly coupled (one is 2π/the other). Collapsed here as nano per analogous Friction A16→N15.3 decision; founder spot-check welcome.

Description (what the sim teaches): ω = 2π/T = 2πν. T = period (seconds per cycle), ν = frequency (cycles per second, Hz)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (ν = 1/T, ω = 2π/T); NCERT §15.3.3 (Eq 15.7 ωT = 2π, Eq 15.8 ν = ω/2π); DCWT §17.4 (ω = 2πf = 2π/T). **v1?:** TRUE. Inherited from SHM but central to wave equation.

Description (what the sim teaches): T = time for any particle to complete one cycle
Notes: parent: A11. Sources: NCERT §15.3.3, Fig 15.7.

Description (what the sim teaches): ν = 1/T, unit Hz = oscillations per second
Notes: parent: A11. Sources: HCV1 §15.3, NCERT §15.3.3.

Description (what the sim teaches): Crest = point of max positive displacement (+A); trough = max negative (−A)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3, NCERT §15.3.1, DCWT §17.4 Fig 17.3. **v1?:** TRUE — basic terminology, but worth a standalone atomic because students confuse with amplitude.

Description (what the sim teaches): **Two different velocities exist at the same point.** Wave velocity v = ω/k (constant, depends on medium). Particle velocity v_P = ∂y/∂t = Aω cos(kx−ωt+φ) (varies sinusoidally with time, depends on position+time).
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 explicit ("This velocity is totally different from the wave velocity v"); DCWT §17.5 (very explicit: vP = −v × slope of y-x graph); NCERT — implicit (Eq 15.11 dx/dt = ω/k for fixed phase point). **v1?:** TRUE. **CRITICAL misconception target:** students conflate the two velocities. EPIC-C: STATE_1 shows student saying "wave is moving at 5 m/s, so each particle moves at 5 m/s" → wrong.

Description (what the sim teaches): v = ω/k is set by medium properties (string tension, mass density). Same value for all particles.
Notes: parent: A13. Sources: HCV1 §15.3, NCERT §15.4.

Description (what the sim teaches): v_P = Aω cos(...) — changes sign every half-cycle, depends on time and position
Notes: parent: A13. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 Eq 15.7, DCWT §17.5.

Description (what the sim teaches): At y = ±A, cos(phase) = 0 (since sin = ±1), so v_P = 0
Notes: parent: A13. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit, HCV1 §15.3.

Description (what the sim teaches): At y = 0, cos = ±1, so v_P = ±Aω = maximum particle speed
Notes: parent: A13. Sources: DCWT §17.5, HCV1 (worked example 2).

Description (what the sim teaches): Elegant relation: v_P = −v × (∂y/∂x). Particle velocity = −(wave velocity) × (slope of y-x graph at that point)
Notes: parent: A13. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit (Eq iv), JEE 2008 example. `granularity_question: candidate_micro`.

Description (what the sim teaches): Transverse waves can propagate only in media that can sustain shearing strain — solids only. Longitudinal waves propagate in solids, liquids, gases.
Notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.2 explicit; DCWT §17.2 "Extra Points to Remember" (earthquake P-waves longitudinal, S-waves transverse — S-waves don't pass through Earth's liquid outer core). **v1?:** TRUE. Strong EPIC-C: surprising fact that doesn't fit "all media support all waves" assumption.

Description (what the sim teaches): Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) arrive first everywhere; S-waves (transverse) don't reach the antipode → proof Earth's outer core is liquid
Notes: parent: A26. Sources: DCWT §17.2 "Extra Points to Remember" explicit. Spectacular Indian-context anchor: 2001 Gujarat earthquake, 2015 Nepal earthquake — seismograph readings reflect this.

Description (what the sim teaches): Restating A1 as a misconception-confronting atomic: when a sound wave goes from a speaker to your ear, NO air physically travels — only the disturbance pattern does
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.1 (explicit speaker example: "The air that is near the speaker at the time of uttering a word remains all the time near the speaker"); NCERT §15.1 ("The water mass does not flow outward with the circles, but rather a moving disturbance is created"); DCWT §17.1. **v1?:** TRUE — **CRITICAL EPIC-C atomic.** STATE_1: student says "I hear you, so air moved from your mouth to my ear" → wrong. Visualize: cork bobbing in place on water while ripple expands.

Description (what the sim teaches): Speed of transverse wave on a stretched string: v = √(T/μ). T = tension, μ = linear mass density (mass/length).
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.4 (full circular-arc derivation, Eq 15.16); NCERT §15.4.1 (dimensional-analysis derivation, Eq 15.14); DCWT §17.6 (full derivation including alternative v = √(T/ρA), Examples 17.10, 17.11). **v1?:** TRUE — central problem-pattern formula.

Description (what the sim teaches): [T] = MLT⁻², [μ] = ML⁻¹ → [T/μ] = L²T⁻². So v = C·√(T/μ); exact derivation shows C=1.
Notes: parent: A14. Sources: NCERT §15.4.1 explicit step-by-step.

Description (what the sim teaches): Small string element forms arc of radius R, moves with speed v in moving frame; centripetal force T·Δl/R balances tension components; algebra gives v = √(T/μ)
Notes: parent: A14. Sources: HCV1 §15.4 (with figure), DCWT §17.6 (with figure).

Description (what the sim teaches): μ = m/L. For a uniform string of total mass m and total length L. Units kg/m.
Notes: parent: A14. Sources: HCV1, NCERT, DCWT all explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): Pluck a tighter string → wave travels faster → higher pitch (sitar tuning)
Notes: parent: A14. Sources: HCV1 §15.4. Indian-context anchor: sitar/veena tuning.

Description (what the sim teaches): Thicker (heavier per length) string → slower wave → lower pitch
Notes: parent: A14. Sources: HCV1 §15.4, DCWT §17.6.

Description (what the sim teaches): The medium determines wave speed. Frequency is set by the source; wavelength adapts via λ = v/ν.
Notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.4 explicit ("does not depend on wavelength or frequency of the wave itself"); DCWT §17.6 explicit ("Waves of all frequencies travel in a given medium with same speed"); HCV1 implicit. **v1?:** TRUE. **CRITICAL EPIC-C atomic.** Misconception: "vibrating the string faster makes the wave go faster" → wrong (only changes frequency, wavelength shrinks; v unchanged).

Description (what the sim teaches): Tune fork at higher ν → string vibrates faster → λ becomes shorter, but v = ν·λ stays constant
Notes: parent: A15. Sources: DCWT §17.6 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): When wave crosses from light string to heavy string, v decreases, ν unchanged, λ decreases
Notes: parent: A15. Sources: HCV1 §15.8 (Topic-22 boundary — cross-topic). Touch-mention here.

Description (what the sim teaches): Speed of longitudinal wave in a fluid: v = √(B/ρ). B = bulk modulus, ρ = density.
Notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.4.2 (Eq 15.19 with derivation via dimensional analysis); DCWT §17.6 explicit; HCV1 in Ch.16 (out of §15.1-15.5 scope). **v1?:** FALSE — V2. Belongs jointly with Topic-23 Sound Waves; held here as a wave-speed atomic with cross-topic edge to Sound.

Description (what the sim teaches): Speed of longitudinal wave in a long solid rod: v = √(Y/ρ). Y = Young's modulus.
Notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.4.2 (Eq 15.20, Table 15.1 with speeds — steel 5941 m/s, aluminum 6420 m/s); DCWT §17.6 (Eq v = √(Y/ρ)). **v1?:** FALSE — V2.

Description (what the sim teaches): Newton's formula v = √(P/ρ) ≈ 280 m/s underestimates by ~15%. Laplace correction: process is adiabatic, not isothermal → B_adiabatic = γP → v = √(γP/ρ) = 331 m/s (matches measurement).
Notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.4.2 explicit (Eqs 15.22–15.24 with full Newton-Laplace narrative); DCWT §17.6 explicit. **v1?:** FALSE — V2. Historically important + JEE PYQ-frequent but conceptually advanced.

Description (what the sim teaches): When two or more waves traverse the same medium simultaneously, the displacement of any element is the algebraic sum of displacements due to each wave. The waves pass through unmodified after overlap.
Notes: **Sources:** NCERT §15.5 (FULL treatment, Eq 15.25–15.34, two-pulse figure, phase-difference analysis); HCV1 §15.6 (briefer); DCWT folds into Ch.18 (Topic-22 superposition chapter). **v1?:** TRUE — included per Stage-1 catalog placing NCERT §15.5 in Topic 21. **Caveat:** the deep treatment (standing waves, interference patterns, beats) is Topic 22. This atomic stays at the *principle level* (two pulses → algebraic sum, then waves continue independently).

Description (what the sim teaches): At each (x,t), y_total = y_1 + y_2 + ... + y_n
Notes: parent: A19. Sources: NCERT §15.5 (Eq 15.25), HCV1 §15.6, DCWT (forward ref).

Description (what the sim teaches): Superposition holds for small-amplitude (linear) waves. Non-linear waves (huge amplitude) don't simply add.
Notes: parent: A19. Sources: HCV1 §15.6 explicit ("we shall only be talking about linear waves which obey the superposition principle").

Description (what the sim teaches): Two opposite-shape pulses momentarily cancel at overlap (y_total = 0 across string), then continue unchanged
Notes: parent: A19. Sources: HCV1 §15.6, NCERT Fig 15.9 (both show this dramatically). Strong simulation visual.

Description (what the sim teaches): v_P(x,t) = ∂y/∂t = Aω cos(kx − ωt + φ)
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.3 (Eq 15.7 explicit with notation note); DCWT §17.5 (full derivation); NCERT — implicit. **v1?:** TRUE. Bridges wave equation to SHM behavior of particles.

Description (what the sim teaches): ∂/∂t means hold x fixed (look at one particle); d/dt would mean follow a moving point
Notes: parent: A21. Sources: HCV1 §15.3 explicit ("while differentiating with respect to t, we should treat x as constant"). Math-tools subtlety students get wrong.

Description (what the sim teaches): a_P = ∂²y/∂t² = −ω² · y(x,t). Same form as SHM — each particle executes SHM.
Notes: **Sources:** DCWT §17.5 explicit (Eq v: aP = −ω²·displacement); HCV1 worked examples (Ch.15 WOE 2 explicit); NCERT — implicit. **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (extension of A21).

Description (what the sim teaches): Two visualizations of the same wave: (i) y-vs-x at fixed time (snapshot — shows wavelength λ); (ii) y-vs-t at fixed position (single particle's SHM — shows period T). Same equation, different "slice".
Notes: **Sources:** DCWT §17.5 (DEDICATED full subsection with both graphs explicitly contrasted, JEE PYQ-frequent format); NCERT Figs 15.6 + 15.7 (separated but discussed); HCV1 Fig 15.4 (only spatial snapshot). **v1?:** TRUE — strong pedagogical asset, unique to DCWT format. **PRIMARY aha:** wave = a function of two variables, and you can graph either dimension.

Description (what the sim teaches): On the y-x snapshot, slope = ∂y/∂x at that point (not dy/dx since y is a 2-variable function)
Notes: parent: A23. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): On the y-t graph at fixed x, slope = ∂y/∂t = particle velocity
Notes: parent: A23. Sources: DCWT §17.5.

Description (what the sim teaches): Two particles at distance Δx on the same y-x graph have phase difference φ = (2π/λ)·Δx
Notes: parent: A23. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): Same particle at two times Δt has phase difference φ = (2π/T)·Δt
Notes: parent: A23. Sources: DCWT §17.5 explicit.

Description (what the sim teaches): Energy per unit volume in a sinusoidal wave: u = ½ρω²A². Comes from SHM energy ½kx² applied to each particle.
Notes: **Sources:** DCWT §17.7 explicit derivation; HCV1 §15.5 (related power formula). **v1?:** FALSE — V2 (energy is advanced topic; not in NCERT §15.1-15.5).

Description (what the sim teaches): Average power = ½ρω²A²·S·v (S = cross-sectional area). Alternative form on string: P_av = 2π²μvA²ν².
Notes: **Sources:** HCV1 §15.5 (full derivation, Eq 15.17); DCWT §17.7 (P = uSv). **v1?:** FALSE — V2.

Description (what the sim teaches): When two or more waves traverse the same medium, the resultant displacement at any point at any time = vector sum of individual displacements. y(x,t) = y₁(x,t) + y₂(x,t) + ... Follows from linearity of wave equation (T19).
Notes: SW-G2; **Diamond candidate**; foundation of all wave-interference physics

Description (what the sim teaches): Two pulses approach + cross: at the moment of overlap, displacements add (constructive if same sign; destructive if opposite). After crossing, each pulse continues unchanged — **waves pass through each other**.
Notes: parent; **classroom demo with slinky or rope**

Description (what the sim teaches): At light↔heavy string junction: reflection coefficient r = (v₂−v₁)/(v₂+v₁); transmission coefficient t = 2v₂/(v₂+v₁). Energy conservation: r²+t²·(v₁/v₂)·(μ₂/μ₁)·...
Notes: parent; SW-G8 quantitative form

Description (what the sim teaches): Two oppositely-travelling waves of equal amplitude/frequency superpose to give: y(x,t) = 2A sin(kx) cos(ωt). **Spatial envelope sin(kx) determines amplitude pattern; time-factor cos(ωt) makes whole pattern oscillate in place. No energy translation.**
Notes: SW-G3; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "standing wave doesn't transport energy" mechanism explanation

Description (what the sim teaches): Standing wave construction: y₁(x,t) = A sin(kx − ωt) [right-moving] + y₂(x,t) = A sin(kx + ωt) [left-moving from reflection] → 2A sin(kx) cos(ωt). Trig identity: sin(A−B) + sin(A+B) = 2 sin(A) cos(B).
Notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): At any node, both kinetic + potential energy of string-particle are zero. At antinode, particle KE oscillates max ↔ 0; PE oscillates 0 ↔ max — total energy of segment constant; localised. No power flow across nodes.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): **Nodes:** positions where sin(kx) = 0 → x = 0, λ/2, λ, ... (spacing λ/2). **Antinodes:** sin(kx) = ±1 → x = λ/4, 3λ/4, 5λ/4, ... (also λ/2 spacing). Antinodes between adjacent nodes.
Notes: SW-G5; **cognitive_error_target:** "nodes at boundaries always" → only FIXED boundary; FREE boundary has antinode

Description (what the sim teaches): **Fixed-fixed string** (sitar): node at both ends; allowed wavelengths λ_n = 2L/n. **Open-open pipe** (flute, hollow tube): antinode at both ends; same λ_n = 2L/n but pressure pattern flipped. **Open-closed pipe** (clarinet, single-end closed): node at closed end, antinode at open end; only odd harmonics λ_n = 4L/(2n−1).
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Standing-wave allowed frequencies on fixed-fixed string of length L: f_n = n·v/(2L), n = 1, 2, 3, ... **Harmonics:** f_1 = fundamental; f_2 = 2nd harmonic = 1st overtone; etc. For open-closed pipe: f_n = (2n−1)·v/(4L), only odd harmonics.
Notes: SW-G4; **cross-link to T17 SHM coupled-oscillators normal modes**

Description (what the sim teaches): f_n = n·f_1: n=1 is fundamental (1st harmonic); n=2 is 1st overtone = 2nd harmonic; etc. **Terminology trap:** "nth harmonic" = nth multiple of f_1; "nth overtone" = (n+1)th harmonic. Cognitive scaffold for student.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Sitar/veena/violin: many harmonics simultaneously (tone "timbre"); fundamental gives pitch; relative amplitudes of harmonics determine tonal character (sitar vs violin sound DIFFERENT at same pitch). **ITC Sangeet Research Academy + IIT-Bombay music-cognition** research.
Notes: parent; **consumer/culture-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Superposition of two waves of nearly-equal frequency f₁, f₂ at the same point: amplitude envelope oscillates at f_envelope = (f₁−f₂)/2, but intensity oscillates at f_beat = |f₁−f₂|. Audible as "wah-wah" pulsation.
Notes: SW-G6; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "beats happen in space" → in TIME at fixed location

Description (what the sim teaches): Tabla, sitar, violin tuning by ear: master tuner adjusts string until beats with reference frequency slow to zero — at zero beats, frequencies match. **Indian-classical-music universal practice; ITC Sangeet + Doordarshan music broadcasts.**
Notes: parent; **consumer/culture-strand anchor: ITC Sangeet Research Academy + Indian classical music**

Description (what the sim teaches): Piano tuners (rare in India but exists) use beats between adjacent strings to set "equal temperament" — slight frequency offsets give consonant intervals. Same physics as tabla tuning.
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Standing waves on a string driven by electrical tuning fork at one end; other end attached to weight via pulley. Standing-wave pattern forms when f_driver matches one of f_n = n·v/(2L). Classic Indian Class-11/12 physics-lab apparatus.
Notes: SW-G7; **education/research-strand anchor: CBSE Class-11/12 + IIT physics labs**

Description (what the sim teaches): At f_driver = f_n: forced oscillator (string) responds with very large amplitude (theoretically infinite without damping; practically limited by air drag + internal losses). Bridges to T17 SHM driven-damped-oscillator.
Notes: parent; cross-link T17

Description (what the sim teaches): Microwave cavity resonators (used in Indian Space Programme + DRDO radar): same standing-wave physics in 3D box. Cavity length tunes resonant frequency.
Notes: parent; **defence/space-strand anchor: ISRO + DRDO microwave cavities**

Description (what the sim teaches): Two-source superposition at point P: y_P = y₁ + y₂. Path difference Δ → phase difference φ = 2πΔ/λ. **Constructive interference:** Δ = nλ → amplitude = 2A (intensity 4× single-source). **Destructive:** Δ = (n+½)λ → amplitude = 0. **Foundation for T44 wave optics YDSE.**
Notes: bridge to T44; SW-G9 cross-link

Description (what the sim teaches): I(φ) = 4I₀ cos²(φ/2) for two coherent equal-amplitude sources. Pattern oscillates between 0 (destructive) and 4I₀ (constructive). **Already validated in T44 wave optics YDSE.**
Notes: parent; cross-link to T44

Description (what the sim teaches): Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave: particles of medium oscillate parallel to direction of energy propagation, creating alternating compressions (high pressure) + rarefactions (low pressure). Requires elastic medium (gas, liquid, solid) — cannot travel through vacuum.
Notes: SO-G2; **Diamond candidate**; foundation of all acoustic physics; **cognitive_error_target:** longitudinal-vs-transverse

Description (what the sim teaches): Compression = particles momentarily bunched → local pressure increase ΔP > 0. Rarefaction = particles momentarily spread → ΔP < 0. Pattern travels at v_sound; individual particles oscillate in-place ±s₀. **Mass NOT transported; only the disturbance.**
Notes: parent; **classroom slinky demo**

Description (what the sim teaches): Bell jar with vacuum pump: ringing bell becomes silent as air is evacuated — proves sound needs medium. Contrast with light (EM wave) which propagates through vacuum. **Indian CBSE Class-11 lab demo.**
Notes: parent; cognitive contrast with light

Description (what the sim teaches): Audible band: ~20 Hz–20 kHz (declines with age). **Ultrasound > 20 kHz** (AIIMS medical imaging, industrial NDT, dog whistles, bat echolocation). **Infrasound < 20 Hz** (elephant communication, IMD earthquake-precursor monitoring, volcano monitoring). Same physics; different perception.
Notes: parent; SO-G10; **healthcare + meteorology anchors**

Description (what the sim teaches): Sound wave can be described two equivalent ways: **Displacement wave** s(x,t) = s₀ sin(kx−ωt); **Pressure wave** p(x,t) = p₀ cos(kx−ωt) = BAks₀ cos(kx−ωt) (where B = bulk modulus). **Key:** pressure leads displacement by π/2 → pressure-antinode coincides with displacement-node.
Notes: SO-G3; **cognitive_error_target:** "pressure peaks where displacement peaks" → no, π/2 phase shift

Description (what the sim teaches): Stationary snapshot: where particles are maximally displaced (compression edge), they are momentarily at rest — local pressure neither max nor min. Where particles cross equilibrium (max velocity), they bunch densely → max pressure. **Visualisation requires double-track animation (displacement + pressure synchronised).**
Notes: parent; cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): p₀ = BAks₀ where B = bulk modulus, k = wavenumber, s₀ = displacement amplitude. For air at STP: B ≈ 1.42×10⁵ Pa; typical conversational speech p₀ ≈ 0.01 Pa with s₀ ≈ 10⁻⁸ m — extraordinarily small displacement, measurable pressure.
Notes: parent; quantitative scale-setting

Description (what the sim teaches): Speed of sound in gas: **Newton (1687) — isothermal assumption:** v = √(P/ρ) → predicts 280 m/s at STP (wrong; measured 332 m/s, off by ~16%). **Laplace (1816) — adiabatic correction:** v = √(γP/ρ) where γ = Cp/Cv ≈ 1.40 for air → predicts 332 m/s (matches experiment). Physical reason: sound oscillations are too rapid for heat exchange → adiabatic, not isothermal.
Notes: SO-G4; **Diamond candidate**; classic Newton-Laplace historical-derivation arc; cross-link T26 + T27 (thermodynamics back-edge)

Description (what the sim teaches): From v = √(γRT/M): v ∝ √T (at fixed γ, M). **Indian-classroom-grade rule:** v at temperature T (in K) = v₀·√(T/T₀). At T = 0°C: v = 332 m/s; at T = 30°C (Indian summer): v ≈ 350 m/s. Audible to musicians during humid Indian summers.
Notes: parent; **Indian-climate anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Humid air has slightly LOWER mean molar mass (water vapour M=18 < dry-air M=29) → v slightly HIGHER in humid air. ~0.4% increase per 50% RH change. Negligible in classroom, measurable in precision acoustic engineering (CSIR-NPL standards).
Notes: parent

Description (what the sim teaches): Liquids: v = √(B/ρ); water ≈ 1480 m/s. Solids: v = √(Y/ρ); steel ≈ 5960 m/s. **Solids fastest** because Young's modulus >> bulk modulus of gas. Anchor: DRDO submarine SONAR uses water sound-speed; Indian Railways rail-track sound transmission detectable miles away (rail-ear-press folklore).
Notes: parent; **defence + transport anchors**; cross-link T18

Description (what the sim teaches): Air columns inside pipes support standing waves of sound: pressure node ↔ displacement antinode (and vice versa) at boundaries. **Open-open pipe (flute, bansuri):** displacement antinodes at both ends → f_n = nv/(2L), all harmonics. **Open-closed pipe (clarinet, bottle):** displacement node at closed end + antinode at open end → f_n = (2n−1)v/(4L), only odd harmonics. **End-correction:** effective length = L + 0.6r at each open end.
Notes: SO-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **applies T22 to air-column geometry**; bridges to Indian classical bansuri/shehnai/conch-shell physics

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian bamboo bansuri (Hariprasad Chaurasia tradition) is open-open pipe — full harmonic series accessible (the flute "sweetness"). Shehnai is open-closed-conical-bore — only odd harmonics dominate giving distinctive "nasal" timbre. **ITC Sangeet Research Academy formal pedagogy.**
Notes: parent; **consumer/culture anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): At the open end, the air column extends slightly beyond the physical mouth (because pressure-equalisation extends into open air). End-correction ≈ 0.6r where r = pipe radius. **Indian Class-11/12 physics lab resonance-column experiment with water column directly measures this.**
Notes: parent; **education anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Standard CBSE/ISC + IIT physics-lab apparatus: vertical glass tube + adjustable water reservoir; tuning fork held over open top; resonance heard when air-column length = λ/4, 3λ/4, ... Measures v_sound from known fork frequency. Tests Laplace formula directly.
Notes: parent; **education/research anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): **Intensity** I = power/area = ½ρωv·s₀² = p₀²/(2ρv) [W/m²]. **Decibel scale (logarithmic):** β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) with reference I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² (threshold of human hearing). **Loudness perception is approximately logarithmic** (Weber-Fechner law). Conversational speech ≈ 60 dB; rock concert ≈ 110 dB; pain threshold ≈ 120 dB.
Notes: SO-G6; **cognitive_error_target:** "double intensity = double dB" → no, double intensity = +3 dB

Description (what the sim teaches): Each 10 dB = 10× intensity. 60 dB → 70 dB is 10× more intense, perceived as ~2× louder. Common Indian-context: autorickshaw horn at 1 m ≈ 100 dB; Mumbai traffic ambient ≈ 75 dB; library ≈ 30 dB. Cognitive scaffold: log scale matches human sensory perception.
Notes: parent; **consumer-culture anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Point source in 3D: I ∝ 1/r² → β decreases by 6 dB per doubling of distance. Why: same energy spreads over surface 4πr². Important for noise-pollution regulation + concert-hall design. **CPCB India noise standards reference this.**
Notes: parent; **policy anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) India** mandates: residential zones ≤ 55 dB daytime / 45 dB night; commercial 65/55; industrial 75/70; silence-zone (hospitals, schools) 50/40. Enforcement via state pollution-control boards.
Notes: parent; **policy anchor: CPCB + state-PCB regulation**

Targets misconception: source-vs-observer asymmetry due to medium frame
Description (what the sim teaches): Apparent frequency f' heard by observer differs from source frequency f when source-observer-medium relative motion exists. **General formula:** f' = f·(v ± v_obs)/(v ∓ v_src) where signs depend on directions and v = sound speed in medium. **Asymmetry:** medium is at rest in lab frame — source-moving vs observer-moving give different formulae (not Galilean-equivalent).
Notes: SO-G7; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** source-vs-observer asymmetry due to medium frame

Description (what the sim teaches): Classic anchor: ambulance approaches → f' > f (pitch rises); passes → f' < f (pitch drops). **Indian context:** police siren, ambulance horn, Indian Railways horn-doppler at level crossings — high-pitched-approaching → low-pitched-receding step-change at observer pass-by.
Notes: parent; **healthcare/transport consumer anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Why two formulae? Sound wave is carried by medium (air at rest). If source moves toward observer, wavelength in front compresses (λ' < λ); v_sound unchanged → f' = v/λ' > f. If observer moves toward stationary source, λ unchanged but observer sweeps through wavefronts faster → f' = (v+v_obs)/λ > f. **Different physical mechanisms.**
Notes: parent; cognitive scaffold; **EM-wave Doppler in T38 is symmetric (relativistic) — important contrast**

Description (what the sim teaches): When v_src > v_sound (supersonic): wavefronts pile up into a Mach cone of half-angle sin θ = v/v_src. Sonic boom = shockwave reaching observer. **Indian anchor: HAL Tejas LCA + IAF Sukhoi/Rafale supersonic test flights over Pokhran + Bay of Bengal corridor.**
Notes: parent; SO-G9; **defence anchor: HAL + IAF**

Description (what the sim teaches): Police speed-guns + Indian traffic-radar (Delhi/Mumbai/Bengaluru): emit microwave, measure reflected-wave Doppler shift → vehicle speed. ISRO weather-radar (Doppler weather imaging): wind-velocity from rain-droplet reflection shift. **Same Doppler principle — observer + reflector + source roles inverted.**
Notes: parent; **transport + meteorology anchors**

Description (what the sim teaches): Apply T22 beats to Doppler: two slightly-detuned tuning forks (e.g., 256 Hz vs 258 Hz) produce 2 Hz beats. If one fork moves, Doppler shifts its f, changing beat rate — classic Indian-physics-lab tuning-fork experiment. **Cross-reference T22 beats_atomic.**
Notes: parent; SO-G8; cross-link T22

Description (what the sim teaches): Temperature: measure of thermal energy; thermometric property. Three scales: Celsius (water-ice 0°C, boiling 100°C), Kelvin (absolute, 0 K = −273.15°C), Fahrenheit (legacy). T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15. ΔT(K) = ΔT(°C).
Notes: TP-G1; foundational; bridges T27 microscopic-T to macroscopic-T

Description (what the sim teaches): ΔT in Kelvin = ΔT in Celsius (since both scales have 1-unit = 1-K size). All thermal-physics equations use ΔT directly — Kelvin-vs-Celsius doesn't matter for differences. Critical for student.
Notes: parent: temperature_scales_atomic; cognitive scaffold

Description (what the sim teaches): AIIMS + apollo + Class-12 lab clinical thermometer: 35-42°C range, mercury or digital. Indian-context: fever monitoring during dengue/COVID waves.
Notes: parent: temperature_scales_atomic; **healthcare-strand anchor: AIIMS + Apollo**

Description (what the sim teaches): Solid bodies expand on heating: ΔL = αL₀ΔT (linear); ΔA = βA₀ΔT (area, β ≈ 2α); ΔV = γV₀ΔT (volume, γ ≈ 3α). Coefficient α depends on material. Steel α ≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /K; copper ≈ 17 × 10⁻⁶; aluminum ≈ 23 × 10⁻⁶.
Notes: TP-G2; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "α/β/γ independent" → β ≈ 2α, γ ≈ 3α from same mechanism

Description (what the sim teaches): β = 2α; γ = 3α (to leading order). Derivation: differentiate (L+ΔL)² = L² + 2LΔL + (ΔL)² and (L+ΔL)³ similarly. Cognitive scaffold.
Notes: parent: thermal_expansion_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian Railways 1 km steel rail expands ~1.2 cm per 10°C ΔT. Expansion gaps (4-6 mm) every 13 m rail section. Welded long-rail uses pre-stressing. **Tata Steel + SAIL spec'd to standard α.**
Notes: parent: thermal_expansion_atomic; **transport/industry-strand anchor: Indian Railways + Tata Steel**

Description (what the sim teaches): Water has MAXIMUM density at 4°C; expands on cooling 4→0°C. Causes ice to float + Indian lake-fish to survive winter beneath ice cap. Critical to monsoon climate.
Notes: parent: thermal_expansion_atomic; **agriculture/biology-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Liquid γ_water ≈ 207 × 10⁻⁶ /K. Gas: PV = nRT means γ_gas dominated by P-T behaviour (V proportional to T at constant P). **Cross-cluster link to T20 fluid density-T dependence.**
Notes: parent: thermal_expansion_atomic; **cross-cluster bridge to T20**

Description (what the sim teaches): Heat-lost = Heat-gained in mixing without phase-change: m₁C₁(T₁−T_f) = m₂C₂(T_f−T₂). Conservation of energy applied to thermal interactions.
Notes: TP-G3; **Diamond candidate**; mixing-water-temperatures sim

Description (what the sim teaches): Water equivalent W of calorimeter: heat absorbed by calorimeter = W·C_water·ΔT. Standard Indian physics-lab equipment + IIT-Bombay+IIT-Madras Class-11/12 lab kits.
Notes: parent: calorimetry_principle_atomic; **education/research-strand anchor: IIT lab kits**

Description (what the sim teaches): Q = mCΔT; energy to raise unit mass by unit temperature. Water C ≈ 4186 J/kg·K (exceptionally high); ice ≈ 2100; steam ≈ 2010; iron ≈ 460; copper ≈ 385. **Bridges T26 Cv/Cp gas-specific-heats to operational form.**
Notes: TP-G4; **closes T26 forward-edge** (Cv defined → operational Q = mCΔT form). **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed `calorimetry_principle_atomic` from Requires — Q=mCΔT (specific heat) is the fundamental definition; calorimetry (heat-lost = heat-gained) USES it, so calorimetry requires specific-heat, not the reverse.

Description (what the sim teaches): Water's high C anchors Indian monsoon thermal regulation: oceans heat slowly + cool slowly → moderate coastal climates. Cooking: 1 L water at 25°C → 100°C needs 314 kJ.
Notes: parent: specific_heat_solid_liquid_atomic; **meteorology/consumer-strand anchor: monsoon + cooking**

Description (what the sim teaches): Q = mL; energy absorbed/released at phase transition WITHOUT temperature change. L_f (fusion) ice→water = 334 kJ/kg. L_v (vaporisation) water→steam = 2260 kJ/kg.
Notes: TP-G5; **Diamond candidate**; **cognitive_error_target:** "ice at 0°C = water at 0°C energetically" → no, 334 kJ/kg difference

Description (what the sim teaches): Standard Indian pressure cooker (Hawkins, Prestige): raises boiling-point to ~120°C at 1.5-2 atm; uses L_v released on condensation to cook food faster + with less fuel.
Notes: parent: latent_heat_atomic; **consumer/industry-strand anchor: Hawkins + Prestige**

Description (what the sim teaches): Human sweating in Indian-summer (40-45°C): L_v ≈ 2400 kJ/kg at 35°C → evaporative cooling. 1 g sweat removes 2.4 kJ from skin. **Healthcare/physiology Indian context.**
Notes: parent: latent_heat_atomic; **healthcare-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Fourier's law: Q/t = kA(ΔT/L). k = thermal conductivity. Cu k ≈ 400 W/m·K; Fe ≈ 80; glass ≈ 1; air ≈ 0.025; wool ≈ 0.04 (insulator).
Notes: TP-G6

Description (what the sim teaches): Indian housing materials: brick (k ≈ 0.8) vs concrete (k ≈ 1.4) vs wood (k ≈ 0.13). BIS National Building Code thermal-insulation requirements.
Notes: parent: heat_conduction_atomic; **industry/policy-strand anchor: BIS NBC**

Description (what the sim teaches): Wool sweater + double-layer clothing in North-Indian winter: trapped air (k ≈ 0.025) is the actual insulator, wool just traps. Same principle in thermos flask.
Notes: parent: heat_conduction_atomic; **consumer-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Bulk-fluid motion driven by buoyancy + thermal gradient transfers heat. Hot fluid rises (lower ρ); cold fluid sinks; circulation moves heat. **Convection IS buoyancy-driven flow.**
Notes: TP-G7; **cross-cluster bridge to T20 — convection IS buoyancy + viscosity**

Description (what the sim teaches): Land heats faster than ocean (lower C) → hot air rises over land → cool air flows in from sea = day-time sea breeze. Reverse at night = land breeze. **Indian monsoon is large-scale extension of this.**
Notes: parent: heat_convection_atomic; **meteorology-strand anchor: IMD + monsoon**

Description (what the sim teaches): Bajaj + Usha room-heaters: hot-air convection circulates room air. North-Indian winter context.
Notes: parent: heat_convection_atomic; **consumer-strand anchor: Bajaj + Usha**

Description (what the sim teaches): Total power radiated by black/grey body: P = σεAT⁴. σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴. ε = emissivity (1 for blackbody, <1 for grey body). **Bridges to T38 EM Waves** (radiation IS EM).
Notes: TP-G8; **Diamond candidate**; **closes T26 entropy ← T-dependence forward; opens T38 EM-spectrum back-bridge**

Description (what the sim teaches): NTPC Korba + Tata Power Mundra + BHEL boilers operate at 600°C surface temps; radiative loss σ(T⁴ − T_amb⁴) is major heat-loss source; insulation + reflective coatings critical.
Notes: parent: stefan_boltzmann_atomic; **industry/energy-strand anchor: NTPC + Tata Power + BHEL**

Description (what the sim teaches): Sun ≈ blackbody at 5778 K → P/A ≈ σT⁴ ≈ 6 × 10⁷ W/m². Solar constant at Earth ≈ 1361 W/m². ISRO satellites + Indian solar-PV plants use this.
Notes: parent: stefan_boltzmann_atomic; **space/energy-strand anchor: ISRO + Adani Solar + Tata Solar**

Description (what the sim teaches): λ_max · T = b (b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K). Peak wavelength of blackbody spectrum shifts inversely with T. Sun (5778 K): λ_max ≈ 500 nm (yellow-green). Earth (300 K): λ_max ≈ 10 μm (infrared).
Notes: TP-G8; **Diamond candidate**; explains why "hot objects glow red then white" + IR-imaging

Description (what the sim teaches): DRDO + Indian armed forces thermal-imaging night-vision goggles: detect 300-310 K body heat → IR at λ ≈ 9.7 μm. AIIMS COVID screening thermometers use same principle.
Notes: parent: wien_displacement_atomic; **defence/healthcare-strand anchor: DRDO + AIIMS**

Description (what the sim teaches): IMD INSAT satellites carry IR radiometers measuring cloud-top temperatures via Wien's-law peak shift → weather prediction.
Notes: parent: wien_displacement_atomic; **meteorology/space-strand anchor: IMD + INSAT**

Description (what the sim teaches): dT/dt = −k(T − T_env); small-ΔT-from-environment limit of Stefan-Boltzmann. T(t) = T_env + (T₀ − T_env)·e^(−kt).
Notes: TP-G9; **cognitive scaffold** to differentiate from Stefan-Boltzmann; **5th cross-cluster use of `calculus_exponential_decay`** (after T34/T44/T48/T35)

Description (what the sim teaches): Hot tea (60°C → 25°C ambient) cools per Newton's law; characteristic time τ ≈ 1/k ≈ 10-15 min for standard Indian tea-cup.
Notes: parent: newtons_law_of_cooling_atomic; **consumer-strand anchor**

Description (what the sim teaches): Two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third are in equilibrium with each other; temperature is the operational state-variable defining "thermally equilibrated"
Notes: TD-G2; foundational; cognitive-prep for "temperature ≠ heat"

Description (what the sim teaches): T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15; T(°F) = (9/5)T(°C) + 32; absolute zero = 0 K = −273.15 °C; Kelvin = SI primary
Notes: parent: zeroth_law_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): CSIR-NPL Delhi maintains India's primary temperature standard (ITS-90 platinum-resistance thermometer); gas thermometer = primary definition basis
Notes: parent: zeroth_law_atomic; **research-strand anchor: CSIR-NPL**

Description (what the sim teaches): U = sum of kinetic + potential energies of all molecules; STATE function (path-independent); depends only on (T, V, n) for an ideal gas → depends only on T
Notes: TD-G4; state-function-vs-path-function is the central conceptual distinction

Description (what the sim teaches): State function: U, T, P, V, S (depend only on the state, ΔU = U_f − U_i). Path function: Q, W (depend on the path between states). **Cognitive-error-prevention nano**.
Notes: parent: internal_energy_atomic; TD-G4 cognitive-error-prevention

Description (what the sim teaches): ΔU = Q − W; energy conservation generalised to heat; Q heat ADDED to system, W work DONE BY system
Notes: TD-G3; **closes anticipated bridge from T13 Work-Energy**

Description (what the sim teaches): Physics convention (NCERT/HCV/DCP): Q in = +, W by system = +; ΔU = Q − W. Chemistry convention: Q in = +, W on system = +; ΔU = Q + W. Same physics, opposite sign for W. **Cognitive-error-prevention nano — explicit contrast required.**
Notes: parent: first_law_atomic; TD-G3 cognitive-error-prevention

Description (what the sim teaches): W = ∫P dV (area under P-V curve); positive when V increases (expansion), negative when V decreases (compression)
Notes: parent: first_law_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): T = constant; for ideal gas ΔU = 0 → Q = W; W = nRT ln(V_f/V_i); PV = constant (Boyle's law)
Notes: TD-G5; Diamond candidate — PV hyperbola sim

Description (what the sim teaches): Slow cooling of a car/scooter radiator at idle ≈ isothermal heat rejection at constant T_engine; Maruti Suzuki / Tata Motors / Bajaj engines designed around isothermal idle
Notes: parent: isothermal_process_atomic; consumer/transport anchor

Description (what the sim teaches): Q = 0 (no heat exchange); ΔU = −W; PV^γ = constant; T₁V₁^(γ−1) = T₂V₂^(γ−1); steeper PV-curve than isothermal
Notes: TD-G5; Diamond candidate — adiabatic-vs-isothermal overlay sim

Description (what the sim teaches): Side-by-side PV-overlay: both start at same (P,V), isothermal stays on PV=const, adiabatic on PV^γ=const (steeper, since γ>1). T drops in adiabatic expansion (Q=0 → all work comes from U → U drops → T drops). **Cognitive-error-prevention nano**.
Notes: parent: adiabatic_process_atomic; TD-G5 cognitive-error-prevention

Description (what the sim teaches): Diesel engine compression-stroke is approximately adiabatic; T rises ~5× to auto-ignition temperature ~500 °C — fuel ignites without spark. Indian Railways WDM/WDG diesel locomotives + Tata/Mahindra/Ashok-Leyland diesel trucks operate on this.
Notes: parent: adiabatic_process_atomic; transport-strand anchor

Description (what the sim teaches): P = constant; W = P·ΔV; ΔU = nCv·ΔT; Q = nCp·ΔT
Notes: TD-G1; one of four canonical processes

Description (what the sim teaches): Pressure-cooker pre-vent: isobaric heating at ~2 atm; raises water boiling point to ~120 °C → faster cooking. Hawkins/Prestige household appliance everywhere in India
Notes: parent: isobaric_process_atomic; everyday-Indian anchor

Description (what the sim teaches): V = constant; W = 0; ΔU = Q = nCv·ΔT; pressure scales linearly with T
Notes: TD-G1; one of four canonical processes

Description (what the sim teaches): Petrol engine combustion-stroke is approximately isochoric (volume constant during the brief ignition); used in Maruti/Honda/Bajaj petrol engines + Indian 2-wheeler market (~20M units/yr)
Notes: parent: isochoric_process_atomic; consumer-transport anchor

Description (what the sim teaches): C = Q/(nΔT); Cv (constant volume) and Cp (constant pressure); Cp − Cv = R (Mayer's relation, ideal gas); γ = Cp/Cv (adiabatic index)
Notes: TD-G1; foundational bridge to T27 equipartition

Description (what the sim teaches): Cp − Cv = R (per mole, ideal gas); derived from first law applied to isobaric vs isochoric heating
Notes: parent: specific_heat_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Monatomic γ = 5/3 (He, Ne, Ar); diatomic γ = 7/5 (N₂, O₂, H₂); polyatomic γ ≈ 4/3 (CO₂, NH₃, CH₄). Bridges to T27 equipartition theorem (f degrees of freedom → Cv = fR/2)
Notes: parent: specific_heat_atomic; cross-cluster bridge to T27

Description (what the sim teaches): 4-stroke reversible cycle: isothermal-expansion (T_hot) → adiabatic-expansion → isothermal-compression (T_cold) → adiabatic-compression. η_Carnot = 1 − T_cold/T_hot
Notes: TD-G6; Diamond candidate — 4-stroke PV+TS sim

Description (what the sim teaches): η_real ≤ η_Carnot for any heat engine operating between T_hot and T_cold; equality only for reversible engine. Foundation of second-law optimality.
Notes: parent: carnot_cycle_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): NTPC supercritical coal plants (Sipat, Vindhyachal, Mundra) operate T_hot ~600 °C steam, T_cold ~40 °C cooling water → η_Carnot ~64%, real η ~42-45%. India's ~75% electricity from such cycles.
Notes: parent: carnot_cycle_atomic; **power-strand anchor: NTPC**

Description (what the sim teaches): Cyclic device that converts Q_hot → W + Q_cold; efficiency η = W/Q_hot = 1 − Q_cold/Q_hot. Refrigerator + heat pump = reverse of heat engine (W input pumps heat cold→hot)
Notes: TD-G9; bundles engine + refrigerator + heat pump

Description (what the sim teaches): COP_refrigerator = Q_cold/W; COP_heat_pump = Q_hot/W; COP_HP = COP_R + 1. Voltas/Godrej/LG India refrigerators rated 1-5 BEE stars based on this
Notes: parent: heat_engine_atomic; **HVAC + policy strand anchor: BEE star ratings + Voltas/Godrej**

Description (what the sim teaches): BS-VI norms (since 2020) constrain heat-engine efficiency curves on Indian Diesel/petrol vehicles — η improvements + emission caps. Indian Railways shifting from WDM diesel to electric (75% route-km AC-electrified) reduces aggregate Carnot losses.
Notes: parent: heat_engine_atomic; **policy + transport anchor: BS-VI + Indian Railways electrification**

Description (what the sim teaches): No process exists whose ONLY result is the complete conversion of heat from a single reservoir into work; equivalently, no heat engine has η = 100%
Notes: TD-G7; one of two second-law statements

Description (what the sim teaches): No process exists whose ONLY result is the transfer of heat from a colder body to a hotter body; equivalently, no refrigerator works without external W input
Notes: TD-G7; other second-law statement

Description (what the sim teaches): Proof that Kelvin-Planck violation ⟺ Clausius violation — assume one, construct a composite engine that violates the other. Establishes the two statements are operationally identical.
Notes: TD-G7; cognitive-error-prevention atomic — students think KP and Clausius are independent claims

Description (what the sim teaches): Reversible: infinitely slow, no dissipation, system always in equilibrium; can be reversed by infinitesimal change. Irreversible: real processes (friction, heat conduction across finite ΔT, free expansion, mixing) — direction of time emerges
Notes: The chapter's directionality concept

Description (what the sim teaches): S = ∫dQ_rev/T (operational definition); ΔS_universe ≥ 0 for any real process; entropy is a STATE function (despite Q being path function — Q_rev/T is path-independent for reversible paths)
Notes: TD-G8; **Diamond candidate** — most-confused concept in chapter; **NCERT 2023 condensed; HCV/DCP retain**

Description (what the sim teaches): Σ ΔS_system + ΔS_surroundings ≥ 0 for any real process; equality only for reversible. Statistical interpretation: entropy = k ln(W) (Boltzmann; T27 territory but cross-link here).
Notes: parent: entropy_atomic; cross-cluster bridge to T27

Description (what the sim teaches): NCERT 2023 condensed entropy treatment (removed standalone §12.13); HCV2 Ch.26 + DCWT Ch.21 retain full treatment; state boards (Maharashtra HSC, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka PUC, UP, WB) retain — author at full depth for state-board + JEE-Adv
Notes: parent: entropy_atomic; NCERT-divergence note; same pattern as T50

Description (what the sim teaches): Molecules are point particles + identical mass + random motion + elastic collisions + negligible inter-molecular volume vs container volume + Newtonian mechanics applies
Notes: Foundational assumptions atomic; cognitive-prep for "ideal" vs "real" gas

Description (what the sim teaches): Real molecules have finite size + intermolecular forces (van der Waals); ideal-gas treatment is the high-T low-P limit
Notes: parent: kt_assumptions_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): PV = nRT (macroscopic); equivalent forms PV = NkT (per-molecule) and P = ρRT/M (density form). Bundles Boyle, Charles, Avogadro.
Notes: KT-G2; **bridge atomic to all four T26 process atomics**

Description (what the sim teaches): Boyle: PV=const at fixed T; Charles: V/T=const at fixed P; Avogadro: V ∝ n at fixed P,T. All three reconciled in PV=nRT.
Notes: parent: ideal_gas_equation_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): R = 8.314 J/(mol·K); k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K (Boltzmann constant); SI-unit reconciliation
Notes: parent: ideal_gas_equation_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): PV = (1/3)Nm⟨v²⟩; pressure as time-averaged momentum-flux from molecular collisions on container walls; central derivation of chapter
Notes: KT-G3; **central derivation atomic; Diamond candidate** — molecules bouncing in box → pressure on wall sim

Description (what the sim teaches): Single molecule with v_x hits wall: Δp = 2mv_x; collision rate per molecule = v_x/(2L); pressure contribution per molecule = mv_x²/V
Notes: parent: kinetic_theory_pressure_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): ⟨v_x²⟩ = ⟨v_y²⟩ = ⟨v_z²⟩ = ⟨v²⟩/3 by isotropy → factor 1/3 in PV = (1/3)Nm⟨v²⟩
Notes: parent: kinetic_theory_pressure_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): (1/2)m⟨v²⟩ = (3/2)kT — temperature is proportional to per-molecule average translational kinetic energy
Notes: The microscopic-to-macroscopic bridge atomic; **closes T26 internal_energy ← T27 microscopic origin**

Description (what the sim teaches): T relates only to translational KE per (3/2)kT per molecule; rotational + vibrational KE contribute to Cv but not directly to T. **Cognitive-error-prevention nano** — subtle but central misconception.
Notes: parent: kinetic_temperature_atomic; KT-G10 cognitive-error-prevention

Description (what the sim teaches): v_rms = √(3kT/m), v_avg = √(8kT/πm), v_mp = √(2kT/m); inequality v_mp < v_avg < v_rms
Notes: KT-G4; cognitive-error-prevention bundling

Description (what the sim teaches): Side-by-side table: name, formula, numerical-ratio at fixed T (v_mp : v_avg : v_rms ≈ 1.000 : 1.128 : 1.225)
Notes: parent: molecular_speed_distribution_atomic; KT-G4 cognitive-error-prevention nano

Description (what the sim teaches): N₂ at 300 K: v_rms ≈ 517 m/s; O₂ at 300 K: v_rms ≈ 484 m/s; H₂ at 300 K: v_rms ≈ 1928 m/s (~Earth-escape relevant)
Notes: parent: molecular_speed_distribution_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): f(v) = 4π(m/2πkT)^(3/2) v² exp(−mv²/2kT); probability-density of molecular speeds; peaks at v_mp; broadens with T
Notes: KT-G7; **Diamond candidate** — probability-density curve at varying T = one of physics' most compelling sims

Description (what the sim teaches): At higher T: peak shifts right (higher v_mp), peak height decreases, total area stays = 1; the high-speed tail lengthens
Notes: parent: maxwell_boltzmann_distribution_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): Reaction rates depend on fraction of molecules with KE > activation energy → exponential tail dominates; basis for Arrhenius equation (chemistry)
Notes: parent: maxwell_boltzmann_distribution_atomic; chemistry-bridge nano

Description (what the sim teaches): Each quadratic degree of freedom (KE or PE) contributes (1/2)kT to average per-molecule energy; total U = (f/2)NkT for f dof per molecule
Notes: KT-G5; **closes T26 Cp/Cv ratio derivation**

Description (what the sim teaches): Side-by-side table: Monatomic (3 trans, f=3, γ=5/3, Cv=3R/2). Diatomic rigid (3 trans + 2 rot, f=5, γ=7/5, Cv=5R/2). Diatomic with vib at high T (f=7, γ=9/7, Cv=7R/2). Polyatomic linear (f=5-7), nonlinear (f=6+). **Cognitive-error-prevention nano** — students conflate the three.
Notes: parent: equipartition_theorem_atomic; KT-G10 cognitive-error-prevention

Description (what the sim teaches): Diatomic at room T: f=5 (3 trans + 2 rot). Above ~1000 K: vibrational modes activated → f=7. Explains why Cv is not strictly constant.
Notes: parent: equipartition_theorem_atomic

Description (what the sim teaches): λ = 1/(√2·n·π·d²); average distance a molecule travels between collisions; bridges molecular-scale to bulk-transport phenomena (viscosity, diffusion, conduction)

Description (what the sim teaches): Air at STP: λ ≈ 70 nm; n ≈ 2.7 × 10²⁵ /m³; d ≈ 0.37 nm. Validates point-particle assumption (λ ≫ d)
