Description (what the sim teaches): MFP increases at high altitude (low n) → diffusion becomes dominant transport mechanism; IMD/NCMRWF weather models use kinetic-theory-derived diffusion coefficients for pollutant transport (CPCB Mumbai/Delhi PM2.5 dispersion)

Description (what the sim teaches): F = k\|q₁q₂\|/r² where k = 1/4πε₀ ≈ 9×10⁹ N·m²/C². Force along line joining charges. NCERT 1.6 eq 1.1–1.2, DCEM 24.5, HCV2 29.2 eq 29.1. **Per E-G4: separate scalar atomic from vector form.**

Description (what the sim teaches): When a dielectric medium fills the space, F_medium = F_vacuum / K. K is dielectric constant of medium (K=1 vacuum, K≈80 water). DCEM 24.5 #4.

Description (what the sim teaches): k = 8.988×10⁹ N·m²/C² ≈ 9×10⁹ for problems. Derived from c² × 10⁻⁷. NCERT 1.6, DCEM 24.5.

Notes: F₂₁ = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r²₂₁)r̂₂₁ . Sign of charges + direction of r̂ together encode attract vs repel. Per E-G4: separate atomic. NCERT 1.6 eq 1.3 + Fig 1.6, HCV2 §29.2 eq after 29.1.

Description (what the sim teaches): F on q₁ due to q₂ at positions r₁, r₂: F₁ = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/\|r₁−r₂\|³)(r₁−r₂). 3D vector form for non-collinear charges. DCEM §24.5 Extra Points + Fig 24.5.

Description (what the sim teaches): F_e/F_g for two electrons = 4.17×10⁴² (HCV2 §29.1). For e-p = 2.4×10³⁹ (NCERT Ex 1.4). The take-away: electric force is ~10⁴⁰× stronger than gravity. **HCV2 owns this conceptual atomic.**
Notes: F_e/F_g for two electrons = 4.17×10⁴² (HCV2 §29.1). For e-p = 2.4×10³⁹ (NCERT Ex 1.4). The take-away: electric force is ~10⁴⁰× stronger than gravity. **HCV2 owns this conceptual atomic.**

Notes: Two charges on a line; find position of third where net force = 0. Solution exists between like charges (closer to smaller) or outside unlike charges (beyond smaller). HCV2 W.Ex 2, DCEM-style typed problem.

Description (what the sim teaches): E = lim(q→0) F/q. Vector. SI unit N/C (= V/m). Distinguishes "source" charge Q from "test" charge q. NCERT 1.8 + Eq 1.6–1.9, DCEM 24.6, HCV2 29.3.

Description (what the sim teaches): E depends only on source Q and position r, not on test q. NCERT §1.8 point (ii).
Notes: E depends only on source Q and position r, not on test q. NCERT §1.8 point (ii).

Description (what the sim teaches): E radially outward from +Q, radially inward to −Q. NCERT §1.8 point (iii).
Notes: E radially outward from +Q, radially inward to −Q. NCERT §1.8 point (iii).

Description (what the sim teaches): E = (1/4πε₀)(Q/r²) r̂. Spherical symmetry. NCERT §1.8 eq 1.6, DCEM 24.6, HCV2 29.3. **Per E-G5: own atomic.**
Notes: E = (1/4πε₀)(Q/r²) r̂. Spherical symmetry. NCERT §1.8 eq 1.6, DCEM 24.6, HCV2 29.3. **Per E-G5: own atomic.**

Description (what the sim teaches): E on axis at distance x from ring of radius R, total charge q: E_x = (1/4πε₀)(qx/(x²+R²)^(3/2)). Max at x = R/√2. Zero at center (x=0). Far-field reduces to point charge. **Per E-G5: own atomic.** DCEM §24.6 "Electric Field of a Ring of Charge" Figs 24.12–24.13, HCV2 W.Ex 2.
Notes: E on axis at distance x from ring of radius R, total charge q: E_x = (1/4πε₀)(qx/(x²+R²)^(3/2)). Max at x = R/√2. Zero at center (x=0). Far-field reduces to point charge. **Per E-G5: own atomic.** DCEM §24.6 "Electric Field of a Ring of Charge" Figs 24.12–24.13, HCV2 W.Ex 2.

Description (what the sim teaches): At x = 0 by symmetry forces from opposite ring segments cancel. DCEM §24.6 point (i).
Notes: At x = 0 by symmetry forces from opposite ring segments cancel. DCEM §24.6 point (i).

Description (what the sim teaches): E = λ/(2πε₀r) at perpendicular distance r from infinite line with linear charge density λ. E ∝ 1/r (not 1/r²). DCEM §24.6 "Electric Field of a Line Charge" derivation, HCV2 W.Ex 12. **Per E-G5: own atomic.**
Notes: E = λ/(2πε₀r) at perpendicular distance r from infinite line with linear charge density λ. E ∝ 1/r (not 1/r²). DCEM §24.6 "Electric Field of a Line Charge" derivation, HCV2 W.Ex 12. **Per E-G5: own atomic.**

Description (what the sim teaches): For rod length 2a at perpendicular distance x: E = q/(4πε₀ x√(x²+a²)). Reduces to ∞-line for x ≪ a. DCEM §24.6, HCV2 W.Ex 12.
Notes: For rod length 2a at perpendicular distance x: E = q/(4πε₀ x√(x²+a²)). Reduces to ∞-line for x ≪ a. DCEM §24.6, HCV2 W.Ex 12.

Description (what the sim teaches): E = σ/(2ε₀) perpendicular to infinite uniformly charged sheet. Independent of distance. NCERT §1.15 (in Stage-3 follow-up read). **Per E-G5: own atomic.** Listed here as forward-reference; full derivation belongs to Gauss-law application atomic A29.n2.
Notes: E = σ/(2ε₀) perpendicular to infinite uniformly charged sheet. Independent of distance. NCERT §1.15 (in Stage-3 follow-up read). **Per E-G5: own atomic.** Listed here as forward-reference; full derivation belongs to Gauss-law application atomic A29.n2.

Description (what the sim teaches): Lines tangent to E, density proportional to magnitude. Originate from +, terminate at −, never cross. NCERT §1.11, DCEM (later section, Stage 3), HCV2 §29.4.
Notes: Lines tangent to E, density proportional to magnitude. Originate from +, terminate at −, never cross. NCERT §1.11, DCEM (later section, Stage 3), HCV2 §29.4.

Notes: At any point E has unique direction → field lines can't cross. HCV2 §29.4.

Description (what the sim teaches): U(r) = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r) with U(∞) = 0. Work done by external agent against Coulomb force. HCV2 §29.5 eq 29.5–29.6. **Cross-topic ref T13 ← T30.**
Notes: U(r) = (1/4πε₀)(q₁q₂/r) with U(∞) = 0. Work done by external agent against Coulomb force. HCV2 §29.5 eq 29.5–29.6. **Cross-topic ref T13 ← T30.**

Description (what the sim teaches): U_total = Σ pairs (1/4πε₀)(qᵢqⱼ/rᵢⱼ). HCV2 Ex 29.3 (three 10μC charges at triangle, U = 27 J).
Notes: U_total = Σ pairs (1/4πε₀)(qᵢqⱼ/rᵢⱼ). HCV2 Ex 29.3 (three 10μC charges at triangle, U = 27 J).

Description (what the sim teaches): V_A = U_A/q (PE per unit test charge). Scalar (unlike E which is vector). Unit volt = joule/coulomb. HCV2 §29.6 eq 29.7–29.8, NCERT (later in Stage 3).
Notes: V_A = U_A/q (PE per unit test charge). Scalar (unlike E which is vector). Unit volt = joule/coulomb. HCV2 §29.6 eq 29.7–29.8, NCERT (later in Stage 3).

Notes: V_B − V_A = (U_B − U_A)/q depends only on endpoints. Conservative-field consequence. HCV2 §29.6.

Description (what the sim teaches): V(r) = (1/4πε₀)(Q/r) with V(∞) = 0. Scalar — adds algebraically for multiple charges (no vector decomposition needed). HCV2 §29.7 eq 29.9.
Notes: V(r) = (1/4πε₀)(Q/r) with V(∞) = 0. Scalar — adds algebraically for multiple charges (no vector decomposition needed). HCV2 §29.7 eq 29.9.

Description (what the sim teaches): V_total = Σᵢ Vᵢ. Scalar sum, unlike E which is vector sum. HCV2 §29.7 eq + Ex 29.4 (two charges 10μC + 20μC give V = 27 MV).
Notes: V_total = Σᵢ Vᵢ. Scalar sum, unlike E which is vector sum. HCV2 §29.7 eq + Ex 29.4 (two charges 10μC + 20μC give V = 27 MV).

Description (what the sim teaches): E = −dV/dr along direction of maximum decrease. Components: E_x = −∂V/∂x, etc. The differential link between field and potential. HCV2 §29.8 eq 29.10–29.13. Per E-G7: own atomic.
Notes: E = −dV/dr along direction of maximum decrease. Components: E_x = −∂V/∂x, etc. The differential link between field and potential. HCV2 §29.8 eq 29.10–29.13. Per E-G7: own atomic.

Description (what the sim teaches): E points in direction of fastest V drop. dV/dr is rate of change with position. HCV2 §29.8 "the electric field is along the direction in which the potential decreases at the maximum rate."
Notes: E points in direction of fastest V drop. dV/dr is rate of change with position. HCV2 §29.8 "the electric field is along the direction in which the potential decreases at the maximum rate."

Description (what the sim teaches): Surfaces where V = constant. E always perpendicular to them. No work done moving charge along an equipotential. Per E-G7: own atomic. For point charge → concentric spheres. HCV2 §29.8 Fig 29.11.
Notes: Surfaces where V = constant. E always perpendicular to them. No work done moving charge along an equipotential. Per E-G7: own atomic. For point charge → concentric spheres. HCV2 §29.8 Fig 29.11.

Notes: V = kQ/r → V = const ⇔ r = const. HCV2 §29.8 Fig 29.11.

Description (what the sim teaches): If they did, the point of intersection would have two different V values. HCV2 §29 Short Answer Q8.

Description (what the sim teaches): On dipole axis (end-on, θ=0): E = (1/4πε₀)(2p/r³) parallel to p. Falls as 1/r³ (faster than point charge). HCV2 §29.9 "Special Cases (a)" + eq 29.17.
Notes: On dipole axis (end-on, θ=0): E = (1/4πε₀)(2p/r³) parallel to p. Falls as 1/r³ (faster than point charge). HCV2 §29.9 "Special Cases (a)" + eq 29.17.

Description (what the sim teaches): On perpendicular bisector (broadside, θ=π/2): E = (1/4πε₀)(p/r³) antiparallel to p. Half of axial magnitude. HCV2 §29.9 "Special Cases (b)" + eq 29.17.

Description (what the sim teaches): V at angle θ from axis: V = (1/4πε₀)(p cos θ/r²). Zero on equator (θ=π/2). Falls as 1/r² (faster than point charge). HCV2 §29.9 eq 29.16.

Description (what the sim teaches): τ = p × E. Magnitude τ = pE sin θ. Restoring direction. HCV2 §29.10 eq 29.19, NCERT §1.10 (Stage 3).

Description (what the sim teaches): U(θ) = −p·E = −pE cos θ. Minimum (most stable) at θ=0 (aligned with E); maximum (unstable) at θ=π (antiparallel). HCV2 §29.11 eq 29.20.

Description (what the sim teaches): Hot road heats air near surface; air layers near ground have lower μ (less dense, hotter); successive refraction bends light upward; at high enough incidence angle → TIR → observer sees inverted "puddle of sky" near road. Per RF-G3: own atomic. Indian-context: every May/June highway between Delhi-Jaipur, Mumbai-Pune
Notes: Hot road heats air near surface; air layers near ground have lower μ (less dense, hotter); successive refraction bends light upward; at high enough incidence angle → TIR → observer sees inverted "puddle of sky" near road. Per RF-G3: own atomic. Indian-context: every May/June highway between Delhi-Jaipur, Mumbai-Pune

Description (what the sim teaches): Inverse case: cold air below, warm above (in seas near poles). Ships appear "floating in sky." Not Indian but worth a callout.
Notes: Inverse case: cold air below, warm above (in seas near poles). Ships appear "floating in sky." Not Indian but worth a callout.

Description (what the sim teaches): 45°-45°-90° prism with light entering perpendicular to short side: TIR off the hypotenuse, exits perpendicular to other short side. Used to bend light by 90° or 180°, OR to invert an image without flipping (NCERT Fig.9.15 a,b,c). Per RF-G3: own atomic. Indian-context: Indian Army periscope, classroom binoculars, naval submarine periscope
Notes: 45°-45°-90° prism with light entering perpendicular to short side: TIR off the hypotenuse, exits perpendicular to other short side. Used to bend light by 90° or 180°, OR to invert an image without flipping (NCERT Fig.9.15 a,b,c). Per RF-G3: own atomic. Indian-context: Indian Army periscope, classroom binoculars, naval submarine periscope

Description (what the sim teaches): TIR has 100% reflection (no silver-tarnish loss). Mirrors lose ~5-10% per bounce; binoculars with 4-6 reflections must use prisms for brightness
Notes: TIR has 100% reflection (no silver-tarnish loss). Mirrors lose ~5-10% per bounce; binoculars with 4-6 reflections must use prisms for brightness

Description (what the sim teaches): μ₂/v - μ₁/u = (μ₂ - μ₁)/R. Foundation for lens-maker's formula. Derived via paraxial + Snell at the curved interface. Sign convention: incident-light direction positive. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "this formula is only for lenses" — it works for any single curved interface (e.g., a glass-rod end, an air-bubble in glass)
Notes: μ₂/v - μ₁/u = (μ₂ - μ₁)/R. Foundation for lens-maker's formula. Derived via paraxial + Snell at the curved interface. Sign convention: incident-light direction positive. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "this formula is only for lenses" — it works for any single curved interface (e.g., a glass-rod end, an air-bubble in glass)

Description (what the sim teaches): Glass μ=1.5, air bubble μ=1.0 (lower). Light passing through behaves like a concave (diverging) lens. DCP Example 31.16 + Fig.31.49
Notes: Glass μ=1.5, air bubble μ=1.0 (lower). Light passing through behaves like a concave (diverging) lens. DCP Example 31.16 + Fig.31.49

Description (what the sim teaches): Per RF-G2: own atomic. 1/f = (μ_lens/μ_medium - 1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂). In air (μ_medium=1): 1/f = (μ-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂). DERIVATION question — "what f does this lens have, given material + curvature?"
Notes: Per RF-G2: own atomic. 1/f = (μ_lens/μ_medium - 1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂). In air (μ_medium=1): 1/f = (μ-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂). DERIVATION question — "what f does this lens have, given material + curvature?"

Description (what the sim teaches): R₁: positive if first surface convex toward incident light. R₂: positive if second surface convex toward outgoing light. The biconvex lens: R₁>0, R₂<0
Notes: R₁: positive if first surface convex toward incident light. R₂: positive if second surface convex toward outgoing light. The biconvex lens: R₁>0, R₂<0

Description (what the sim teaches): For biconvex (R₁=R, R₂=-R) lens with μ=1.5: f = R. Useful "memorize this" result for problems
Notes: For biconvex (R₁=R, R₂=-R) lens with μ=1.5: f = R. Useful "memorize this" result for problems

Description (what the sim teaches): For glass (μ=1.5) lens in water (μ=4/3): f_water = 4 × f_air. (Because μ_g/μ_w - 1 = 1.5/1.33 - 1 = 0.125, vs. μ_g - 1 = 0.5 in air — ratio 4×). DCP Example 31.16 + NCERT Example 9.8(iii)
Notes: For glass (μ=1.5) lens in water (μ=4/3): f_water = 4 × f_air. (Because μ_g/μ_w - 1 = 1.5/1.33 - 1 = 0.125, vs. μ_g - 1 = 0.5 in air — ratio 4×). DCP Example 31.16 + NCERT Example 9.8(iii)

Description (what the sim teaches): Per RF-G2: own atomic. 1/v - 1/u = 1/f. **Note the sign — different from mirror formula (1/v + 1/u = 1/f).** USAGE question — "where is the image, given u + f?" EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "lens formula is 1/v + 1/u = 1/f like mirrors" — common Class 12 error from over-generalization
Required-by (unlocks): lens_magnification, power_of_lens, lens_combination_in_contact, two_lenses_separated_by_distance, lens_image_formation_cases, silvered_lens
Notes: Per RF-G2: own atomic. 1/v - 1/u = 1/f. **Note the sign — different from mirror formula (1/v + 1/u = 1/f).** USAGE question — "where is the image, given u + f?" EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "lens formula is 1/v + 1/u = 1/f like mirrors" — common Class 12 error from over-generalization

Description (what the sim teaches): The sign difference traces to incident-light direction: in mirror reflection, light reverses; in lens transmission, light continues. Sign-convention applies the difference cleanly
Notes: The sign difference traces to incident-light direction: in mirror reflection, light reverses; in lens transmission, light continues. Sign-convention applies the difference cleanly

Description (what the sim teaches): m = v/u (lens) vs m = -v/u (mirror) — see N11.1. For real images: m negative ⇒ inverted; for virtual: m positive ⇒ erect. Same magnitude-interpretation as mirror but opposite sign convention
Notes: m = v/u (lens) vs m = -v/u (mirror) — see N11.1. For real images: m negative ⇒ inverted; for virtual: m positive ⇒ erect. Same magnitude-interpretation as mirror but opposite sign convention

Description (what the sim teaches): P = 1/f (where f in metres). Unit: dioptre (D). Positive for converging (convex), negative for diverging (concave). Indian-context anchor: optometrist's "−1.5 D" or "+2.5 D" spectacle prescription
Notes: P = 1/f (where f in metres). Unit: dioptre (D). Positive for converging (convex), negative for diverging (concave). Indian-context anchor: optometrist's "−1.5 D" or "+2.5 D" spectacle prescription

Description (what the sim teaches): 1 D = 1 m⁻¹. The unit. A lens of f=50cm has P = +2 D. NCERT Example 9.8(i)
Notes: 1 D = 1 m⁻¹. The unit. A lens of f=50cm has P = +2 D. NCERT Example 9.8(i)

Description (what the sim teaches): Per RF-G4: own atomic. 1/F = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ (in contact, d→0). Powers add: P = P₁ + P₂. Used to design achromatic doublets (chromatic-aberration cancellation). NCERT Exercise 9.10 + 9.21
Notes: Per RF-G4: own atomic. 1/F = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ (in contact, d→0). Powers add: P = P₁ + P₂. Used to design achromatic doublets (chromatic-aberration cancellation). NCERT Exercise 9.10 + 9.21

Description (what the sim teaches): 1/F = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ + 1/f₃ for three lenses in contact. Linear extension
Notes: 1/F = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ + 1/f₃ for three lenses in contact. Linear extension

Description (what the sim teaches): Per RF-G4: own atomic. 1/F = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ - d/(f₁f₂). When d = f₁ + f₂: configuration becomes telescope (zero net power but angular magnification). EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "separation doesn't matter, only individual powers" — wrong for d > 0
Notes: Per RF-G4: own atomic. 1/F = 1/f₁ + 1/f₂ - d/(f₁f₂). When d = f₁ + f₂: configuration becomes telescope (zero net power but angular magnification). EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "separation doesn't matter, only individual powers" — wrong for d > 0

Description (what the sim teaches): When d = f₁ + f₂, the system has effective f = -f₁f₂/(0) — degenerate, behaves as angular magnifier (telescope). Bridge to T43 instruments
Notes: When d = f₁ + f₂, the system has effective f = -f₁f₂/(0) — degenerate, behaves as angular magnifier (telescope). Bridge to T43 instruments

Description (what the sim teaches): 6 cases for convex lens: (i) ∞ → F (real, dim, inv); (ii) beyond 2F → between F-2F (real, dim, inv); (iii) at 2F → at 2F (real, same-size, inv); (iv) between F-2F → beyond 2F (real, mag, inv); (v) at F → ∞ (used in collimation); (vi) between O-F → virtual, same-side, erect, mag. Plus 1 concave case (always virtual, erect, diminished). DCP Table 31.1 + Fig.31.39–31.44 canonical
Notes: 6 cases for convex lens: (i) ∞ → F (real, dim, inv); (ii) beyond 2F → between F-2F (real, dim, inv); (iii) at 2F → at 2F (real, same-size, inv); (iv) between F-2F → beyond 2F (real, mag, inv); (v) at F → ∞ (used in collimation); (vi) between O-F → virtual, same-side, erect, mag. Plus 1 concave case (always virtual, erect, diminished). DCP Table 31.1 + Fig.31.39–31.44 canonical

Description (what the sim teaches): Spherical aberration (marginal rays focus shorter f than paraxial — HCV §18.17 + Fig.18.29–18.30) + chromatic aberration (μ varies with λ → red f longer than violet f, HCV Fig.18.33). Coma + astigmatism + curvature + distortion are sub-types. HCV-only. JEE-Adv sleeper. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "real lenses are perfect" — every camera lens has multiple elements specifically to cancel aberrations
Notes: Spherical aberration (marginal rays focus shorter f than paraxial — HCV §18.17 + Fig.18.29–18.30) + chromatic aberration (μ varies with λ → red f longer than violet f, HCV Fig.18.33). Coma + astigmatism + curvature + distortion are sub-types. HCV-only. JEE-Adv sleeper. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "real lenses are perfect" — every camera lens has multiple elements specifically to cancel aberrations

Description (what the sim teaches): Crown lens (low dispersion) + flint lens (high dispersion) combined in contact: same μ for design wavelength but opposite chromatic-correction. Eyepieces of school microscopes are achromats
Notes: Crown lens (low dispersion) + flint lens (high dispersion) combined in contact: same μ for design wavelength but opposite chromatic-correction. Eyepieces of school microscopes are achromats

Description (what the sim teaches): Big telescopes use parabolic primary mirror instead of spherical lens to avoid both spherical and chromatic aberrations. Indian anchor: 2.34m Cassegrain at Kavalur IIA
Notes: Big telescopes use parabolic primary mirror instead of spherical lens to avoid both spherical and chromatic aberrations. Indian anchor: 2.34m Cassegrain at Kavalur IIA

Description (what the sim teaches): DCP-unique. Half-silvered lens behaves like a curved mirror with effective focal length: 1/f_eff = 2/f_lens + 1/f_mirror_back. Ray path: refract — reflect (off silver back) — refract again. JEE-Adv staple. DCP §31.6 Important Point #8 + Fig.31.82 + Example 31.28
Notes: DCP-unique. Half-silvered lens behaves like a curved mirror with effective focal length: 1/f_eff = 2/f_lens + 1/f_mirror_back. Ray path: refract — reflect (off silver back) — refract again. JEE-Adv staple. DCP §31.6 Important Point #8 + Fig.31.82 + Example 31.28

Description (what the sim teaches): δ = (i + e) - A; r₁ + r₂ = A (where A = prism angle). The 4-angle quadrilateral geometry at the prism cross-section. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "deviation depends only on prism material" — actually depends on incidence angle i too
Notes: δ = (i + e) - A; r₁ + r₂ = A (where A = prism angle). The 4-angle quadrilateral geometry at the prism cross-section. EPIC-C STATE_1 wrong belief: "deviation depends only on prism material" — actually depends on incidence angle i too

Description (what the sim teaches): From the AQNR quadrilateral: ∠A + ∠QNR = 180° (since both Q and R are 90°). And r₁+r₂+∠QNR = 180° from triangle. Hence r₁+r₂ = A
Notes: From the AQNR quadrilateral: ∠A + ∠QNR = 180° (since both Q and R are 90°). And r₁+r₂+∠QNR = 180° from triangle. Hence r₁+r₂ = A

Description (what the sim teaches): At δ_min: i = e, r₁ = r₂ = A/2. Refractive index formula: μ = sin((A + δ_m)/2) / sin(A/2). The standard lab method to measure μ of a prism material in physics-practical class. Indian classroom anchor: every CBSE Class 12 physics-practical
Notes: At δ_min: i = e, r₁ = r₂ = A/2. Refractive index formula: μ = sin((A + δ_m)/2) / sin(A/2). The standard lab method to measure μ of a prism material in physics-practical class. Indian classroom anchor: every CBSE Class 12 physics-practical

Description (what the sim teaches): δ-vs-i plot is U-shaped with minimum at i=e. Two different i values give same δ (symmetric). NCERT Fig.9.24
Notes: δ-vs-i plot is U-shaped with minimum at i=e. Two different i values give same δ (symmetric). NCERT Fig.9.24

Description (what the sim teaches): For small A: δ = (μ - 1) A. Simpler formula, used in achromatic-prism design. Direct-vision spectroscope uses combination of thin prisms with no net deviation but full dispersion
Notes: For small A: δ = (μ - 1) A. Simpler formula, used in achromatic-prism design. Direct-vision spectroscope uses combination of thin prisms with no net deviation but full dispersion

Description (what the sim teaches): μ varies with λ (Table 9.2: crown glass μ_violet=1.533 > μ_red=1.515; flint glass μ_violet=1.663 > μ_red=1.622). Hence δ varies with λ. White light splits into VIBGYOR. Indian-context anchor: every Class 12 physics-practical prism experiment
Notes: μ varies with λ (Table 9.2: crown glass μ_violet=1.533 > μ_red=1.515; flint glass μ_violet=1.663 > μ_red=1.622). Hence δ varies with λ. White light splits into VIBGYOR. Indian-context anchor: every Class 12 physics-practical prism experiment

Description (what the sim teaches): Newton's classic: first prism splits → second inverted prism recombines into white. NCERT Fig.9.26
Notes: Newton's classic: first prism splits → second inverted prism recombines into white. NCERT Fig.9.26

Description (what the sim teaches): Shorter λ → higher μ → larger δ. Violet bends most, red least. The "VIBGYOR top-to-bottom in rainbow" order
Notes: Shorter λ → higher μ → larger δ. Violet bends most, red least. The "VIBGYOR top-to-bottom in rainbow" order

Description (what the sim teaches): Two prisms (one crown + one flint) of different angles can combine to produce: (a) deviation without dispersion (achromatic prism — used in periscopes that need to bend light a fixed angle without color-fringing), OR (b) dispersion without deviation (direct-vision spectroscope — used in astronomy to identify Fraunhofer lines without bending the beam). NCERT Exercise 9.23
Notes: Two prisms (one crown + one flint) of different angles can combine to produce: (a) deviation without dispersion (achromatic prism — used in periscopes that need to bend light a fixed angle without color-fringing), OR (b) dispersion without deviation (direct-vision spectroscope — used in astronomy to identify Fraunhofer lines without bending the beam). NCERT Exercise 9.23

Description (what the sim teaches): Per RF-G5: own atomic. Primary rainbow: sunlight refracts into water droplet → internally reflects ONCE → refracts out at 42° to incoming sunlight (red outer, violet inner). Secondary rainbow: TWO internal reflections → 50° angle (red inner, violet outer — REVERSED order). Indian-context anchor: monsoon-rainbow universal experience. NCERT Fig.9.27 (a)+(b)+(c)
Notes: Per RF-G5: own atomic. Primary rainbow: sunlight refracts into water droplet → internally reflects ONCE → refracts out at 42° to incoming sunlight (red outer, violet inner). Secondary rainbow: TWO internal reflections → 50° angle (red inner, violet outer — REVERSED order). Indian-context anchor: monsoon-rainbow universal experience. NCERT Fig.9.27 (a)+(b)+(c)

Description (what the sim teaches): The color-order reversal in secondary rainbow is the diagnostic. If you see only one, it's primary; if both, check which is brighter (primary is) and which has red on top (primary does)
Notes: The color-order reversal in secondary rainbow is the diagnostic. If you see only one, it's primary; if both, check which is brighter (primary is) and which has red on top (primary does)

Description (what the sim teaches): Sun on one side of sky (~western horizon at sunset rainbow), rain on other side. The "back to sun" rule
Notes: Sun on one side of sky (~western horizon at sunset rainbow), rain on other side. The "back to sun" rule

Description (what the sim teaches): Per RF-G5: own atomic. Intensity scattered ∝ 1/λ⁴. Shorter λ (blue, violet) scatter more. Sky appears blue (violet scattered even more but eye less sensitive). Sunset/sunrise red (long path through atmosphere strips blue/violet, leaves red). Indian-context anchor: every Indian evening sky + diamond/peacock-feather iridescence
Notes: Per RF-G5: own atomic. Intensity scattered ∝ 1/λ⁴. Shorter λ (blue, violet) scatter more. Sky appears blue (violet scattered even more but eye less sensitive). Sunset/sunrise red (long path through atmosphere strips blue/violet, leaves red). Indian-context anchor: every Indian evening sky + diamond/peacock-feather iridescence

Description (what the sim teaches): When scatterers (water droplets) are much larger than λ: all wavelengths scatter equally → white. Hence clouds appear white, sky appears blue
Notes: When scatterers (water droplets) are much larger than λ: all wavelengths scatter equally → white. Hence clouds appear white, sky appears blue

Description (what the sim teaches): NCERT explicit note: red used for traffic signals + ambulance lights because least scattered → reaches farthest. Indian-traffic universal anchor
Notes: NCERT explicit note: red used for traffic signals + ambulance lights because least scattered → reaches farthest. Indian-traffic universal anchor

Description (what the sim teaches): Per RF-G6: bundled atomic. Eye lens varies focal length via ciliary muscle (accommodation). Defects: (i) Myopia (near-sighted, f too short) → fix with concave (diverging) lens; (ii) Hypermetropia (far-sighted, f too long) → fix with convex (converging); (iii) Astigmatism (cornea non-spherical) → fix with cylindrical lens; (iv) Presbyopia (age-related accommodation loss) → fix with bifocals. Indian anchors: Lenskart / Titan Eye+ / school eye-camps. NCERT §9.9.1 + Examples 9.10–9.12
Notes: Per RF-G6: bundled atomic. Eye lens varies focal length via ciliary muscle (accommodation). Defects: (i) Myopia (near-sighted, f too short) → fix with concave (diverging) lens; (ii) Hypermetropia (far-sighted, f too long) → fix with convex (converging); (iii) Astigmatism (cornea non-spherical) → fix with cylindrical lens; (iv) Presbyopia (age-related accommodation loss) → fix with bifocals. Indian anchors: Lenskart / Titan Eye+ / school eye-camps. NCERT §9.9.1 + Examples 9.10–9.12

Description (what the sim teaches): Myopic person's far point is closer than infinity (e.g., 80 cm in NCERT Example 9.11). Required power of corrective lens: P = -1/(far point in m). For 80cm: P = -1.25 D (concave)
Notes: Myopic person's far point is closer than infinity (e.g., 80 cm in NCERT Example 9.11). Required power of corrective lens: P = -1/(far point in m). For 80cm: P = -1.25 D (concave)

Description (what the sim teaches): Hypermetropic person's near point > 25cm (e.g., 75cm in NCERT Example 9.12). For comfortable reading at 25cm: needs +2.67 D (convex). Common in elderly Indians
Notes: Hypermetropic person's near point > 25cm (e.g., 75cm in NCERT Example 9.12). For comfortable reading at 25cm: needs +2.67 D (convex). Common in elderly Indians

Description (what the sim teaches): Cornea has different curvature in vertical vs horizontal plane. Vertical lines clear but horizontal blurred (or vice versa). Cylindrical lens with axis aligned correctly compensates
Notes: Cornea has different curvature in vertical vs horizontal plane. Vertical lines clear but horizontal blurred (or vice versa). Cylindrical lens with axis aligned correctly compensates

Description (what the sim teaches): After ~40 years, ciliary muscle weakens, lens can't deform enough for near vision. Many Indians need both — distance correction + reading correction → bifocal/progressive spectacles
Notes: After ~40 years, ciliary muscle weakens, lens can't deform enough for near vision. Many Indians need both — distance correction + reading correction → bifocal/progressive spectacles

Description (what the sim teaches): Anatomy + accommodation of human eye (cornea / iris / lens / ciliary / retina)
Notes: NCERT §9.9.1 + HCV §19.1. Anchor: schematic + Aravind Eye Hospital outreach.

Description (what the sim teaches): Most bending happens at cornea (n ≈ 1.336/1.396)
Notes: Surprises students — they expect "lens" to do the work.

Description (what the sim teaches): Ciliary muscles bend lens; f changes from f_max (relaxed) to f_min (strained)
Notes: HCV equation (i): 1/u_o = 1/f − 1/v_o, with v_o = 2.5 cm (retina).

Description (what the sim teaches): D = 25 cm for normal eye; varies with age (7–8 cm child → 1–2 m elder)
Notes: HCV §19.1 + NCERT §9.9.2. The "D" symbol appears in every microscope formula.

Description (what the sim teaches): Near point = closest clear; far point = farthest clear (normal eye = ∞)
Notes: Splits eye anatomy from defects cleanly.

Description (what the sim teaches): Apparent size ∝ angle subtended on eye, not linear size; instruments **increase visual angle** artificially
Notes: HCV §19.2. The reason ALL optical instruments work.

Description (what the sim teaches): 52" boy at 4m subtends 13"/m; 55" boy at 5m subtends 11"/m → first looks taller
Notes: HCV Example 19.1 — sleeper-success anchor.

Description (what the sim teaches): Single converging lens, object between F and lens; virtual erect magnified image at D or ∞
Image at ∞: m = D/f
Notes: NCERT 9.32: m = D/f, less strain.

Description (what the sim teaches): Image at near-point D: m = 1 + D/f
Notes: HCV (19.2): higher magnification but eye strained.

Description (what the sim teaches): Objective (short f_o, small aperture) + eyepiece (longer f_e, larger field); ray diagram
Notes: NCERT §9.9.3 + HCV §19.4. **Two-step magnification** = the key insight.

Description (what the sim teaches): m = (L/f_o)·(D/f_e) for normal adjustment; m = (L/f_o)·(1 + D/f_e) for near-point
L = v + f_e (separation between O and E); for f_o ≪ v, m ≈ (L/f_o)(D/f_e)
Notes: The simplification that makes formula tractable.

Description (what the sim teaches): Why f_o is small — to maximize v/u factor
Notes: Linked to JEE pattern Q.

Description (what the sim teaches): Large-aperture, long-f objective + short-f eyepiece, both converging; objects at ∞
Notes: NCERT §9.9.4 + HCV §19.5(A). Image at focal plane of objective.

Description (what the sim teaches): m = −f_o/f_e (normal adjustment); m = (f_o/f_e)(1 + f_e/D) (near-point); L = f_o + f_e
Notes: HCV (19.5, 19.6). Negative sign = inverted image.

Description (what the sim teaches): Final image is inverted — fine for stars, bad for terrestrial use
Notes: Motivates next two telescope atomics.

Description (what the sim teaches): Adds inverting lens (f) between O and E; L = f_o + 4f + f_e; image now erect
Notes: HCV §19.5(B). Length increases by 4f → bulky. Anchor: **Indian Army L70 telescopic sight**.

Description (what the sim teaches): Convex objective + **concave** (diverging) eyepiece; intercepts converging rays before image; L = f_o − \|f_e\|; **erect image, shorter tube**
Notes: HCV §19.5(C). Anchor: **opera glasses, traditional Indian binoculars**, original Galileo design.

Description (what the sim teaches): Parabolic mirror objective (no chromatic aberration, large aperture feasible); Newtonian secondary plane mirror @ 45° vs Cassegrain hyperbolic-secondary through hole in primary
Notes: NCERT §9.9.4. Anchor: **Vainu Bappu 2.34 m telescope Kavalur, Tamil Nadu** + **Devasthal 3.6 m Liquid Mirror Telescope, Uttarakhand**.

Description (what the sim teaches): No chromatic aberration + larger aperture + cheaper than refractors at large size
Notes: Story-anchor for the atomic.
HCV §19.6 + NCERT §10.6.3. **Why telescopes have large objective**.

Description (what the sim teaches): Two stars just resolved when central max of one ≈ first dark ring of other
RP = 2n sinβ / (1.22λ); n sinβ = numerical aperture
Replace air gap with oil (n ≈ 1.5) to increase n sinβ → better RP
Notes: NCERT §10.6.3. Anchor: pathology lab microscopes, **B.J. Medical College Ahmedabad**.

Description (what the sim teaches): RP ≠ magnification; high m without high RP shows blurry larger image
Notes: NCERT §10.6.3 final paragraph. Critical conceptual error students make.

Description (what the sim teaches): f_max < eye-retina distance → distant rays focus before retina; correction = **diverging (concave) lens**, P = −1/x where x = max clear distance
Notes: HCV §19.7(A). Anchor: **Indian metro screen-time epidemic** — myopia prevalence in urban India rising 30%+ in school kids per recent ICMR studies.

Description (what the sim teaches): f_min > required for near vision → near objects focus past retina; correction = **converging (convex) lens** of appropriate power
Notes: HCV §19.7(B). Anchor: **reading glasses** common in Indian elders 40+.

Description (what the sim teaches): Ciliary muscles weaken → near-point recedes; remedy = bifocal (convex bottom, plain top)
Notes: HCV §19.7 last paragraph.

Description (what the sim teaches): Cornea/lens curvature non-spherical; correction = **cylindrical lens** along specific axis
Notes: Anchor: standard prescription axis-angle in **Lenskart/Titan Eye+** orders.

Description (what the sim teaches): Two 45°-prisms in submarine/army periscope; uses TIR (no mirrors to corrode)
Notes: Anchor: **Indian Navy Kalvari-class submarine periscope** + **Indian Army L70 anti-aircraft sight**. *(Crosslinks to T42 A8.)*

Description (what the sim teaches): Light pipes via TIR; medical endoscope, telecommunication
Notes: Anchor: **AIIMS endoscope** + **BSNL/Jio fibre rollout under Bharat Net**. *(Crosslinks to T42 A6.)*

Description (what the sim teaches): Two TIR-prism subsystems (one per eye) compress long telescope length into compact L
Notes: Anchor: Nikon/Olympus binoculars used by **Indian Forest Service** for tiger census in Sundarbans + Sariska.

Description (what the sim teaches): f-number = f/D_aperture; depth-of-field; exposure
Notes: Anchor: **smartphone camera marketing** — every Indian student knows "f/1.8 lens" from phone specs.

Description (what the sim teaches): Both have variable-aperture diaphragm + adjustable lens + photo-sensitive surface; eye accommodates by changing f, camera changes v
Notes: Pedagogical bridge, recurring board question.

Description (what the sim teaches): Single convex lens for reading; m = 1 + D/f; choose f ≈ 2–10 cm
Notes: Already covered as simple_microscope; **note overlap** — may collapse one of them in Stage 4. Flagged.

Description (what the sim teaches): Plane / spherical / cylindrical wavefronts; locus of in-phase points
Notes: NCERT §10.2 intro.

Description (what the sim teaches): Every point on wavefront = source of secondary spherical wavelets; new wavefront = tangent envelope after time τ
Notes: NCERT §10.2 + HCV §17.4. The foundational atomic.

Description (what the sim teaches): Why secondary wavelets don't generate backward wave — historical hand-wave, resolved by Kirchhoff diffraction theory
Notes: Conceptual flag students raise; addressing it directly avoids confusion.

Description (what the sim teaches): Derivation of law of reflection (i = r) using Huygens construction (BC = vτ = AE)
Notes: NCERT §10.3.3 + HCV §17.5. Triangles EAC ≅ BAC → i = r.

Description (what the sim teaches): Derivation of n_1 sin i = n_2 sin r using BC = v_1τ and AE = v_2τ; n_2/n_1 = v_1/v_2
Notes: NCERT §10.3.1 + HCV §17.6. **The pedagogical centerpiece of Class 12 wave optics.**

Description (what the sim teaches): At a medium boundary, ν stays; λ and v change
Notes: NCERT Example 10.2(a). Common misconception.

Description (what the sim teaches): Δν/ν = −v_radial/c for v ≪ c; red shift (galaxy receding) vs blue shift (approaching)
Notes: NCERT §10.3.4 + Example 10.1 (galaxy at 306 km/s, Δλ = 0.6 nm). Anchor: **Hubble's law + Big Bang cosmology — NCERT explicit anchor**.

Description (what the sim teaches): Resultant displacement = vector sum of individual displacements at a point
Notes: NCERT §10.4 intro. Cross-link: same principle in T15 Waves chapter.

Description (what the sim teaches): Coherent = constant phase relationship → stable interference; incoherent = random phase → intensities add (I = 2I₀)
Notes: NCERT §10.4 + Fig 10.11 (two sodium lamps → no fringes).

Description (what the sim teaches): When φ rapidly varies, ⟨cos²(φ/2)⟩ = 1/2 → I = 2I₀
Δφ = (2π/λ)·Δx; constructive: Δx = nλ; destructive: Δx = (n+½)λ
Two pinholes S₁, S₂ behind master pinhole S; spherical waves interfere on distant screen
Notes: NCERT §10.5 + Fig 10.12. Foundational JEE Mains pattern.

Description (what the sim teaches): Master pinhole S "locks" phases — any abrupt phase change in S transfers identically to S₁, S₂
Notes: NCERT §10.5. The crucial trick that made the experiment work in 1801.

Description (what the sim teaches): Δx = xd/D for x, d ≪ D (binomial approximation)
Notes: NCERT eq (10.15–10.17). Derivation uses (S₂P)² − (S₁P)² = 2xd.

Description (what the sim teaches): β = λD/d (fringe spacing); uniform spacing between consecutive bright OR dark
Notes: NCERT eq (10.20) + HCV §17.8. **The most-asked JEE-Main formula in wave optics.**

Description (what the sim teaches): Bright at x_n = nλD/d; dark at x_n = (n+½)λD/d
I = 4I₀cos²(φ/2); periodic pattern between 0 and 4I₀
Notes: NCERT eq (10.12) + Fig 10.14.

Description (what the sim teaches): Central fringe = white (all λ overlap at Δx = 0); side fringes = coloured (red farthest, then no clear pattern)
Notes: NCERT Example 10.4(f). Single Indian-context anchor: **rainbow-coloured CD reflection** (real diffraction grating but visually identical).

Description (what the sim teaches): Source-slit condition s/S < λ/d for fringes to be visible
Notes: NCERT Example 10.4(d). Causes fringes to disappear when violated — non-obvious.

Description (what the sim teaches): Slit width a, screen at distance D, divide into N parallel secondary sources (Huygens); path difference between edges = a sinθ ≈ aθ
Zero intensity at aθ = nλ, n = ±1, ±2, ...; halve-and-cancel argument
Notes: NCERT eq (10.22). Critical: n = 0 is NOT a minimum (it's the central max).

Description (what the sim teaches): Weaker peaks at aθ = (n+½)λ; intensity drops off rapidly
Notes: NCERT §10.6.1 + Fig 10.16. Third-the-slit argument explains decay.

Description (what the sim teaches): Central bright fringe = 2λ/a wide; secondary maxima = λ/a wide
Notes: NCERT §10.6.1 + Fig 10.17. Why the diffraction envelope dominates intensity profile.

Description (what the sim teaches): Few sources → "interference"; many sources → "diffraction" (Feynman quote); intensity-redistribution explanation
Notes: NCERT §10.6.1 quotes Feynman directly. **Essential cognitive-clarification atomic.**

Description (what the sim teaches): Real YDSE pattern = double-slit cos² × single-slit (sinα/α)² envelope; missing orders when d/a is integer
Notes: NCERT §10.6.1 final + Fig 10.17.

Description (what the sim teaches): Airy pattern: central disk of radius r_0 ≈ 1.22λf/(2a); concentric rings
Notes: NCERT §10.6.3 eq (10.24). Source of the 1.22 factor in T43.

Description (what the sim teaches): z_F = a²/λ; beyond z_F diffraction spreading dominates over geometric aperture; ray optics breaks down
Notes: NCERT eq (10.31) + Example 10.7 (a = 3 mm, λ = 500 nm → z_F = 18 m). Quantifies where rays-vs-waves boundary lies.

Description (what the sim teaches): Two-polaroid experiment: rotate analyzer relative to polarizer → intensity varies from full to zero → light is transverse
Notes: NCERT §10.7. Sound waves don't polarise — clinches transverse nature of EM.

Description (what the sim teaches): Slot polariser on long string: only wave parallel to slot passes
Notes: NCERT §10.7 string analogy. Sleeper-success pedagogy.

Description (what the sim teaches): I = I₀ cos²θ where θ = angle between polariser and analyzer transmission axes
Notes: NCERT §10.7 (Malus' law).

Description (what the sim teaches): Polaroid material absorbs E-vector along one axis, transmits perpendicular axis (selective absorption)
Notes: NCERT §10.7. Anchor: **polaroid sunglasses** reducing road glare.

Description (what the sim teaches): At Brewster's angle iB where tan iB = n, reflected light is fully polarised perpendicular to plane of incidence
Notes: NCERT §10.7 Brewster's law. iB + rB = 90°.

Description (what the sim teaches): Rayleigh scattering polarises light; explains why sky-blue and why polaroid sunglasses block sky glare
Notes: NCERT §10.7 application. Anchor: **monsoon sky** + Indian-summer hazy noon sky.

Description (what the sim teaches): Hot rarefied gas emits at discrete wavelengths only; "fingerprint" of element; appears as bright lines on dark background
Notes: NCERT §12.3 + HCV §43.2. The empirical phenomenon that motivated Bohr.

Description (what the sim teaches): White light through cool gas → dark lines at same wavelengths as emission; gas absorbs only matching photons
Notes: NCERT §12.3 end. Anchor: **Fraunhofer dark lines in solar spectrum** — identification of elements in Sun.

Description (what the sim teaches): A good emitter at a wavelength is also a good absorber at that wavelength
Notes: Bridges to T18 Thermodynamics black-body.

Description (what the sim teaches): H spectrum has 5 visible+UV+IR series: Lyman, Balmer, Paschen, Brackett, Pfund. Each series = transitions to same final n_f
Notes: NCERT Fig 12.5 + HCV Fig 43.3.

Description (what the sim teaches): n_f = 2; 1/λ = R(1/4 − 1/n²) for n = 3, 4, 5...; H_α = 656.3 nm (red), H_β = 486.1 nm (blue-green), H_γ = 434.1 nm (violet); series limit = 364.6 nm
Notes: NCERT §12.3.1 + eq 12.5 + Fig 12.6 + HCV eq 43.1. **THE classic hydrogen spectrum series — every Indian Class 12 student sees the H_α red line.**

Description (what the sim teaches): n_f = 1; transitions n → 1; 1/λ = R(1 − 1/n²); 91.2–121.6 nm; ALL in UV (invisible to eye)
Notes: NCERT eq 12.6 + HCV §43.4 "Series structure".

Description (what the sim teaches): n_f = 3; transitions n → 3; 820–1875 nm range; ALL in IR
Notes: NCERT eq 12.7 + HCV §43.4.

Description (what the sim teaches): Brackett: n_f = 4; Pfund: n_f = 5; both far-IR. Formulas analogous
Notes: NCERT eq 12.8, 12.9 + HCV §43.4. Less-taught; merged per AS-G2.

Description (what the sim teaches): 1/λ = RZ²(1/n_f² − 1/n_i²); R = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹; derived theoretically from Bohr energy formula
Notes: NCERT eq 12.21–12.22 + HCV eq 43.9. **THE keystone spectral formula.**

Description (what the sim teaches): R = me⁴/(8ε₀²h³c); theoretical R = 1.03 × 10⁷ m⁻¹ very close to empirical 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹. **Direct confirmation of Bohr model.**
Notes: NCERT eq 12.23. The validation.

Description (what the sim teaches): n_i → ∞: 1/λ_min = R Z²/n_f². Balmer limit = 364.6 nm; Lyman limit = 91.2 nm; Paschen limit = 820 nm
Notes: NCERT p.422 + HCV §43.4. Convergence wavelength of each series; beyond it = continuous spectrum.

Description (what the sim teaches): From state n_max, atoms can transition to any lower state → total possible lines = n(n−1)/2
Notes: HCV §43 Worked Ex 7: "From n=4, atoms emit 6 different wavelengths" = 4(3)/2 = 6. **JEE-Mains predictable pattern.**

Description (what the sim teaches): For He⁺ (Z=2), Li²⁺ (Z=3): wavelengths scale as 1/Z². Same Lyman/Balmer/Paschen structure but Z² shorter
Notes: HCV Example 43.2: He⁺ in n=3 → n=2 emits at 164 nm vs hydrogen's 656 nm. **JEE-Adv top-tier pattern.**

Description (what the sim teaches): Moving source: λ_observed = λ_emitted (1 ± v/c); receding stars → red shift; approaching → blue shift
Notes: NCERT §10.3.4 Example 10.1 cross-link. Anchor: **NASA Hubble Space Telescope + India's AstroSat (2015) measure red shifts**.

Description (what the sim teaches): Filament + metal target + high voltage (~kV); accelerated electrons strike target; X-rays emerge through Be window
Notes: HCV §44.1 + Fig 44.1. Coolidge 1913 tube design — still standard medical-imaging tube.

Description (what the sim teaches): Decelerating electrons emit continuous-wavelength X-rays; cutoff wavelength λ_min = hc/eV (depends ONLY on accelerating voltage); independent of target material
Notes: HCV §44.2 + Fig 44.2. **Sharp cutoff is direct evidence of photon nature.** Duane-Hunt law.

Description (what the sim teaches): Sharp peaks at specific wavelengths characteristic of target material; K_α = transition L→K; K_β = M→K; depends on Z of target
Notes: HCV §44.2. Inner-shell electron transitions, NOT outer-shell like visible.

Description (what the sim teaches): √ν = a(Z − b); X-ray frequency √ proportional to (Z − screening constant); led to ordering elements by Z not atomic mass
Notes: HCV §44 (anticipated; mentioned briefly). Anchor: **periodic table reordering 1913 — Indian-curriculum chemistry bridge**.

Description (what the sim teaches): 3 processes: (a) absorption: photon kicks atom from E₁→E₂; (b) spontaneous: E₂→E₁ random emission (Einstein A); (c) stimulated: photon causes E₂→E₁ emission of identical photon (Einstein B)
Notes: HCV §43.9 + Figs 43.6, 43.7. **Three-arrow Einstein 1917 framework.**

Description (what the sim teaches): Spontaneous emission rate = A₂₁; stimulated rate = B₂₁ · u(ν); detailed balance gives A/B = 8πhν³/c³
Notes: Optional advanced; usually skipped at Class 12.

Description (what the sim teaches): Normally N₂ < N₁ (Boltzmann); need pumping to invert (N₂ > N₁); metastable state (lifetime ~ms not ~ns) accumulates population
Notes: HCV §43.9. The trick that makes lasing work.

Description (what the sim teaches): He (90%) + Ne (10%) gas mixture; He excited by discharge → resonantly pumps Ne to E₂ = 20.66 eV → lasing transition E₂ → E₁ at 632.8 nm (red)
Notes: HCV §43.9 + Fig 43.9. Anchor: **standard physics-lab He-Ne laser pointer in every Indian college**.

Description (what the sim teaches): All photons same wavelength + same phase + same direction; Δλ/λ ~ 10⁻⁶; beam diverges <1 mrad
Notes: HCV §43.9 list.

Description (what the sim teaches): Aravind Eye Hospital LASIK + medical surgery + CD/DVD readers + barcode scanners + Indian Army range-finders + fiber-optic communication (BharatNet)
Notes: Anchor-rich nano covering 6 real Indian uses.

Description (what the sim teaches): Min energy required for electron to escape metal surface. φ₀ in eV. Cs lowest (2.14), Pt highest (5.65). Depends on metal + surface
Notes: NCERT §11.2 + Table 11.1. The energy barrier electrons must overcome. **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed `electron_emission_modes` from Requires — φ₀ is an independent material property; the emission modes presuppose it (now in Required-by).

Description (what the sim teaches): 3 ways electrons escape: (i) thermionic (heating), (ii) field emission (~10⁸ V/m), (iii) photoelectric (light)
Notes: NCERT §11.2 (i-iii). Sets context for photoelectric as one of three.

Description (what the sim teaches): Hertz 1887 + Hallwachs/Lenard 1886-1902: UV light on metal → electron emission → photocurrent. Photosensitive substances.
Notes: NCERT §11.3 + §11.3.1-3.2. Foundational phenomenon.

Description (what the sim teaches): At fixed ν > ν₀ and fixed positive V: photocurrent ∝ intensity (linear). Saturation current = N_photoelectrons/sec
Notes: NCERT §11.4.1 + Fig 11.2. **JEE-Mains pattern: "what does intensity affect?"**

Description (what the sim teaches): At fixed ν, fixed intensity: photocurrent saturates at high +V (all photoelectrons collected); reverses to 0 at retarding −V (stopping potential V₀)
Notes: NCERT §11.4.2 + Fig 11.3. The I-V curve.

Description (what the sim teaches): The minimum negative V₀ at which photocurrent = 0; relates to K_max of most energetic photoelectron via eV₀ = K_max
Notes: NCERT eq 11.1 + Fig 11.3. **THE key measurement-bridge to Einstein equation.**

Description (what the sim teaches): For fixed ν, V₀ does NOT depend on intensity — only on frequency + metal
Notes: NCERT §11.4.2 Fig 11.3. **The observation that disproves wave theory** (wave theory predicts V₀ should scale with intensity).

Description (what the sim teaches): V₀ vs ν straight line: slope = h/e (universal), y-intercept = −φ₀/e (metal-specific); below threshold frequency ν₀, no photoemission
Notes: NCERT §11.4.3 + Fig 11.5 + eq 11.4. **THE plot that Millikan used to measure h.**

Description (what the sim teaches): ν₀ = φ₀/h; minimum frequency for photoemission. Below ν₀ NO emission no matter how bright the light
Notes: NCERT eq 11.3 + §11.4.3. Cs ν₀ = 5.16 × 10¹⁴ Hz (visible); Pt ν₀ = UV. **Cycle break (2026-06-13):** removed `einstein_photoelectric_equation` from Requires — the threshold is the OBSERVATION; Einstein's equation explains it. Teaching order: threshold observed → wave theory fails → Einstein resolves.

Description (what the sim teaches): Emission starts within ~10⁻⁹ s of light striking surface, even at very dim light. NOT energy-accumulation as wave theory predicts
Notes: NCERT §11.4.3 paragraph 4. The 4th wave-theory-failure observation.

Description (what the sim teaches): 4 failures: (1) K_max should depend on intensity (doesn't — depends on ν); (2) no ν₀ should exist (does); (3) emission should be slow at dim light (instantaneous); (4) wave can't pump energy into one electron only
Notes: NCERT §11.5. **Pedagogical pivot point** — sets stage for Einstein's photon hypothesis.

Description (what the sim teaches): K_max = hν − φ₀ = (1/2)mv²_max = eV₀. Each photon = 1 quantum hν; absorbed by 1 electron; explains ALL 4 observations
Notes: NCERT §11.6 eq 11.2 + 11.4. **Einstein's 1905 Nobel-winning equation.** THE keystone atomic of T46.

Description (what the sim teaches): Higher intensity = more photons/sec → more electrons/sec, but EACH electron still gets hν energy (not more)
Notes: NCERT §11.6 bullet 4. The crucial conceptual point.

Description (what the sim teaches): 5 properties: (i) E = hν, (ii) p = hν/c = h/λ, (iii) all photons of given ν have same E, p; intensity = N·E (count, not energy/photon); (iv) electrically neutral; (v) energy+momentum conserved in photon-particle collisions
Notes: NCERT §11.7 (i-v). Compton scattering (1924) further confirmed.

Description (what the sim teaches): Millikan 1906-1916: measured V₀ vs ν slope for sodium → calculated h. Got h ≈ 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, matching Planck. Nobel 1923
Notes: NCERT §11.6 paragraph 5. Triple-Nobel-bedrock (Planck 1918, Einstein 1921, Millikan 1923).

Description (what the sim teaches): All particles have a wavelength λ = h/p = h/(mv); massive objects → tiny λ (unobservable); electrons → measurable λ ~ X-ray range; **theory by symmetry: if waves are particles, particles must be waves**
Notes: NCERT §11.8 eq 11.5. **1924 hypothesis; 1929 Nobel.** Sleeper-success conceptual atomic.

Description (what the sim teaches): Electron accelerated through V: K = eV, p = √(2meV), λ = h/√(2meV) = 1.227/√V nm (V in volts). For V = 100 V, λ = 0.123 nm
Notes: NCERT §11.8 eq 11.9-11.11. **JEE-Mains pattern: "find λ of e⁻ at V volts."**

Description (what the sim teaches): 1927: electron gun → Ni crystal → diffraction peaks at θ=50° for V=54 V. Measured λ = 0.165 nm matches de Broglie 0.167 nm. Nobel 1937 (Davisson+Thomson)
Notes: NCERT §11.9 + Fig 11.7. **Experimental verification of de Broglie.**

Description (what the sim teaches): 1989 double-slit electron interference; 1994 iodine-molecule interference (10⁶× heavier than electron). Wave nature is universal
Notes: NCERT §11.9 last paragraph.

Description (what the sim teaches): Δx · Δp ≈ ℏ (or ℏ/2 rigorously). Cannot simultaneously measure position and momentum precisely. Fundamental, not measurement-error
Notes: NCERT §11.8 eq 11.12. Bohr's "definite orbit" violates this — links to T47 limitations.

Description (what the sim teaches): Localized wave packet → range of wavelengths → range of momenta. Visualizes Δx-Δp tradeoff
Notes: NCERT §11.8 Fig 11.6.

Description (what the sim teaches): Photoelectric device used in automatic doors, fire alarms, burglar alarms, light meters, traffic counters, motion-picture audio playback
Notes: NCERT §11.7 inset box. **Anchor-rich** — multiple Indian retail/security/film-industry uses.

Description (what the sim teaches): Atom = positive sphere with electrons embedded like seeds in a watermelon (J.J. Thomson 1898)
Notes: NCERT §12.1 + HCV §43.1 Fig 43.2. The model alpha-scattering disproved.

Description (what the sim teaches): Geiger-Marsden 1911 gold-foil setup; 5.5 MeV α from ²¹⁴Bi source; ZnS scintillation screen; observation that ~1 in 8000 deflected >90°
Notes: NCERT §12.2 Figs 12.1–12.3 + HCV §43.1. **Iconic Indian-JEE diagram.**

Description (what the sim teaches): Trajectory of α depends on impact parameter b: small b → large deflection; large b → small deflection; head-on → 180° rebound
Notes: NCERT Fig 12.4.

Description (what the sim teaches): All positive charge + most of mass concentrated in tiny nucleus (10⁻¹⁵ m); electrons orbit in surrounding empty space (10⁻¹⁰ m atomic radius)
Notes: NCERT §12.2 + HCV §43.1. Atom is 99.999% empty space.

Description (what the sim teaches): NCERT Example 12.1: if atom scaled to solar system, electron would be 100× farther than Earth from Sun
Notes: The "atom-is-mostly-empty" sleeper-success anchor.

Description (what the sim teaches): d = 2Ze²/(4πε₀K); for 7.7 MeV α + gold (Z=79): d ≈ 3.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ m. Upper bound on nuclear radius.
Notes: NCERT Example 12.2 + HCV §43.1. Energy-conservation derivation.

Description (what the sim teaches): Classical EM theory: accelerating electron radiates → spirals into nucleus in ~10⁻⁸ s. Atoms shouldn't exist.
Notes: NCERT §12.4 + HCV §43.3 + Fig 12.7. **The crisis that motivated Bohr.**

Description (what the sim teaches): Classical: spiraling electron emits continuous spectrum. Observation: hydrogen emits discrete line spectrum. Disagreement.
Notes: NCERT §12.4 paragraph 2.

Description (what the sim teaches): Electron revolves in certain stable orbits WITHOUT emitting radiation (defies classical EM); these are "stationary states"
L = nh/2π; angular momentum is integer multiple of h/2π; n = principal quantum number
Standing-wave condition 2πr = nλ + de Broglie λ = h/p gives mvr = nh/2π — Bohr postulate as de Broglie matter-wave standing-wave
Notes: T46 cross-link. Beautiful retrospective justification.

Description (what the sim teaches): Electron jumps from higher energy E_i to lower E_f emitting photon of frequency ν: hν = E_i − E_f
Notes: NCERT §12.4(iii) eq 12.12 + HCV §43.4(c). **THE bridge to T45 Atomic Spectra.**

Description (what the sim teaches): Combining postulate 2 + Coulomb's law: rₙ = (n²/m)(h/2π)²(4πε₀/e²); a₀ = r₁ = 5.29 × 10⁻¹¹ m (Bohr radius); rₙ ∝ n²
vₙ = e/√(4πε₀mrₙ) = (1/n)(e²/4πε₀)(1/(h/2π)); v ∝ 1/n; v₁/c ≈ 1/137 (fine structure constant!)
Notes: NCERT eq 12.14 + HCV §43.4 eq 43.2.

Description (what the sim teaches): Eₙ = K + U = −me⁴/(8ε₀²h²n²) = −13.6/n² eV; ground state E₁ = −13.6 eV
Notes: NCERT eq 12.17–12.19 + HCV §43.4 eq 43.6, 43.8. **THE keystone formula of modern physics for Class 12.**

Description (what the sim teaches): E₁ = −13.6 eV, E₂ = −3.4 eV, E₃ = −1.51 eV, E₄ = −0.85 eV; levels converge to 0 at n = ∞; visualization
Notes: NCERT Fig 12.8 + HCV Fig 43.4(b). The visual organizing principle of atomic physics.

Description (what the sim teaches): Energy to take electron from ground state to ∞: 13.6 eV. Ionization potential = 13.6 V.
Notes: NCERT §12.4.1 + HCV §43.4 "Ionization potential".

Description (what the sim teaches): Energy to raise atom from ground state to nth excited state. H first excited (n=2): E₂ − E₁ = 10.2 eV; potential = 10.2 V
Notes: NCERT §12.4.1 + HCV §43.4 "Excitation potential".

Description (what the sim teaches): Mercury vapor + variable-energy electron beam; energy absorbed only in discrete jumps (4.9 eV first); emitted at λ = 253 nm. Nobel 1925.
Notes: NCERT §12.4.1 inset box (p.428). **The strongest experimental proof of energy quantization.** Anchor: 1914 + 1925 Nobel.

Description (what the sim teaches): For He⁺, Li²⁺ (one electron, Z protons): rₙ = a₀n²/Z; Eₙ = −13.6 Z²/n² eV; vₙ proportional to Z/n
Notes: HCV eq 43.7 + Example 43.1. **JEE-Adv #1 pattern: "energy of He⁺ in first excited state."**

Description (what the sim teaches): (1) Only one-electron atoms work; (2) cannot explain fine structure splittings; (3) no intensity predictions; (4) no Zeeman effect; (5) violates uncertainty principle
Notes: HCV §43.5. **Critical for cognitive honesty** — Bohr isn't "the" model.

Description (what the sim teaches): Circular orbits → elliptical (under inverse-square force); same energy formula holds; introduces orbital angular momentum quantum number ℓ
Notes: NCERT p.426 paragraph 2. Anchor: **Sommerfeld's name routinely taught in Indian JEE prep**.

Description (what the sim teaches): Bohr "orbit" = definite circular path. Quantum mechanics "orbital" = probability cloud ψ(r,t); ψ² is probability density
Notes: NCERT p.426 explicit inset + HCV §43.7. **The conceptual handoff from "Class 12 physics" to "you'll learn this in college."**

Description (what the sim teaches): P(r)dr = |ψ|² dV; for ground state hydrogen, P(r) peaks at r = a₀ (matches Bohr radius!)
Notes: HCV Fig 43.5. Bohr's wrong picture happens to give right radius.
NCERT §13.2 eq 13.1 + Discovery of Neutron section. Foundation unit.

Description (what the sim teaches): Isotopes: same Z, different N (¹H, ²H, ³H); Isobars: same A, different Z (³H, ³He); Isotones: same N, different Z (¹⁹⁸Hg, ¹⁹⁷Au)
Notes: NCERT §13.2 end. Notation ᴬZX where X = chemical symbol. JEE-Mains classification atomic.

Description (what the sim teaches): Nucleus = Z protons + N neutrons; A = Z + N (mass number); chemical identity from Z (atomic number)
Notes: NCERT §13.2 eq 13.4. Chadwick neutron 1932 (Nobel 1935).

Description (what the sim teaches): Free neutron decays in ~1000s (β⁻ → proton + e⁻ + ν̄); but stable inside nucleus due to nuclear force
Notes: NCERT §13.2 paragraph 5. Surprising fact.

Description (what the sim teaches): R = R₀ A^(1/3) with R₀ = 1.2 fm = 1.2 × 10⁻¹⁵ m. Volume ∝ A. Universal across all nuclei
Notes: NCERT §13.3 eq 13.5. **Continuation of T47 Rutherford scattering upper-bound.**

Description (what the sim teaches): ρ_nuclear = mass/volume = A·u / [(4/3)π R₀³ A] = constant ≈ 2.3 × 10¹⁷ kg/m³. **Independent of A.** Comparable to neutron star density
Notes: NCERT §13.3 + Example 13.1. The non-obvious universal-density consequence.

Description (what the sim teaches): Einstein 1905: E = mc². 1 u ≡ 931.5 MeV. Energy "stored" in mass; convertible
Notes: NCERT §13.4.1 eq 13.6 + Example 13.2. **The bridge from classical mechanics to nuclear physics.**

Description (what the sim teaches): ΔM = [Z·m_p + (A−Z)·m_n] − M_nucleus. Always positive. The "missing mass" went into binding
Notes: NCERT §13.4.2 eq 13.7. Example: ¹⁶O has ΔM = 0.13691 u → 127.5 MeV.

Description (what the sim teaches): E_b = ΔM·c². Energy needed to break nucleus into separate nucleons. Measures nuclear stability
Notes: NCERT §13.4.2 eq 13.8. ¹⁶O: E_b = 127.5 MeV.

Description (what the sim teaches): E_bn = E_b/A. Plot vs A: peaks ~8.75 MeV at A=56 (Fe), drops to ~7.6 MeV at A=238 (U). Light nuclei A<30 + heavy A>170 have lower E_bn
Notes: NCERT §13.4.2 Fig 13.1. **THE organizing principle of nuclear physics. Fission goes A=240 → 2× A=120 (gains binding); Fusion goes A=2 + A=2 → A=4 (gains more).**

Description (what the sim teaches): Strong, short-range (~few fm), attractive at r > 0.8 fm, repulsive at r < 0.8 fm. Independent of charge (n-p, p-p, n-n all same). Much stronger than EM
Notes: NCERT §13.5 + Fig 13.2. Why nucleus doesn't collapse despite Coulomb proton-repulsion.

Description (what the sim teaches): Unstable nuclei spontaneously decay via: α (helium nucleus ⁴He), β (electron/positron), γ (high-energy photon)
Notes: NCERT §13.6 (i-iii). Becquerel 1896 discovery.

Description (what the sim teaches): dN/dt = −λN; N(t) = N₀ e^(−λt). λ = decay constant. Activity R = λN = R₀ e^(−λt)
Notes: NCERT §13.6.1 eq 13.10-13.15 + Fig 13.3. **THE keystone decay formula.**

Description (what the sim teaches): 1 Bq = 1 decay/sec; 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq (older unit).
T_{1/2} = ln2/λ ≈ 0.693/λ. Time for N to halve. After n half-lives, N/N₀ = (1/2)ⁿ
Notes: NCERT eq 13.17. **JEE-Mains: "find sample remaining after n half-lives."**

Description (what the sim teaches): τ = 1/λ; time at which N drops to N₀/e. T_{1/2} = τ·ln2 ≈ 0.693τ. Distinct from half-life
Notes: NCERT §13.6.1 + eq 13.18. **Cognitive error trap: students confuse τ and T_{1/2}.**

Description (what the sim teaches): ᴬZX → ᴬ⁻⁴_{Z−2}Y + ⁴₂He. Mass number drops 4, atomic number drops 2. Q-value = (m_X − m_Y − m_He)c²
Notes: NCERT §13.6.2 eq 13.19-13.21. Example: ²³⁸U → ²³⁴Th + ⁴He.

Description (what the sim teaches): β⁻: n → p + e⁻ + ν̄ (Z→Z+1, A unchanged); β⁺: p → n + e⁺ + ν (Z→Z−1, A unchanged). Neutrino preserves energy-momentum conservation

Description (what the sim teaches): Excited daughter nucleus (after α or β decay) emits γ-photon to reach ground state. Z, A unchanged. Energies hundreds of keV to MeV

Description (what the sim teaches): Heavy nucleus (²³⁵U, ²³⁹Pu) absorbs neutron → splits into 2 medium nuclei + 2-3 neutrons + ~200 MeV. Neutrons cause further fissions = chain reaction

Description (what the sim teaches): Controlled fission: fuel (²³⁵U) + moderator (D₂O, graphite) slows neutrons + control rods (Cd, B) absorb excess neutrons + coolant extracts heat → drives turbine

Description (what the sim teaches): Tarapur 1969 (TAPS) + Kakrapar + Rawatbhata + Kudankulam (Indo-Russian) + Madras Atomic Power Station + Kaiga. ~7-8 GW installed. AERB regulator. BARC Mumbai R&D.
